cards.title: Normal Vital Sign Ranges (Adult)
cards.body:
Pulse: 60–100 bpm
Respiratory rate: 12–20 /min
BP: <120/80 ideal
Temperature: 36.5–37.5 °C orally
SpO₂: 95–100%

Know the cut-offs, questions often hide on the borderline values.
cards.clinicalNote: Your post-op patient says they feel their heart racing. You count a pulse of 130 and BP 88/54. Don't chart it and move on. Tachycardia plus a falling BP is early shock. Escalate before the numbers get worse.

cards.title: Remember Hypertension Stages
cards.body:
JNC 8 / ACC-AHA:
• Normal: <120/<80
• Elevated: 120–129 / <80
• Stage 1: 130–139 / 80–89
• Stage 2: ≥140 / ≥90
• Crisis: >180 / >120 → emergency if end-organ damage

Memory hook: "13/8 starts the stage."

cards.title: When Vitals Are Lying
body: Patient just ran in → wait 5 min before BP
Cuff too small → falsely HIGH
Cuff too large → falsely low
Hot drink within 15 min → oral temp unreliable
Cold extremity → SpO₂ inaccurate
White-coat HTN → patient nervous in clinic
cards.clinicalNote: An anxious patient in clinic reads 158/96. Before you flag hypertension, sit them quietly for five minutes and recheck on a properly sized cuff, white-coat effect and a small cuff both push the number up falsely.

cards.title: Quick Self-Check
cards.body:
Pulse 110 in a febrile patient, primary or compensatory tachycardia?

Answer: COMPENSATORY. Each 1°C above 38.3 °C raises HR by ~10 bpm. Treat the fever, recheck the pulse.

cards.title: Pulse and respiration: what you must count
body: Count the pulse for a FULL minute if it is irregular. A 15 second count is only valid when the rhythm is regular
Apical pulse: 5th intercostal space, midclavicular line, counted for one full minute
PULSE DEFICIT is the apical rate minus the radial rate. It means beats too weak to reach the wrist
Count respirations WITHOUT telling the patient, or they will change their breathing
Bradypnoea below 12, tachypnoea above 20, apnoea absent
CHEYNE-STOKES: cycles of deepening then shallowing breaths with periods of apnoea. Heart failure, dying patients
KUSSMAUL: deep, laboured, sighing breaths. It means metabolic acidosis, classically DKA
cards.clinicalNote: Keep your fingers on the wrist while you count the breaths. The patient believes you are still taking the pulse and breathes naturally. Tell them you are counting respirations and they will breathe self consciously, and your number is worthless.

cards.title: WHO 5 Moments of Hand Hygiene
cards.body:
1. BEFORE touching a patient
2. BEFORE clean/aseptic procedure
3. AFTER body fluid exposure risk
4. AFTER touching a patient
5. AFTER touching patient surroundings

Moments 1, 2 protect the patient. Moments 3, 4, 5 protect YOU and OTHERS.

cards.title: Levels of Asepsis
cards.body:
Cleaning < Disinfection < Sterilisation

• Cleaning: removes visible dirt
• Disinfection: kills most microbes (not always spores)
• Sterilisation: kills ALL, including spores

Spaulding classification:
• Critical (enters sterile tissue) → Sterilise
• Semi-critical (mucosa contact) → High-level disinfection
• Non-critical (skin contact) → Low-level disinfection

cards.title: PPE Donning vs Doffing
body: Don IN ORDER: Gown → Mask → Goggles → Gloves
Doff IN OPPOSITE ORDER (mostly): Gloves → Goggles → Gown → Mask
Most contaminated piece comes off first
Mask comes off LAST (highest infectious risk near face)
Hand hygiene between each step

cards.title: The five moments for hand hygiene
body: 1. BEFORE touching a patient
2. BEFORE a clean or aseptic procedure
3. AFTER a body fluid exposure risk
4. AFTER touching a patient
5. AFTER touching the patient's surroundings
Alcohol rub is faster and kills most organisms. Use it when hands are visibly clean
SOAP AND WATER is mandatory for C. difficile and for visibly soiled hands. Alcohol does NOT kill C. diff spores
cards.clinicalNote: Alcohol gel does not touch Clostridium difficile spores. If your patient has C. diff, the gel is a false comfort. You have to physically wash the spores off with soap and running water.

cards.title: Transmission based precautions
cards.body:
STANDARD precautions apply to EVERY patient, always. Treat all blood and body fluid as infectious.

CONTACT (MRSA, C. difficile, scabies): gloves and gown, dedicated equipment, single room.

DROPLET (influenza, meningococcus, pertussis, mumps): surgical mask within 1 metre. Droplets are heavy and fall quickly, they do not travel far.

AIRBORNE (TUBERCULOSIS, measles, chickenpox): N95 RESPIRATOR, negative pressure room, door kept closed. These particles stay suspended and travel.

The classic exam trap: TB is AIRBORNE, not droplet. A surgical mask is not enough.

PUTTING PPE ON: gown, mask, goggles, gloves.
TAKING PPE OFF: gloves, goggles, gown, mask.
GLOVES COME OFF FIRST, they are the dirtiest thing you are wearing. The mask comes off LAST, because you are still breathing the room's air.
cards.clinicalNote: Most self contamination happens during removal, not during care. That is why the order of taking PPE off is examined more often than the order of putting it on.

cards.title: Device → FiO₂ → Flow
cards.body:
Nasal cannula: 1–6 L/min → ~24–44% (each 1 L adds ~4%)
Simple face mask: 5–8 L/min → ~40–60% (min 5 L to flush CO₂)
Venturi mask: precise, fixed FiO₂ (24–60%), best for COPD
Non-rebreather: 10–15 L/min → up to ~90%, emergencies

Rule: the more precise the control needed, the more you favour a Venturi.

cards.title: Oxygen Safety
body: No open flames / smoking near O₂
Humidify flows >4 L/min to prevent mucosal drying
COPD CO₂-retainers: target SpO₂ 88–92%, low flow
A non-rebreather needs the reservoir bag inflated before applying
Check tubing for kinks; assess SpO₂ and work of breathing, not the number alone

cards.title: Oxygen devices: flow rates and what they deliver
body: NASAL CANNULA: 1–6 L/min, roughly 24–44%. Comfortable, the patient can eat and talk
SIMPLE FACE MASK: 5–8 L/min, about 40–60%. NEVER run below 5 L/min or the patient rebreathes CO2
VENTURI MASK: delivers a precise, fixed percentage (24%, 28%, 35%, 40%). The device of choice in COPD
NON REBREATHER: 10–15 L/min, up to 90% or more. For emergencies. The reservoir bag must stay inflated
Humidify oxygen above 4 L/min. Dry gas damages the mucosa
Oxygen is a DRUG. It needs a prescription, a target saturation and monitoring
cards.clinicalNote: A simple face mask running at 2 L/min is more dangerous than no mask at all. There is not enough flow to flush the patient's own exhaled CO2 out of the mask, so they breathe it back in.

cards.title: Oxygen safety and the COPD target
cards.body:
Oxygen does not burn. It makes EVERYTHING ELSE burn ferociously.

• NO smoking, no naked flames, no matches anywhere near it
• NO oil, grease, petroleum jelly or alcohol based products near the flow. They ignite
• Use a water based lubricant for dry lips, never Vaseline
• Secure cylinders upright, check tubing is not kinked or trapped under a wheel

AND THE CLINICAL TARGET:
Most patients: SpO2 94 to 98%.
CO2 retaining COPD patient: SpO2 88 to 92%, delivered by a controlled device such as a Venturi mask.

But never withhold oxygen from a dying hypoxic patient out of fear. Treat the hypoxia, control the delivery, and watch the gases.

cards.title: Self-check: the collapsing reservoir bag
cards.body:
The reservoir bag on a non rebreather mask collapses completely every time the patient breathes in. What does that mean?

Answer: THE FLOW RATE IS TOO LOW. The bag must stay at least partly inflated throughout the breath, otherwise the patient is not getting the oxygen they need and is rebreathing their own air. Turn the flow up, usually to 10 to 15 L/min, until the bag stays inflated.

cards.title: The nursing process: ADPIE
cards.body:
The five steps, in this exact order. Questions asking what the nurse does FIRST are nearly always testing this order.

A: ASSESSMENT, gather the data. Subjective (what the patient says) and objective (what you measure)
D: DIAGNOSIS, the nursing diagnosis, not the medical one
P: PLANNING, set measurable goals
I: IMPLEMENTATION, carry out the interventions
E: EVALUATION, did it work? If not, reassess

If one of the options is 'assess', it is nearly always the answer. You cannot act on what you have not assessed.

cards.title: Nursing diagnosis versus medical diagnosis
cards.body:
A MEDICAL diagnosis names the disease: pneumonia, stroke, diabetes. A doctor makes it, and it does not change.

A NURSING diagnosis names the patient's RESPONSE to that problem, which the nurse can treat independently. It CAN change from shift to shift.

The PES format:
P: Problem, the nursing diagnosis
E: Etiology, the 'related to' cause
S: Signs and symptoms, the 'as evidenced by'

Correct: 'Impaired gas exchange RELATED TO alveolar fluid accumulation AS EVIDENCED BY an SpO2 of 88% and bibasal crackles.'

Incorrect: 'Pneumonia'. That is the medical diagnosis, and it is not yours to write.
cards.clinicalNote: This distinction is not academic. The medical diagnosis tells you what disease the patient has. The nursing diagnosis tells you what you are going to do about it in the next eight hours.

cards.title: Writing a goal that can actually be evaluated
body: It must be MEASURABLE, or you can never evaluate it
It must be PATIENT centred: 'the patient will...', never 'the nurse will...'
It must have a TIME frame: 'within 24 hours', 'by discharge'
It must be realistic for this patient's condition
Good: 'The patient will walk 20 metres with a frame by day 3'
Bad: 'The patient will mobilise better'. Better than what, and measured how?
Short term goals are met within days, long term goals over weeks

cards.title: Maslow: how to decide what comes first
cards.body:
When two nursing problems compete for your time, Maslow decides the order.

1. PHYSIOLOGICAL: airway, breathing, circulation, food, fluid, elimination, sleep, pain
2. SAFETY: falls, infection, a safe environment
3. LOVE and belonging: family, relationships, not being isolated
4. ESTEEM: dignity, independence, self respect
5. SELF ACTUALISATION: reaching one's potential

Always work bottom up. An airway problem beats a pain problem. Pain beats anxiety. Anxiety beats a body image concern.

And inside the physiological level, ABC decides: AIRWAY, then BREATHING, then CIRCULATION.
cards.clinicalNote: When every option in an exam looks like something a good nurse would do, ask which one keeps the patient alive for the next five minutes. That is almost always the answer.

cards.title: Assessment: subjective, objective, and the order of examination
body: SUBJECTIVE data is what the patient TELLS you. Symptoms: 'I feel dizzy', 'my chest hurts'
OBJECTIVE data is what you OBSERVE or MEASURE. Signs: BP 90/50, pallor, a rash
PAIN is subjective, and the patient's report is the gold standard. Never override it
Order of physical examination: INSPECT, PALPATE, PERCUSS, AUSCULTATE
The ABDOMEN is the exception: INSPECT, AUSCULTATE, then percuss and palpate
Palpating the abdomen first changes the very bowel sounds you were about to listen to
cards.clinicalNote: The abdomen is the one place you listen before you touch. Press on it first and you stimulate the bowel, so the sounds you hear afterwards are the ones you caused, not the ones the patient arrived with.

cards.title: Self-check: what does the nurse do first
cards.body:
A patient reports sudden chest pain. Your options: give the prescribed analgesia, call the doctor, assess the pain and vital signs, or reposition them. Which comes first?

Answer: ASSESS. You cannot treat, report or reposition intelligently until you know what you are dealing with. Assessment is step one of ADPIE, and it is nearly always the correct first action.

cards.title: The rights of medication administration
cards.body:
The classic FIVE, and what has been added since:

1. Right PATIENT (two identifiers, never the bed number)
2. Right DRUG
3. Right DOSE
4. Right ROUTE
5. Right TIME

Plus:
6. Right DOCUMENTATION, AFTER giving it, never before
7. Right REASON, does this indication make sense?
8. Right RESPONSE, did it work, did it harm?
9. Right to REFUSE, a competent patient may decline

Check the label THREE times: taking it from the shelf, preparing it, and returning or discarding the container.

cards.title: Injections: angle, site and volume
body: INTRADERMAL: 10–15 degrees, bevel UP, about 0.1 mL. A bleb should form. Mantoux and allergy testing
SUBCUTANEOUS: 45–90 degrees, up to 1 mL. Insulin, heparin, low molecular weight heparin
INTRAMUSCULAR: 90 degrees. Up to 3 mL in a large adult muscle, about 1 mL in the deltoid
The VENTROGLUTEAL site is the SAFEST intramuscular site: no large nerves or vessels nearby
The VASTUS LATERALIS is the site of choice in INFANTS
Z-TRACK for irritant drugs such as iron: pull the skin sideways, inject, withdraw, then release
NEVER massage after heparin or insulin. It causes bruising and alters absorption
cards.clinicalNote: The dorsogluteal site in the upper outer buttock is what generations were taught, and it sits uncomfortably close to the sciatic nerve. The ventrogluteal site is safer, and it is the one the exam expects you to name.

cards.title: Insulin: the rules that prevent disasters
cards.body:
MIXING TWO INSULINS: draw up CLEAR before CLOUDY. Regular (clear) before NPH (cloudy).

Why it matters: if cloudy long acting insulin contaminates the clear regular vial, you have unpredictably altered a fast acting drug, and every dose drawn from that vial afterwards is wrong.

Air is injected into the CLOUDY vial first, then the clear vial, then you draw up the CLEAR and finally the CLOUDY.

OTHER RULES:
• Only REGULAR (short acting) insulin may be given INTRAVENOUSLY
• Rotate sites to prevent lipohypertrophy, which makes absorption erratic
• Absorption is fastest from the ABDOMEN, then arm, then thigh, then buttock
• Never shake insulin, roll it gently between the palms
• The in use pen is kept at room temperature, spares in the fridge, never frozen
cards.clinicalNote: Never abbreviate 'units' to 'U'. A handwritten 10U has been misread as 100 and it has killed people. Write the word units in full, every time, without exception.

cards.title: High-alert drugs and the independent double check
body: INSULIN, HEPARIN, potassium chloride, opioids, chemotherapy, anticoagulants
These need an INDEPENDENT double check by a second registered nurse
Independent means the second nurse calculates it themselves, without being told your answer first
IV potassium is NEVER a bolus or a push. Always diluted, always on a pump, always monitored
Concentrated electrolytes should not be stored loose on the ward
Question any dose needing more than two ampoules or tablets. It usually signals a calculation error
Check allergies EVERY time, not only on admission

cards.title: Oral medicines: what must never be crushed
cards.body:
NEVER CRUSH:

• ENTERIC COATED tablets (EC). The coating protects the stomach from the drug, or the drug from the acid. Crushing destroys both
• SUSTAINED or MODIFIED RELEASE (SR, MR, XL, LA, CR). Crushing dumps an entire 12 or 24 hour dose at once. This is a potentially fatal overdose
• Capsules containing coated pellets
• Cytotoxic drugs, because of the powder exposure risk to you
• Sublingual and buccal tablets, which are designed to dissolve in the mouth

If the patient cannot swallow, do NOT simply crush it. Ask the pharmacist for a liquid, dispersible or alternative preparation.
cards.clinicalNote: Crushing a modified release tablet for a patient with swallowing difficulty is a well meaning act that can kill them. A dose engineered to trickle out over 24 hours is delivered in ten minutes. Ask the pharmacist first, every time.

cards.title: Self-check: clear before cloudy
cards.body:
You are mixing regular and NPH insulin in one syringe. Which is drawn up first and why does it matter?

Answer: REGULAR (CLEAR) FIRST, then NPH (cloudy). If cloudy long acting insulin contaminates the clear regular vial, you have permanently altered a fast acting drug, and every subsequent dose from that vial is unpredictable.

cards.title: Falls: who is at risk and what actually prevents them
body: Highest risk: over 65, a previous fall, confusion, poor vision, incontinence
Drugs that cause falls: sedatives, antihypertensives, diuretics, opioids, anything causing a postural drop
Keep the bed at its LOWEST position with the BRAKES ON
Call bell, water and glasses within reach. Most falls happen on the way to the toilet
Non slip footwear, clear floors, adequate lighting and a night light
Offer toileting on a regular round rather than waiting for the bell
BED RAILS ARE NOT FALL PREVENTION. A confused patient climbs over them and falls further
cards.clinicalNote: Putting all four bed rails up on a confused patient feels like safety, but it turns a roll out of bed into a fall from a greater height over a barrier. It is also a restraint, and it needs the same justification as one.

cards.title: After a fall: assess before you lift
cards.body:
The instinct is to pick the patient up straight away. Resist it.

1. DO NOT MOVE THEM YET. Moving a fractured hip or an injured spine is what causes the lasting damage
2. Assess: conscious? Airway, breathing, circulation
3. Look for injury: head, hip, wrist. A SHORTENED, EXTERNALLY ROTATED leg means a hip fracture
4. Vital signs and a BLOOD GLUCOSE. Ask WHY they fell, not just that they fell
5. Only then move them, with enough help and the right equipment
6. Neurological observations if they hit their head or the fall was unwitnessed
7. Document factually and complete an incident report

An unwitnessed fall in a patient on anticoagulants needs a CT head, no matter how well they look.

cards.title: Restraints: the last resort, never the first
cards.body:
A restraint is anything that limits a patient's free movement: physical, chemical (sedation) or environmental.

RULES:
• Only when every less restrictive alternative has failed
• Only to prevent harm to the patient or others. NEVER for staff convenience, and never as punishment
• Requires a doctor's order, time limited and frequently reviewed
• Use the LEAST restrictive option that works
• Observe continuously or very frequently
• Release, reposition, check circulation and offer fluid and toileting at least 2 hourly
• Use a QUICK RELEASE knot, tied to the BED FRAME, never to a side rail
• Document the reason, the alternatives tried, and every observation

Alternatives first: reorientation, familiar objects, family presence, a lower bed, distraction, and treating the underlying delirium.
cards.clinicalNote: A restraint tied to a side rail becomes a strangulation hazard the moment someone lowers that rail. It goes to the immovable bed frame, and the knot must release with a single pull in an emergency.

cards.title: Patient identification: the errors this prevents
body: Use TWO identifiers before any drug, procedure or transfusion. Name and date of birth, or a hospital number
The BED NUMBER is NEVER an identifier. Patients get moved
Ask the patient to STATE their name. Do not ask 'are you Mr Sharma?' A confused patient will say yes
Check the wristband against the chart and the prescription every single time
For surgery, the site is MARKED and a team TIME OUT is performed before the incision
Never rely on a confused or sedated patient's own account alone

cards.title: Medication error: what to do when it happens
cards.body:
You have given the wrong drug, or the wrong dose. What matters now is the patient, not your reputation.

1. ASSESS THE PATIENT FIRST. Vital signs, conscious level, any adverse effect. Their safety comes before any paperwork
2. Report to the doctor and the nurse in charge IMMEDIATELY
3. Carry out whatever corrective treatment is ordered
4. Monitor the patient closely
5. Document factually: what was given, when, and what you did about it
6. Complete an incident report. It is a LEARNING tool, not a punishment

Never hide it. Never alter the record. Never wait to see whether anything happens. Concealing an error is what turns a mistake into misconduct.
cards.clinicalNote: Every experienced nurse has made an error. What separates a safe nurse from a dangerous one is not making none, it is reporting the one they made immediately, so the patient can be protected.

cards.title: Self-check: the first action after an error
cards.body:
You realise you gave a double dose of an antihypertensive an hour ago. What is your FIRST action?

Answer: ASSESS THE PATIENT. Check their blood pressure, pulse and conscious level. Patient safety comes before reporting and before the incident form. Then report immediately, treat as directed, document, and complete the report.

cards.title: Positions: which one, and what for
body: FOWLER'S (45–60 degrees): breathlessness, feeding, after meals, most respiratory problems
HIGH FOWLER'S (90 degrees): severe breathlessness, acute pulmonary oedema
SEMI FOWLER'S (30 degrees): after most neurosurgery, and during nasogastric feeding to prevent aspiration
SUPINE: flat on the back. After spinal anaesthesia or lumbar puncture
PRONE: on the abdomen. Improves oxygenation in ARDS
LATERAL / RECOVERY: unconscious but breathing. Protects the airway from vomit
LITHOTOMY: vaginal and rectal procedures, and childbirth
SIMS (left lateral, semi prone): enema administration and rectal examination

cards.title: Recovery position and the left lateral rule
cards.body:
An unconscious patient who IS BREATHING goes into the RECOVERY position.

Why: the tongue falls forward instead of back, and vomit drains OUT of the mouth rather than into the lungs.

Do NOT use it if you suspect a SPINAL injury, unless the airway is threatened and you have no other option. Then use a jaw thrust and log roll with help.

For an ENEMA the patient lies in the LEFT lateral (Sims) position, and the reason is pure anatomy: the sigmoid and descending colon lie on the LEFT, so gravity carries the fluid along the natural curve of the bowel and the patient retains it.

cards.title: Immobility: what it damages, system by system
cards.body:
Immobility is not merely inconvenient. It damages every system, and preventing that is core nursing.

RESPIRATORY: secretions pool, atelectasis, pneumonia
CARDIOVASCULAR: DVT, pulmonary embolism, postural hypotension, increased cardiac work
MUSCULOSKELETAL: muscle wasting (up to about 3% of strength lost per DAY), CONTRACTURES, FOOT DROP, osteoporosis
SKIN: pressure ulcers
GASTROINTESTINAL: constipation, poor appetite
URINARY: stasis, infection, renal stones as calcium leaches from bone
PSYCHOLOGICAL: depression, disorientation, loss of confidence

The single most effective intervention against ALL of this is EARLY MOBILISATION.
cards.clinicalNote: A week of bed rest can cost an older person the independence they never get back. Getting a patient out of bed is not a comfort measure. It is treatment, and it is as important as anything on the drug chart.

cards.title: Safe moving and handling: protecting your own back
body: Assess first: the patient, the load, the environment, the equipment
NEVER lift manually what a hoist or slide sheet can move
Bend the KNEES, not the back. Keep the spine straight
Keep the load CLOSE to your body. Distance multiplies the strain
Feet apart, one slightly forward, for a stable base
TURN WITH YOUR FEET. Never twist at the waist
Use the strong leg muscles, not the weak back muscles
Agree a clear command with the team, and one person leads the count

cards.title: Range of motion and preventing contractures
cards.body:
A contracture is a permanent shortening of muscle and tendon. Once it is established it needs surgery. It is entirely PREVENTABLE.

PASSIVE range of motion: the nurse moves the joint, for a patient who cannot.
ACTIVE: the patient moves it themselves. Always preferred, because it maintains strength as well as flexibility.
ACTIVE ASSISTED: the patient moves, the nurse helps.

Perform them at least twice daily, moving each joint slowly and smoothly through its full range. Stop at pain or resistance, never force a joint.

FOOT DROP is the classic preventable contracture. Use a footboard or splint to hold the ankle at 90 degrees.
cards.clinicalNote: Foot drop develops silently in a patient lying with unsupported feet under a tight sheet. Once the Achilles tendon shortens, they cannot walk properly even after everything else has healed. A footboard is a trivial thing that prevents a permanent disability.

cards.title: Self-check: the enema position
cards.body:
Which position is used to give an enema, and why that one?

Answer: LEFT LATERAL (SIMS). The descending and sigmoid colon lie on the LEFT, so lying on the left side lets gravity carry the solution along the natural curve of the bowel, and the patient can retain it.

cards.title: Pain: the fifth vital sign
cards.body:
PAIN IS WHATEVER THE PATIENT SAYS IT IS, EXISTING WHENEVER THEY SAY IT DOES.

That is not a slogan, it is the professional standard. The patient's self report is the GOLD STANDARD. You do not overrule it because they look comfortable, because they are laughing, or because you would not be in pain.

SCALES:
• Numeric rating scale, 0 to 10: adults who can communicate
• Wong-Baker FACES: children over 3, and adults with communication difficulty
• FLACC (Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability): infants and non verbal patients
• Behavioural signs in the non verbal: grimacing, guarding, restlessness, a rising pulse and BP

CHRONIC pain often has NO physical signs at all. A normal pulse does not mean the absence of pain.
cards.clinicalNote: A patient with chronic pain may have a perfectly normal pulse, blood pressure and expression while reporting 8 out of 10. Their body has adapted. Believing the number rather than the vital signs is the correct nursing response.

cards.title: Pain assessment: PQRST
cards.body:
P: Provocation and Palliation. What brings it on, what relieves it?
Q: Quality. Sharp, dull, burning, crushing, cramping?
R: Region and Radiation. Where, and does it travel?
S: Severity. Score out of 10
T: Timing. When did it start, how long, constant or intermittent?

The QUALITY often names the diagnosis:
• Crushing, radiating to arm or jaw → cardiac
• Burning, shooting, electric → neuropathic
• Colicky, in waves → obstruction of a hollow organ (bowel, ureter, bile duct)
• Tearing, radiating to the back → aortic dissection

cards.title: The WHO analgesic ladder
body: STEP 1: non opioid. Paracetamol, NSAIDs, plus adjuvants
STEP 2: weak opioid. Codeine, tramadol, plus a non opioid, plus adjuvants
STEP 3: strong opioid. Morphine, fentanyl, plus a non opioid, plus adjuvants
ADJUVANTS at every step: antidepressants and anticonvulsants for NEUROPATHIC pain, steroids, muscle relaxants
For chronic pain, give analgesia BY THE CLOCK, not only when asked
PREVENT the pain, do not chase it. Bringing pain down is far harder than keeping it down
Non drug methods work alongside: positioning, heat, cold, distraction, relaxation, massage

cards.title: Opioids: what must be watched
cards.body:
SIDE EFFECTS:
• RESPIRATORY DEPRESSION. The one that kills. It is ALWAYS preceded by SEDATION, so a sedation score is your early warning
• CONSTIPATION. Universal, it does NOT wear off, and it must be prescribed for from day one
• Nausea and vomiting, which usually settles
• Itching, urinary retention, hypotension
• PINPOINT PUPILS

MONITOR: respiratory rate, sedation level, pain score.

A respiratory rate BELOW 8 with rising sedation means overdose. The antidote is NALOXONE.

Naloxone acts fast but WEARS OFF FASTER than morphine, so the patient can re sedate afterwards and must be watched continuously.

TOLERANCE, DEPENDENCE and ADDICTION are three different things. Fear of addiction is not a reason to leave a patient in pain.
cards.clinicalNote: Sedation always comes before respiratory depression. A patient who is hard to rouse but whose respiratory rate is still 12 is already in trouble. Do not wait for the rate to fall before you act.

cards.title: Patient controlled analgesia: the one rule
body: ONLY THE PATIENT presses the button. Never a nurse, never a relative
This is the entire safety mechanism: a patient too sedated to press it cannot overdose themselves
A relative pressing it 'to help them sleep' has killed patients. Teach the family explicitly
The pump has a LOCKOUT interval that prevents a second dose too soon
Monitor respiratory rate, sedation score and pain score regularly
Naloxone and a bag valve mask must be immediately available
The patient must be alert enough to understand it and physically able to press it
cards.clinicalNote: Teaching a family that they must never press the button for their loved one is one of the most important safety conversations on a surgical ward. To them it looks like kindness. It is the exact mechanism by which the safety feature is defeated.

cards.title: Self-check: the over sedated patient
cards.body:
A post op patient on morphine has a respiratory rate of 7 and is very hard to rouse. Priority action, and the antidote?

Answer: SUPPORT AIRWAY AND BREATHING, call for help, and give NALOXONE as prescribed. Remember naloxone has a SHORTER half life than morphine, so they can re sedate once it wears off. They need continuous observation, not a single dose and a tick.

cards.title: Urinary catheter: preventing the infection
body: Catheter associated urinary infection is one of the commonest hospital acquired infections
The best prevention is NOT inserting one, and removing it as EARLY as possible
Strict aseptic insertion, and a CLOSED drainage system that is never broken
Keep the bag BELOW bladder level at ALL times, so urine cannot flow back
Never let the bag touch the floor. Never lift it above the bladder when moving the patient
Keep the tubing free of kinks and dependent loops
Daily meatal hygiene with soap and water. Antiseptics are not needed
Empty via the drainage tap using a separate clean container for each patient
cards.clinicalNote: The commonest way a nurse causes a catheter infection is lifting the drainage bag above the bladder during a transfer. The urine in that tube runs straight back into the bladder, carrying whatever has been growing in the bag with it.

cards.title: Nasogastric tube: confirming placement safely
cards.body:
A misplaced nasogastric tube feeding into the LUNG is fatal. Confirmation is not optional.

ACCEPTABLE CONFIRMATION:
1. Aspirate and test the pH. A pH of 5.5 OR BELOW indicates gastric placement
2. If there is any doubt, or the patient is on acid suppressing drugs, an X RAY is definitive

NO LONGER ACCEPTED AND GENUINELY DANGEROUS:
• The 'whoosh test', injecting air and listening over the stomach. A tube in the bronchus can produce an identical sound
• Watching for bubbling in water
• The absence of coughing. A sedated or neurologically impaired patient may not cough at all

Check placement BEFORE every feed, before every medicine, and after any vomiting, retching or coughing fit.

MEASURE the length: tip of the NOSE, to the EAR LOBE, to the XIPHOID process.
cards.clinicalNote: The whoosh test is still performed on wards, and it is exactly the kind of shortcut that kills. Air pushed into a tube sitting in the bronchus makes a convincing noise over the epigastrium. pH or X ray. Nothing else.

cards.title: Enteral feeding: preventing aspiration
cards.body:
The greatest risk of tube feeding is aspiration pneumonia. Four things prevent it:

1. HEAD OF THE BED UP, 30 to 45 degrees, during the feed and for 30 to 60 minutes after
2. CHECK PLACEMENT before every feed
3. CHECK GASTRIC RESIDUAL VOLUME per policy. A high residual means the stomach is not emptying, and feeding on top of it invites vomiting
4. FLUSH the tube with water before and after feeds and medicines, so it does not block

Give medicines SEPARATELY, one at a time, flushing between each. Never add drugs to the feed bag.

Stop the feed and sit the patient up if they vomit, cough persistently or become breathless.

cards.title: Bowels: constipation, diarrhoea and the trap between them
body: Constipation: hard stool, straining, fewer than three stools a week, incomplete emptying
Causes: immobility, low fibre, low fluid, OPIOIDS, ignoring the urge, no privacy
Opioid constipation does NOT wear off. Prescribe for it from the very first dose
FAECAL IMPACTION can present as OVERFLOW DIARRHOEA: liquid stool leaking past a hard mass
Never treat that 'diarrhoea' with a constipating agent. You will make it dramatically worse
Diarrhoea: assess for dehydration, isolate if infective, send a stool specimen
Sudden diarrhoea after antibiotics is C. difficile until proven otherwise. Soap and water, not gel
cards.clinicalNote: An elderly constipated patient who suddenly develops watery diarrhoea usually has impaction with overflow, not gastroenteritis. Giving loperamide is precisely the opposite of what they need, and a rectal examination gives you the answer in seconds.

cards.title: Stoma care: what a healthy stoma looks like
cards.body:
A HEALTHY stoma is PINK or RED, moist and slightly raised. It has no nerve endings, so it should not be painful.

REPORT IMMEDIATELY:
• PALE, DUSKY, PURPLE or BLACK. This is ISCHAEMIA. The stoma is dying
• Retraction below the skin surface
• Prolapse, or a large parastomal hernia
• Bleeding beyond a slight ooze on cleaning

COLOSTOMY output is formed or semi formed.
ILEOSTOMY output is LIQUID, and the patient loses far more fluid and sodium, so DEHYDRATION is a real risk.

Protecting the peristomal skin is the main nursing challenge: an appliance that is too large lets output burn the skin, one that is too tight damages the stoma.

Body image and psychological support are core, not optional extras.
cards.clinicalNote: A black stoma is a surgical emergency. It means the blood supply has failed and the bowel is dying. Pink and moist is what you want to see, and you should be looking at it every single day.

cards.title: Self-check: confirming the NG tube
cards.body:
A colleague confirms nasogastric placement by injecting air and listening over the stomach. Is that acceptable?

Answer: NO. The whoosh test is unreliable and has caused deaths, because air injected into a tube sitting in the bronchus makes the same sound over the epigastrium. Confirm with an aspirate pH of 5.5 or below, or an X ray if there is any doubt at all.

cards.title: Documentation: the rules that protect the patient and you
body: If it is NOT documented, it was NOT done. That is the legal position
Record CONTEMPORANEOUSLY, as soon as possible after the event
Be FACTUAL and OBJECTIVE. Record what you saw, heard and measured
Write 'refused breakfast, states she feels nauseated', not 'patient is being difficult'
NEVER erase, use correction fluid, or obliterate an entry
To correct an error: ONE LINE through it so the original stays READABLE, write 'error', then sign and date
Sign every entry: name, designation, date and time
Never document care before you have given it. Never sign for another nurse
Never leave blank lines that someone else could fill in later
cards.clinicalNote: Signing a drug as given before you have actually given it is how a patient ends up receiving nothing, or receiving it twice. The chart is signed after the medicine is swallowed, not before you walk to the bed.

cards.title: The four principles of ethics
cards.body:
AUTONOMY: the patient's right to decide for themselves. This underpins consent and the right to refuse
BENEFICENCE: do good, act in the patient's best interest
NON MALEFICENCE: first, do no harm
JUSTICE: fairness. Treat patients equitably, do not discriminate

Plus:
VERACITY: truthfulness
FIDELITY: keeping your promises
CONFIDENTIALITY: protecting their information

THE CLASSIC EXAM CONFLICT: autonomy versus beneficence. A competent patient refuses treatment you know would help them.

AUTONOMY WINS. A competent adult has the right to make a decision you believe is wrong.

cards.title: Informed consent: what it actually requires
cards.body:
THREE elements, and ALL THREE must be present:

1. The patient has CAPACITY to decide
2. Consent is given VOLUNTARILY, free of coercion
3. The patient is adequately INFORMED: the nature of the procedure, its benefits, its risks, the alternatives, and what happens if they do nothing

WHOSE JOB: the person PERFORMING the procedure explains it and obtains consent. The nurse may WITNESS the signature and confirm understanding. The nurse does not obtain consent for a procedure they are not performing.

If the patient says 'I still do not really understand what they are going to do', you STOP and call the doctor back. You do not fill the gap yourself, and you do not let them sign.

In a true EMERGENCY, where the patient cannot consent and there is no time, life saving treatment may proceed under the doctrine of necessity.
cards.clinicalNote: The most important thing a nurse does at the consent form is not witnessing the signature. It is asking the patient to explain, in their own words, what is about to happen to them. That question catches everyone who has signed without understanding.

cards.title: Confidentiality and its limits
body: Information is shared only with those directly involved in the patient's care
Never discuss patients in lifts, corridors, canteens or on social media
Never access the records of a patient you are not caring for, including your own family
Confidentiality may be broken only in narrow circumstances:
• With the patient's own consent
• Where there is a serious risk of harm to the patient or to others
• Where required by law or a court order
• For notifiable diseases, under public health law
Even then, disclose the MINIMUM necessary, to the appropriate person only

cards.title: Negligence, and how it differs from a mistake
cards.body:
For NEGLIGENCE to be established, FOUR elements must ALL be present:

1. DUTY of care existed (you were this patient's nurse)
2. BREACH of that duty (you fell below the standard a reasonable nurse would meet)
3. CAUSATION (that breach actually caused the harm)
4. DAMAGE (the patient was actually harmed)

All four. A mistake that harms nobody is not negligence. A bad outcome without a breach of standard is not negligence either.

RELATED TERMS:
• MALPRACTICE: professional negligence by a licensed practitioner
• ASSAULT: threatening to touch someone without consent
• BATTERY: actually touching or treating them without consent. Injecting a patient who has refused is battery
• DEFAMATION: damaging a reputation. Written is libel, spoken is slander
cards.clinicalNote: Giving an injection to a competent patient who has clearly refused it is not simply poor practice. It is battery, and it is a legal offence, however convinced you are that they need it.

cards.title: Self-check: correcting a charting error
cards.body:
You realise you have written an entry in the wrong patient's notes. How do you correct it?

Answer: DRAW A SINGLE LINE THROUGH IT so the original remains READABLE, write 'error', then sign, date and time. Never use correction fluid, never scribble it out, never erase. An obliterated record looks like concealment and destroys the credibility of the entire chart.

cards.title: The Cardiac Cycle in 30 Seconds
cards.body:
DIASTOLE (relaxation, filling):
• Atria contract → push final 20–30% blood into ventricles
• "Atrial kick", lost in AFib

SYSTOLE (contraction, ejection):
• AV valves CLOSE → S1 ("lub")
• Ventricles contract isovolumetrically
• Aortic & pulmonary valves open
• Blood ejects
• Semilunar valves CLOSE → S2 ("dub")

Stroke Volume = End-Diastolic − End-Systolic Volume

cards.title: Heart Sounds
cards.body:
S1 ("lub"): AV valves closing, beginning of systole
S2 ("dub"), semilunar valves closing, end of systole
S3, early diastole rapid filling, normal in young, pathological in HF (ventricular gallop)
S4, late diastole atrial kick, pathological, ventricular stiffness (atrial gallop)

Mnemonic: "Tennessee" = S4 S1 S2 (a-S4-S1-S2 = ten-ne-ssee). "Kentucky" = S1 S2 S3.

cards.title: Cardiac Output Equation
body: CO = SV × HR
Normal: ~4–8 L/min
SV depends on: Preload (filling), Afterload (resistance), Contractility
Frank-Starling Law: more stretch = stronger contraction (up to a point)
In heart failure, the curve flattens, more preload no longer helps

cards.title: The Conduction System: A Pacemaker Hierarchy
cards.body:
The impulse travels in one fixed order:
SA node → AV node → Bundle of His → Right and Left bundle branches → Purkinje fibres

Each site has its OWN intrinsic rate, and the fastest one in charge wins:
• SA node: 60–100 /min, the normal pacemaker
• AV node: 40–60 /min, the backup
• Ventricles / Purkinje: 20–40 /min, the last resort

The AV node deliberately DELAYS the impulse by about 0.1 second. That pause is what lets the atria finish emptying before the ventricles squeeze.

So a patient with complete heart block often sits at 30–40 bpm: the atria are firing normally but nothing is getting through, and the ventricles have fallen back on their own slow escape rhythm.
cards.clinicalNote: You find a pulse of 34 in an elderly patient who feels faint. Do not reach for atropine and stop there. A rate in the 30s is the ventricles pacing themselves, which means the higher centres have failed. This patient needs an ECG and pacing backup, not reassurance.

cards.title: Coronary Arteries and What They Feed
cards.body:
The heart feeds itself FIRST, during diastole, when the muscle is relaxed. Tachycardia shortens diastole, so a fast heart gets less of its own blood supply.

LEFT MAIN → divides into:
• Left Anterior Descending (LAD): anterior wall + most of the interventricular septum. Blocking it causes the largest infarcts, hence the nickname "the widow maker".
• Left Circumflex: lateral wall.

RIGHT CORONARY ARTERY (RCA): right ventricle, inferior wall, and in most people the SA and AV nodes.

That last point carries marks: an INFERIOR MI (RCA territory) classically comes with BRADYCARDIA and heart block, because the same artery supplies the nodes.

cards.title: Cardiac Self-Check: Trace the Blood
cards.body:
Blood leaves the right ventricle. Name every structure it passes through until it reaches the aorta.

Answer: Right ventricle → pulmonary valve → pulmonary artery → lungs (gas exchange) → pulmonary veins → LEFT ATRIUM → mitral valve → left ventricle → aortic valve → aorta. Remember the trick: the pulmonary ARTERY carries deoxygenated blood, and the pulmonary VEINS carry oxygenated blood. They are the only pair in the body that break the usual rule.

cards.title: ROME: Read an ABG Fast
cards.body:
Step 1 – pH: <7.35 acidosis, >7.45 alkalosis
Step 2 – ROME: Respiratory Opposite, Metabolic Equal
  • pH and CO₂ move OPPOSITE → respiratory
  • pH and HCO₃⁻ move the SAME way → metabolic
Step 3 – Compensation: is the other value shifting to push pH back to normal?

Normals: pH 7.35–7.45 · PaCO₂ 35–45 · HCO₃⁻ 22–26

cards.title: Common Causes
body: Metabolic acidosis: DKA, lactic acidosis, diarrhoea, renal failure
Metabolic alkalosis: vomiting, excess antacids, diuretics
Respiratory acidosis: hypoventilation, COPD, opioid overdose
Respiratory alkalosis: hyperventilation, anxiety, high altitude

cards.title: Compensation: Full, Partial or None
cards.body:
The body always tries to drag the pH back to normal. HOW FAR it has got tells you the stage.

UNCOMPENSATED: pH abnormal, only ONE value (CO₂ or HCO₃⁻) has moved. The other is still normal.
PARTIALLY COMPENSATED: pH still abnormal, but BOTH values have moved. The rescue has started and has not finished.
FULLY COMPENSATED: pH is back INSIDE 7.35–7.45, but both values are abnormal. The system is holding the line by working hard.

Which pH half? In full compensation, the pH sits on the side of the original problem:
• pH 7.36–7.39 with abnormal values → the original problem was ACIDOSIS
• pH 7.41–7.44 with abnormal values → the original problem was ALKALOSIS

Speed matters too. The LUNGS compensate in minutes (change the breathing rate). The KIDNEYS take hours to days.
cards.clinicalNote: A COPD patient's gas shows pH 7.37, CO₂ 60, HCO₃⁻ 34. The numbers look alarming but the pH is normal. This is chronic, fully compensated respiratory acidosis, their baseline. Do not chase the CO₂ with oxygen. Compare it with their previous gas, not with a textbook normal.

cards.title: Symptoms That Point to Acidosis or Alkalosis
body: ACIDOSIS, in general: drowsiness, confusion, headache, warm flushed skin, coma. The brain slows down.
Metabolic acidosis specifically: Kussmaul breathing, deep and fast, blowing off CO₂ to compensate
Acidosis pushes potassium OUT of cells → HYPERkalaemia. Watch for peaked T waves and arrhythmias.
ALKALOSIS, in general: agitation, light-headedness, tingling around the mouth and fingers, muscle twitching, tetany, seizures. The nerves become irritable.
Alkalosis drives potassium INTO cells → HYPOkalaemia. And it drops ionised calcium, which is what causes the tetany.
The memory hook: acid makes you SLEEPY, alkali makes you TWITCHY.

cards.title: ABG Self-Check: pH 7.25, CO₂ 55, HCO₃⁻ 24
cards.body:
Interpret it. pH 7.25, PaCO₂ 55 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 24 mEq/L.

Answer: UNCOMPENSATED RESPIRATORY ACIDOSIS. The pH is low (acidosis). CO₂ is high and moves OPPOSITE to the pH, so it is respiratory (ROME). HCO₃⁻ is still normal, so the kidneys have not started compensating yet. Cause: the patient is not ventilating. Think opioid overdose, COPD exacerbation, exhaustion. The fix is ventilation, not bicarbonate.

cards.title: From Nose to Alveolus: The Airway Map
cards.body:
UPPER AIRWAY: nose → pharynx → larynx (the epiglottis is the trapdoor that closes over the larynx when you swallow)

LOWER AIRWAY: trachea → carina → right and left main bronchi → bronchioles → terminal bronchioles → alveoli

Two facts that carry marks:
• The RIGHT main bronchus is WIDER, SHORTER and more VERTICAL. So an aspirated object, or a misplaced endotracheal tube, almost always ends up on the RIGHT.
• Everything above the terminal bronchioles is ANATOMICAL DEAD SPACE, roughly 150 mL in an adult. Air sits there but no gas exchange happens.

Gas exchange happens ONLY at the alveolus, across the alveolar-capillary membrane, by simple diffusion down a pressure gradient.
cards.clinicalNote: After intubation you hear breath sounds on the right but almost nothing on the left. Do not assume a collapsed left lung. The tube is most likely sitting in the right main bronchus. Deflate the cuff, withdraw slightly, re-auscultate both sides, and get the confirmatory X-ray.

cards.title: Lung Volumes and Capacities
body: Tidal Volume (TV): the air in a normal quiet breath, about 500 mL
Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV): the extra you can force IN on top of a normal breath, about 3000 mL
Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV): the extra you can force OUT after a normal breath, about 1100 mL
Residual Volume (RV): the air that ALWAYS stays behind, about 1200 mL. You can never breathe it out, and it is why lungs float.
Vital Capacity (VC) = TV + IRV + ERV, about 4600 mL. Everything you can move.
Total Lung Capacity (TLC) = VC + RV, about 5800 mL
A CAPACITY is just two or more volumes added together. Residual Volume can only be measured indirectly, never by simple spirometry.
In COPD, air trapping raises the Residual Volume, so the chest looks barrel-shaped.

cards.title: Oxyhaemoglobin Curve: CADET, Face Right
cards.body:
The curve describes how tightly haemoglobin HOLDS oxygen.

SHIFT RIGHT = haemoglobin RELEASES oxygen more easily, it gives O₂ to the tissues. Caused by CADET:
C: raised CO₂
A: Acidosis (low pH)
D: raised 2,3-DPG
E: Exercise
T: raised Temperature

"CADET, face RIGHT!"

SHIFT LEFT = haemoglobin HOLDS ON to oxygen, it loads in the lungs but does not let go in the tissues. Caused by the exact opposites: low CO₂, alkalosis, low 2,3-DPG, hypothermia. Also carbon monoxide and fetal haemoglobin.

Makes sense biologically: hard-working, hot, acidotic muscle is exactly where oxygen is needed, so the blood lets go of it there.
cards.clinicalNote: Your hypothermic trauma patient has a good SpO₂ of 98% but looks terrible. Cold shifts the curve LEFT, so the haemoglobin is loaded but refuses to hand oxygen over to the tissues. The pulse oximeter looks reassuring while the cells starve. Warm the patient.

cards.title: How Breathing Is Controlled
cards.body:
The respiratory centre sits in the MEDULLA (with support from the pons). It sets the rate and depth automatically.

CENTRAL CHEMORECEPTORS (in the medulla) are the main drivers, and they respond to CO₂, not oxygen. A rising CO₂ (really the resulting fall in cerebrospinal fluid pH) is the strongest, fastest stimulus to breathe.

PERIPHERAL CHEMORECEPTORS (carotid and aortic bodies) respond to a LOW OXYGEN level, but only when the PaO₂ falls below about 60 mmHg.

So in a healthy person: CO₂ drives breathing.

THE HYPOXIC DRIVE: in a chronic CO₂ retainer (severe COPD), the centre has been bathed in high CO₂ for years and has stopped responding to it. Their stimulus to breathe becomes the LOW oxygen level. That is why flooding them with high-flow oxygen can reduce their drive to breathe. Target their SpO₂ at 88–92%, and never withhold oxygen from a patient who is clearly hypoxic and dying.

cards.title: Ventilation and Perfusion: V/Q in One Card
cards.body:
V = ventilation, the air reaching the alveoli.
Q = perfusion, the blood reaching the capillaries.
Healthy ratio: roughly 0.8.

Two ways it goes wrong:

DEAD SPACE (high V/Q): the alveolus gets AIR but no BLOOD. Ventilation is wasted.
→ Classic cause: PULMONARY EMBOLISM. The clot blocks the blood, the air arrives with nowhere to go.

SHUNT (low V/Q): the alveolus gets BLOOD but no AIR. Blood passes through unoxygenated.
→ Classic causes: PNEUMONIA, atelectasis, pulmonary oedema, ARDS. The alveolus is full of pus or fluid.

The practical discriminator: shunt hypoxaemia responds POORLY to supplemental oxygen, because the oxygen cannot reach the blocked alveolus at all. If a patient stays hypoxic despite a non-rebreather, suspect a large shunt.

cards.title: Respiratory Self-Check: Which Gas Drives Breathing
cards.body:
In a healthy adult, what is the strongest stimulus to breathe: a rising CO₂ or a falling oxygen?

Answer: A RISING CO₂. The central chemoreceptors in the medulla respond to CO₂ and are the primary driver. Low oxygen only becomes a significant stimulus below a PaO₂ of about 60 mmHg. The reversal of this (the hypoxic drive) is the exception, and it belongs to the chronic CO₂ retainer.

cards.title: Central and Peripheral: The Big Map
cards.body:
CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM: brain + spinal cord.
• Cerebrum: thought, movement, sensation, speech
• Cerebellum: BALANCE and coordination. Damage gives ataxia, an unsteady wide gait, past-pointing.
• Brainstem (midbrain, pons, MEDULLA): the survival centres. The medulla runs breathing, heart rate and blood pressure. Damage here is life-ending.
• Hypothalamus: temperature, hunger, thirst, the master of the pituitary

PERIPHERAL NERVOUS SYSTEM: 12 cranial nerves + 31 pairs of spinal nerves.
• SOMATIC: voluntary, skeletal muscle
• AUTONOMIC: involuntary
  → SYMPATHETIC, "fight or flight": pupils dilate, heart races, bronchi dilate, gut slows, glucose rises
  → PARASYMPATHETIC, "rest and digest": pupils constrict, heart slows, gut works, bladder empties

The vagus nerve (CN X) carries most of the parasympathetic supply to the chest and abdomen. That is why bearing down, vomiting or suctioning too deeply can drop a heart rate: you are stimulating the vagus.

cards.title: The Twelve Cranial Nerves and Their Jobs
body: I Olfactory: smell
II Optic: vision
III Oculomotor: most eye movement, pupil CONSTRICTION, eyelid lift. A blown, fixed, dilated pupil means CN III is being compressed, a neurosurgical emergency.
IV Trochlear: eye moves down and in
V Trigeminal: facial SENSATION + chewing. The corneal reflex is its sensory arc.
VI Abducens: eye moves outward (abduction)
VII Facial: facial EXPRESSION + taste on the front of the tongue. Bell's palsy affects the WHOLE half of the face, including the forehead. A stroke SPARES the forehead.
VIII Vestibulocochlear: hearing and balance
IX Glossopharyngeal: swallowing, gag reflex, taste at the back
X Vagus: the parasympathetic highway. Heart, lungs, gut, plus the gag and swallow with IX.
XI Accessory: shoulder shrug, head turn
XII Hypoglossal: tongue movement. A tongue that deviates to one side is a sign of weakness on THAT side.
cards.clinicalNote: A head injury patient's left pupil is 6 mm and no longer reacts to light. That is CN III being crushed by a swelling brain pushing through the tentorium. This is not something to watch and see. Call now, this is minutes, not hours.

cards.title: Cranial Nerves: Names, and Sensory or Motor
cards.body:
THE NAMES, in order I to XII:
"On Old Olympus' Towering Top A Finn And German Viewed Some Hops"
Olfactory, Optic, Oculomotor, Trochlear, Trigeminal, Abducens, Facial, Auditory (Vestibulocochlear), Glossopharyngeal, Vagus, Accessory (spinal), Hypoglossal

SENSORY, MOTOR OR BOTH:
"Some Say Marry Money, But My Brother Says Big Brains Matter Most"
S S M M B M B S B B M M

So:
• Purely SENSORY: I, II, VIII
• Purely MOTOR: III, IV, VI, XI, XII
• BOTH: V, VII, IX, X

cards.title: Glasgow Coma Scale in Plain Words
cards.body:
Three parts. Always chart the three sub-scores, never just the total. E4 V1 M5 tells the doctor far more than "GCS 10".

EYE OPENING, out of 4:
4 spontaneous · 3 to speech · 2 to pain · 1 none

VERBAL RESPONSE, out of 5:
5 orientated · 4 confused conversation · 3 inappropriate words · 2 incomprehensible sounds · 1 none

MOTOR RESPONSE, out of 6:
6 obeys commands · 5 localises to pain · 4 withdraws from pain · 3 abnormal FLEXION (decorticate) · 2 EXTENSION (decerebrate) · 1 none

MINIMUM 3, MAXIMUM 15. There is no zero, a dead patient still scores 3.

Severity: 13–15 mild · 9–12 moderate · 8 or less SEVERE.

GCS 8 or less: the airway is not safe. "Less than 8, intubate."

Decorticate (flexion, arms drawn toward the CORE) is BETTER than decerebrate (extension). A patient sliding from flexion to extension is deteriorating. Report it immediately.

cards.title: Raised Intracranial Pressure: The Warning Signs
cards.body:
The skull is a closed box holding brain, blood and cerebrospinal fluid. Add anything (a bleed, a tumour, oedema) and the pressure rises, because nothing can expand outward.

EARLY signs (act here):
• A DECREASING LEVEL OF CONSCIOUSNESS, the earliest and most sensitive sign
• Headache, worse in the morning and on coughing or straining
• Projectile vomiting, often WITHOUT nausea
• Restlessness, irritability, confusion
• Pupil changes, sluggish then unequal

LATE signs (the brain is herniating):
• CUSHING'S TRIAD: a RISING systolic BP with a WIDENING pulse pressure, BRADYCARDIA, and IRREGULAR breathing
• A fixed, dilated pupil
• Posturing, decorticate then decerebrate

Nursing actions: head of bed at 30°, head in MIDLINE (a turned head kinks the jugular veins and traps blood in the skull), avoid neck flexion, keep the patient calm, prevent coughing and straining, treat fever and pain.
cards.clinicalNote: Cushing's triad is not an early warning. By the time you see a bradycardia of 45 with a BP of 190/70, the brainstem is already being squeezed. The sign you must act on is the one four hours earlier: a patient who was chatting and is now just a little drowsy and hard to rouse.

cards.title: Neuro Self-Check: The Lowest Possible GCS
cards.body:
A patient does not open their eyes, makes no sound and does not move at all. What is their GCS?

Answer: 3, and that is the LOWEST possible score. E1 + V1 + M1 = 3. There is no score of 0. Anything of 8 or below means the airway is unprotected and needs securing.

cards.title: The Nephron: Where Each Thing Happens
cards.body:
The nephron is the functional unit, about 1 million per kidney. Three processes: FILTRATION, REABSORPTION, SECRETION.

GLOMERULUS: filtration. Pressure pushes water and small solutes out of the blood. Protein and red cells are too big to pass. Finding them in urine means the filter is damaged.

PROXIMAL CONVOLUTED TUBULE: the workhorse. About 65–70% of everything is reabsorbed here, and ALL of the glucose and amino acids. Glucose only appears in urine once the blood level overwhelms the transporters, at roughly 180 mg/dL.

LOOP OF HENLE: creates the concentration gradient. The DESCENDING limb is permeable to WATER only. The ASCENDING limb pumps out SALT (and is water-impermeable).

DISTAL CONVOLUTED TUBULE: fine tuning. ALDOSTERONE acts here and downstream: retain sodium and water, excrete potassium.

COLLECTING DUCT: the final water decision. ADH (vasopressin) makes it permeable to water → concentrated urine, water retained. No ADH → dilute urine.

Memory hook: ADH = "Add water back to the blood."

cards.title: Renal Numbers You Must Know
body: Urine output, minimum acceptable: 30 mL/hour in an adult, or 0.5 mL/kg/hour. Below that is OLIGURIA. Report it.
Normal daily output: about 1–2 litres
Anuria: less than 100 mL in 24 hours
Serum creatinine: about 0.6–1.2 mg/dL. It is the more SPECIFIC marker of kidney function.
Blood urea nitrogen (BUN): about 7–20 mg/dL. It also rises with dehydration, GI bleeding and a high protein intake, so it is less specific.
GFR: about 90–120 mL/min. Below 60 for 3 months or more defines chronic kidney disease.
Urine specific gravity: 1.005–1.030. HIGH means concentrated urine, think dehydration. LOW means dilute urine.
Normal urine has NO protein, NO glucose, NO ketones, NO blood.
Urine output is the most sensitive bedside marker of tissue perfusion. When the kidneys stop making urine, the organs are not being perfused.
cards.clinicalNote: Your post-op patient has passed 20 mL in the last two hours. It is tempting to chart it and wait for the morning round. Do not. Falling urine output is often the FIRST sign that the circulation is failing, appearing before the blood pressure ever drops. Check the catheter for a kink, then escalate.

cards.title: Where the Diuretics Act on the Nephron
cards.body:
Walk down the nephron in order:

1. PROXIMAL tubule → Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (acetazolamide). Weak. Also used in glaucoma.
2. LOOP of Henle, thick ascending limb → LOOP diuretics (furosemide). The most POWERFUL. Wastes potassium AND calcium.
3. DISTAL convoluted tubule → THIAZIDES (hydrochlorothiazide). Moderate. Wastes potassium but KEEPS calcium.
4. COLLECTING duct → POTASSIUM-SPARING (spironolactone, an aldosterone blocker). Weak, but it SAVES potassium.

The exam discriminator, calcium:
• LOOP diuretics LOSE calcium
• THIAZIDES KEEP calcium
"Loops LOSE calcium, thiazides TAKE it back."

The potassium warning: furosemide and thiazides both drop potassium, which is exactly why they are so dangerous alongside DIGOXIN. Low potassium multiplies digoxin toxicity.

cards.title: Hormones That Control Fluid Balance
cards.body:
ADH (antidiuretic hormone / vasopressin), from the POSTERIOR pituitary:
• Trigger: rising blood osmolality, or a falling blood volume
• Effect: the collecting duct reabsorbs WATER. Urine becomes concentrated and scanty.
• Too MUCH ADH = SIADH: water retention, dilutional HYPOnatraemia, concentrated urine
• Too LITTLE ADH = DIABETES INSIPIDUS: litres of pale, dilute urine, raging thirst, HYPERnatraemia

ALDOSTERONE, from the adrenal CORTEX:
• Effect: retain SODIUM and water, excrete POTASSIUM
• Memory: "Save sodium, Sack potassium."

RENIN-ANGIOTENSIN-ALDOSTERONE SYSTEM (RAAS): low renal perfusion → renin → angiotensin I → (ACE, in the lung) → angiotensin II, a powerful vasoconstrictor → aldosterone → sodium and water retained. Blood pressure rises. ACE inhibitors block this chain.

ANP (atrial natriuretic peptide), released by a STRETCHED atrium when the volume is too high, does the exact opposite: it dumps sodium and water. It is the body's own diuretic.

cards.title: Acute Kidney Injury: Pre-Renal, Renal, Post-Renal
cards.body:
Find the level of the problem and the management writes itself.

PRE-RENAL, the blood never arrives. The kidney itself is healthy and thirsty.
• Causes: hypovolaemia, haemorrhage, dehydration, shock, heart failure
• Clues: concentrated urine, high specific gravity, low urinary sodium, a BUN:creatinine ratio above 20:1
• Fix: RESTORE THE PERFUSION. Fluids. This is the most reversible type, and the most common.

INTRA-RENAL, the kidney tissue itself is damaged.
• Causes: nephrotoxic drugs (aminoglycosides, NSAIDs, contrast dye), acute tubular necrosis, glomerulonephritis, prolonged untreated pre-renal injury
• Clues: dilute urine despite dehydration (the tubules cannot concentrate), specific gravity fixed near 1.010
• Fix: stop the toxin, support the kidney

POST-RENAL, the urine cannot get OUT.
• Causes: prostate enlargement, stones, tumour, a blocked catheter
• Clues: sudden anuria, a distended, painful bladder
• Fix: RELIEVE THE OBSTRUCTION. Sometimes a bladder scan and a catheter flush fix an "acute kidney injury" in 60 seconds.
cards.clinicalNote: You are told a patient has passed no urine for six hours and needs urgent bloods. Before anything else, feel the lower abdomen and scan the bladder. A blocked catheter and a bladder full to the umbilicus is post-renal, and it is fixed at the bedside, not in the lab.

cards.title: Renal Self-Check: The Minimum Hourly Urine Output
cards.body:
Your 70 kg adult patient has passed 90 mL over the last 4 hours. Is that acceptable?

Answer: NO. That is roughly 22 mL/hour. The minimum is 30 mL/hour, or 0.5 mL/kg/hour, which for 70 kg is 35 mL/hour. This is OLIGURIA. Check the catheter for obstruction, assess perfusion and fluid balance, and escalate.

cards.title: The Pituitary: The Master Gland
cards.body:
The HYPOTHALAMUS instructs the pituitary. The pituitary instructs everyone else.

ANTERIOR PITUITARY makes its own hormones:
• GH: growth. Excess in a child = gigantism. Excess in an adult (plates fused) = ACROMEGALY, the hands, jaw and feet enlarge.
• TSH → tells the thyroid to work
• ACTH → tells the adrenal cortex to make cortisol
• FSH and LH → the gonads
• Prolactin → milk production
• Memory: "FLAT PiG" (FSH, LH, ACTH, TSH, Prolactin, GH)

POSTERIOR PITUITARY makes NOTHING. It only STORES and releases two hormones made in the hypothalamus:
• ADH (water retention)
• OXYTOCIN (uterine contraction and milk ejection)

The feedback loop that examiners love: in PRIMARY hypothyroidism the thyroid has failed, so T3 and T4 are LOW and the pituitary screams louder, pushing TSH HIGH. A high TSH with a low T4 = primary hypothyroidism. The two moving in OPPOSITE directions tells you the problem is in the gland itself, not the pituitary.

cards.title: Gland, Hormone, Job
body: Thyroid → T3 and T4 → set the metabolic rate. Also calcitonin → LOWERS blood calcium.
Parathyroid (4 tiny glands behind the thyroid) → PTH → RAISES blood calcium
Adrenal CORTEX → 3 layers, 3 products: aldosterone (salt), cortisol (sugar and stress), androgens (sex). Memory: "Salt, Sugar, Sex, the deeper you go the sweeter it gets."
Adrenal MEDULLA → adrenaline and noradrenaline → the fight-or-flight surge
Pancreas, beta cells → INSULIN → drives glucose INTO cells, lowers blood sugar
Pancreas, alpha cells → GLUCAGON → releases glucose from the liver, raises blood sugar
Insulin also drives POTASSIUM into cells. That is why it is a treatment for hyperkalaemia, and why insulin therapy in DKA drops the serum potassium.
A total thyroidectomy risks accidentally removing the parathyroids. Watch for tetany, tingling and a positive Chvostek's or Trousseau's sign in the first 24 to 48 hours.

cards.title: Thyroid: Hyper Speeds Up, Hypo Slows Down
cards.body:
Every symptom follows from one idea: the thyroid sets the body's SPEED.

HYPERthyroid, everything is FAST (Graves' disease):
• Weight LOSS despite a big appetite
• HEAT intolerance, sweating
• Tachycardia, palpitations, atrial fibrillation
• DIARRHOEA
• Anxiety, tremor, insomnia
• Exophthalmos (bulging eyes), goitre
• Crisis: THYROID STORM, fever, tachycardia, agitation, a medical emergency

HYPOthyroid, everything is SLOW (Hashimoto's):
• Weight GAIN despite a poor appetite
• COLD intolerance
• Bradycardia
• CONSTIPATION
• Fatigue, depression, slow speech and thought
• Dry skin, hair loss, puffy face
• Crisis: MYXOEDEMA COMA, hypothermia, bradycardia, a medical emergency

Memory: "HYPER = HOT and FAST. HYPO = COLD and SLOW."

Levothyroxine is taken in the MORNING, on an EMPTY stomach, 30 to 60 minutes before food, and it is lifelong.

cards.title: Cortisol: Cushing's Versus Addison's
cards.body:
Cortisol raises sugar, raises blood pressure, suppresses immunity and breaks down protein.

CUSHING'S, too MUCH cortisol (commonest cause: long-term steroid therapy):
• Moon face, buffalo hump, central obesity with thin limbs
• Purple striae, thin skin, easy bruising
• HYPERglycaemia
• HYPERtension, sodium and water retention
• HYPOkalaemia
• Osteoporosis, muscle wasting, poor wound healing, infections

ADDISON'S, too LITTLE cortisol:
• Fatigue, weakness, weight LOSS, anorexia
• HYPOglycaemia
• HYPOtension, salt craving
• HYPERkalaemia, HYPOnatraemia
• BRONZE hyperpigmentation of the skin (a clue exclusive to primary Addison's)
• Crisis: ADDISONIAN CRISIS, profound hypotension and vascular collapse

The pattern to memorise: everything is a MIRROR. Cushing's is high sugar, high sodium, high BP, low potassium. Addison's is the exact opposite.
cards.clinicalNote: A patient on long-term prednisolone stops it suddenly because they "felt better". Days later they are hypotensive and collapsing. Their own adrenal glands have been switched off for months and cannot restart on demand. Steroids are ALWAYS tapered, never stopped abruptly. Teach this on every steroid discharge.

cards.title: Calcium Control: PTH Versus Calcitonin
cards.body:
Two hormones, opposite jobs. Normal serum calcium: about 8.5–10.5 mg/dL.

PTH (parathyroid hormone) RAISES calcium:
• Pulls calcium out of BONE
• Reabsorbs calcium in the KIDNEY
• Activates vitamin D → absorbs calcium from the GUT
• Memory: "PTH = Pulls The calcium Higher"

CALCITONIN (from the thyroid) LOWERS calcium:
• Drives calcium back INTO bone
• Memory: "CalciTONIN TONES DOWN calcium"

Calcium and phosphate move in OPPOSITE directions. Calcium up, phosphate down.

HYPOcalcaemia = irritable nerves and muscles: tingling around the mouth and fingertips, cramps, TETANY, laryngospasm, seizures. Two bedside signs:
• CHVOSTEK'S: tap the facial nerve in front of the ear → the cheek twitches
• TROUSSEAU'S: inflate a BP cuff above systolic for 3 minutes → the hand cramps into a claw

HYPERcalcaemia = sluggish everything: "stones, bones, abdominal groans and psychiatric moans". Kidney stones, bone pain, constipation, confusion, muscle weakness.

cards.title: Endocrine Self-Check: The Addisonian Crisis
cards.body:
A known Addison's patient develops severe vomiting and diarrhoea. What is the immediate danger, and what does she need?

Answer: ADDISONIAN CRISIS. Any physical stress (illness, infection, surgery, trauma) demands extra cortisol, and her adrenals cannot supply it. She will collapse with profound hypotension, hyponatraemia and hyperkalaemia. She needs IV hydrocortisone and IV normal saline, urgently. Teach every Addison's patient the sick-day rule: when you are ill, the steroid dose goes UP, never down.

cards.title: Bone, Muscle and Joint Basics
cards.body:
BONE:
• 206 bones in the adult skeleton
• AXIAL skeleton: skull, vertebral column, ribs, sternum. The core.
• APPENDICULAR skeleton: limbs and their girdles. The attachments.
• OSTEOBLASTS BUILD bone. OSTEOCLASTS CHEW bone away. Memory: "blasts Build, clasts Consume."
• Bone stores 99% of the body's calcium, and the red marrow makes blood cells.

MUSCLE, three types:
• SKELETAL: voluntary, striated, moves the skeleton
• CARDIAC: involuntary, striated, only in the heart
• SMOOTH: involuntary, non-striated, in the gut, vessels and bladder

JOINTS:
• Fibrous (immovable): skull sutures
• Cartilaginous (slightly movable): the intervertebral discs, the pubic symphysis
• SYNOVIAL (freely movable): the ones that matter clinically, with a capsule, cartilage and synovial fluid. Knee, hip, shoulder, elbow.

TENDON joins muscle to BONE. LIGAMENT joins bone to BONE. Memory: "LigaMENT connects bone to bone, both start with the same idea of a MENDing strap between bones."

cards.title: Fracture Types You Must Recognise
body: CLOSED (simple): the skin is intact
OPEN (compound): the bone has broken THROUGH the skin. High infection risk, this is a surgical emergency.
GREENSTICK: an incomplete break, the bone bends and cracks on one side. Seen in CHILDREN, whose bones are soft.
COMMINUTED: the bone is shattered into three or more fragments. Often high energy.
TRANSVERSE: a straight break across the bone. Usually a direct blow.
OBLIQUE: an angled break
SPIRAL: caused by a TWISTING force. In a non-mobile infant, a spiral fracture is a red flag for non-accidental injury.
IMPACTED: one fragment is driven into the other
PATHOLOGICAL: a break through diseased bone (tumour, osteoporosis) from trivial or no force
AVULSION: a tendon or ligament tears a fragment of bone away with it

cards.title: Neurovascular Check: The 6 Ps
cards.body:
After ANY fracture, cast, splint or traction, check distal to the injury, every single time:

P: PAIN, especially pain OUT OF PROPORTION to the injury, and pain on PASSIVE STRETCH of the fingers or toes. This is the EARLIEST sign of compartment syndrome.
P: PALLOR, the limb is white or dusky
P: PULSELESSNESS, a LATE and ominous sign
P: PARAESTHESIA, pins and needles, numbness
P: PARALYSIS, cannot move it, a LATE sign
P: POIKILOTHERMIA, the limb is cold

COMPARTMENT SYNDROME is the emergency you are screening for. Pressure builds inside a closed muscle compartment, blood cannot get in, muscle dies within hours.

What you do: DO NOT elevate the limb above heart level (it lowers arterial inflow), DO NOT apply ice, remove or split the cast and the dressings, and CALL SURGICALLY, now. The definitive treatment is a fasciotomy.

Do not wait for pulselessness. By then the muscle is already dead.
cards.clinicalNote: A child with a new arm cast keeps crying and the analgesia is not touching it. The temptation is to give more morphine and settle her. Don't. Escalating pain under a cast, made worse when you straighten her fingers, is compartment syndrome until proven otherwise. Split the cast and get a surgeon.

cards.title: Layers of the Skin and What They Do
cards.body:
The skin is the body's LARGEST organ and its first line of defence.

EPIDERMIS, the outer layer:
• No blood vessels. It is fed by diffusion from below.
• Contains keratin (waterproofing) and melanin (UV protection)
• Fully replaced roughly every 4 weeks

DERMIS, the working layer:
• Blood vessels, nerve endings, hair follicles, sweat and sebaceous glands
• Collagen and elastin, the strength and the stretch
• This is where PAIN and pressure are FELT. A burn that is intensely painful still has living nerve endings, so it is partial thickness.

HYPODERMIS (subcutaneous):
• Fat. Insulation, cushioning, energy store
• The layer used for subcutaneous injections such as insulin and heparin

FUNCTIONS: protection, temperature regulation, sensation, vitamin D synthesis, excretion, and fluid balance. Lose enough skin, as in a major burn, and you lose all six at once. That is why burns kill by fluid loss and infection, not by the burn itself.

cards.title: How Bone and Wounds Heal
cards.body:
FRACTURE HEALING, five stages:
1. HAEMATOMA formation, within hours. A clot fills the break.
2. Fibrocartilaginous (SOFT) CALLUS, days. A soft bridge forms.
3. Bony (HARD) CALLUS, weeks. The bridge calcifies.
4. OSSIFICATION
5. REMODELLING, months. The bone is reshaped along the lines of stress.

What SLOWS it: poor blood supply, movement at the site, infection, age, smoking, diabetes, steroids, poor nutrition. What HELPS: immobilisation, protein, calcium, vitamin D and vitamin C.

WOUND HEALING, four phases:
1. HAEMOSTASIS, minutes. Vasoconstriction and clotting.
2. INFLAMMATION, days 1 to 4. Redness, warmth, swelling, pain. This is NORMAL and necessary, it is not automatically infection.
3. PROLIFERATION, days 4 to 21. Granulation tissue (beefy red), new vessels, contraction, epithelialisation.
4. MATURATION, up to 1 to 2 YEARS. The scar remodels and pales. Scar tissue only ever regains about 80% of the original strength.

Healing by PRIMARY intention: clean, closed edges, minimal scar (a surgical incision).
By SECONDARY intention: the wound is left open to granulate up from the base (a pressure ulcer, an abscess). Slower, bigger scar.
By TERTIARY intention: left open deliberately, closed later once infection is controlled.
cards.clinicalNote: A relative points at a red, warm, slightly swollen surgical wound on day 2 and asks if it is infected. Usually it is not. That is the inflammatory phase doing its job. The things that DO signal infection: pus, a foul smell, spreading redness, increasing pain after day 3, fever, and wound edges pulling apart.

cards.title: MSK Self-Check: The Cast That Feels Too Tight
cards.body:
Six hours after a below-knee cast is applied, the patient reports deep, worsening pain and numb toes. The toes are pale and cold. What is your FIRST action?

Answer: DO NOT ELEVATE, DO NOT ICE, and do not give more analgesia and leave. Suspect COMPARTMENT SYNDROME. The cast must be split or removed immediately and a surgeon called. Elevating the limb above the heart lowers arterial inflow and makes the ischaemia worse. Muscle dies within 4 to 6 hours.

cards.title: DKA vs HHS: How To Tell Them Apart
cards.body:
DKA (Type 1, younger, acute):
• Glucose 250–600
• Severe ketosis ↑↑
• pH < 7.3 (acidosis)
• Kussmaul breathing, fruity breath
• Onset: hours to days

HHS (Type 2, older, slower):
• Glucose >600 (often >1000)
• Minimal ketosis
• pH > 7.3 (no acidosis)
• Profound dehydration, altered mental state
• Onset: days to weeks
cards.clinicalNote: A young Type 1 diabetic arrives drowsy, breathing deeply, with fruity breath. Think DKA first: check glucose, ketones and pH. The deep 'Kussmaul' breathing is the body dumping acid. Never mistake it for simple anxiety.

cards.title: DKA Management Priorities
cards.body:
F-I-K (in that order):

FLUIDS first: NS 1L in first hour, then continue
INSULIN second, regular insulin infusion 0.1 U/kg/hr
POTASSIUM third, replenish before serum K+ drops with insulin therapy (insulin drives K+ into cells)

DO NOT give insulin first without fluids, risk of vascular collapse.
DO NOT give insulin if K+ < 3.3. Give K+ first.

cards.title: Hypoglycaemia: Rule of 15
body: Conscious patient with BG < 70: give 15g fast carbs (e.g. 3 glucose tabs, 150mL juice)
Recheck in 15 minutes
If still <70, repeat
When >70 → give a complex carb + protein snack
Unconscious: IV dextrose 25% 50mL OR IM glucagon 1 mg
NEVER give oral glucose to an unconscious patient (aspiration)

cards.title: DKA: the numbers that define it
body: Blood glucose > 250 mg/dL (in HHS it is > 600)
Arterial pH < 7.3 and bicarbonate < 18 mEq/L
Ketones present in blood or urine
Anion gap > 12: (Na+) minus (Cl− plus HCO3−)
Kussmaul breathing: deep, sighing, trying to blow off CO2
Fruity acetone breath, the smell is the diagnosis in the corridor
cards.clinicalNote: A young Type 1 patient is drowsy, breathing deeply and smells of nail polish remover. Do not treat the drowsiness as sleep. That breathing pattern is the body compensating for acidosis, and it stops when they tire, which is the moment they crash.

cards.title: Self-check: insulin and potassium
cards.body:
A DKA patient arrives with serum potassium 3.0 mEq/L. The doctor orders an insulin infusion. What do you do first?

Answer: HOLD THE INSULIN AND GIVE POTASSIUM. Insulin drives potassium into the cells, so starting it with a K+ below 3.3 can drop the level far enough to stop the heart. Correct the potassium first, then start insulin.

cards.title: Four Types at a Glance
cards.body:
HYPOVOLAEMIC: blood/fluid loss → ↓preload (haemorrhage, burns)
CARDIOGENIC: pump failure (MI) → ↓cardiac output, congestion
DISTRIBUTIVE: massive vasodilation (septic, anaphylactic, neurogenic) → ↓SVR, warm then cold
OBSTRUCTIVE: physical block (tamponade, tension pneumothorax, PE)

All end the same way: inadequate tissue perfusion → cellular hypoxia.

cards.title: Early Recognition & Priorities
body: Early signs: tachycardia, restlessness, cool clammy skin, narrowing pulse pressure
Late signs: hypotension, weak pulse, oliguria (<30 mL/hr), altered consciousness
Position: flat with legs raised (except cardiogenic → semi-Fowler)
Hypovolaemic: stop bleeding + IV fluids; Anaphylactic: IM adrenaline first
Monitor urine output: the most sensitive bedside perfusion marker

cards.title: Shock: the shock index shortcut
cards.body:
Shock Index = heart rate ÷ systolic BP.

Normal is 0.5 to 0.7.
Above 0.9 means significant shock, even if the BP still looks acceptable.

HR 120 ÷ SBP 100 = 1.2. That patient is in shock while their blood pressure still reads 100.

This is how you catch compensated shock before the BP falls.

cards.title: Shock: the first five nursing actions
body: Position flat with legs raised, unless breathing is compromised
High flow oxygen, aim for SpO2 above 94%
Two large bore IV cannulas, 16G or 18G, before the veins collapse
Fluid resuscitation as prescribed, warmed if possible
Continuous monitoring: HR, BP, urine output and level of consciousness
Urine output below 0.5 mL/kg/hr is the kidney telling you perfusion has failed
cards.clinicalNote: You cannot get a line into a shocked patient later. Veins collapse as shock progresses, so the time to get two large cannulas in is now, while you still can, not after the BP has fallen.

cards.title: Self-check: which shock is warm
cards.body:
A septic patient has warm, flushed skin and a bounding pulse. Their BP is 84/40. Is this shock?

Answer: YES, WARM SEPTIC SHOCK. Early sepsis causes vasodilation, so the skin stays warm and pink while the blood pressure falls. Cold, clammy skin is hypovolaemic and cardiogenic shock. Warm skin does not mean the patient is stable.

cards.title: MI: recognising it fast
cards.body:
Classic: crushing central chest pain, radiating to the left arm or jaw, lasting more than 20 minutes, not relieved by rest or nitrates.

Associated: sweating, nausea, breathlessness, a sense of doom.

ATYPICAL presentations, which examiners love:
• Women: fatigue, jaw or back pain
• Diabetics: SILENT MI, neuropathy blunts the pain
• Elderly: confusion or sudden breathlessness only

An MI without chest pain is still an MI.
cards.clinicalNote: An elderly diabetic on your ward is suddenly confused and short of breath, with no pain at all. Do not go looking for an infection first. Get an ECG. Diabetics infarct silently, and this is exactly how a silent MI presents.

cards.title: MI: the diagnostic markers in order
body: Troponin I and T: rise in 3–4 hours, peak at 24 hours, stay up 10–14 days. The gold standard
CK-MB: rises in 4–6 hours, peaks at 18 hours, normal by 72 hours. Useful for RE-infarction
Myoglobin: rises earliest at 1–3 hours but is NOT specific to heart muscle
ECG: ST elevation means STEMI, ST depression or T inversion means NSTEMI
A normal first troponin does NOT rule out an MI. It must be repeated
cards.clinicalNote: Troponin stays elevated for up to two weeks, which is useful for a late diagnosis but useless for asking whether the patient has infarcted AGAIN. For a suspected re-infarction, CK-MB is the marker, because it has already returned to normal.

cards.title: MI: MONA greets every chest pain
cards.body:
M: Morphine (for pain not relieved by nitrates)
O: Oxygen (only if SpO2 is below 90%, routine oxygen is now discouraged)
N: Nitroglycerin (sublingual, up to 3 doses, 5 minutes apart)
A: Aspirin 300 mg, CHEWED, not swallowed whole
