Chewing matters. It is absorbed through the buccal mucosa and works far faster.

DO NOT give nitrates if the patient has taken sildenafil in the last 24 hours, or if the systolic BP is below 90. Both cause catastrophic hypotension.

cards.title: MI: the reperfusion clock
cards.body:
TIME IS MUSCLE. Every minute of occlusion kills myocardium.

Primary PCI (angioplasty): the gold standard. Target door to balloon under 90 minutes.

Thrombolysis (streptokinase, tenecteplase): when PCI is not available. Target door to needle under 30 minutes. Best within 3 hours, still useful up to 12 hours.

ABSOLUTE contraindications to thrombolysis:
• Any previous intracranial haemorrhage
• Ischaemic stroke within 3 months
• Active internal bleeding
• Suspected aortic dissection
• Known intracranial tumour or vascular malformation

cards.title: MI: post-infarct nursing priorities
body: Complete bed rest for the first 12–24 hours, then graded mobilisation
Continuous cardiac monitoring: arrhythmia is the commonest cause of early death
Watch for ventricular fibrillation, most likely in the first hour
Stool softeners: straining (Valsalva) can trigger a fatal arrhythmia
Small, low salt, low fat meals. A large meal diverts blood to the gut
Cardiac rehabilitation and secondary prevention teaching before discharge
cards.clinicalNote: A stool softener looks like a trivial order on a cardiac chart. It is not. Straining at stool raises intrathoracic pressure and drops venous return, and in an irritable post infarct heart that Valsalva can be the last thing a patient does.

cards.title: Self-check: chew or swallow
cards.body:
Why is aspirin CHEWED in a suspected MI rather than swallowed?

Answer: FOR SPEED. Chewing lets aspirin absorb through the mouth's mucosa and inhibit platelets within minutes, instead of waiting for gastric dissolution. In a coronary thrombosis, those minutes are myocardium.

cards.title: Heart failure: left versus right
cards.body:
LEFT sided failure backs blood up into the LUNGS.
• Breathlessness, orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea
• Crackles at the lung bases
• Pink frothy sputum in acute pulmonary oedema
• Fatigue, because the output forward is poor

RIGHT sided failure backs blood up into the BODY.
• Pitting oedema of the ankles and sacrum
• Raised jugular venous pressure
• Enlarged tender liver, ascites
• Weight gain before the swelling is even visible

The commonest cause of right sided failure is left sided failure.
cards.clinicalNote: Weight is the most sensitive fluid sign you have, and it is free. A gain of 2 kg in two days is roughly two litres of retained fluid, and it appears days before the ankles swell. Teach every heart failure patient to weigh themselves each morning.

cards.title: Heart failure: LEFT is lungs
cards.body:
LEFT = LUNGS.
RIGHT = REST of the body.

Say it once and you will never mix them up in an exam.

Left ventricle fails → blood backs up behind it → into the pulmonary veins → wet lungs.
Right ventricle fails → blood backs up behind it → into the systemic veins → swollen legs, big liver, raised JVP.

cards.title: Acute pulmonary oedema: what to do first
body: Sit the patient UPRIGHT with legs dependent. This alone reduces preload
High flow oxygen, then consider non invasive ventilation (CPAP)
IV loop diuretic: furosemide, as prescribed
Nitrates to reduce preload, provided the BP allows it
Morphine to relieve distress and reduce preload, used cautiously
Strict fluid balance and hourly urine output
Do NOT lie this patient flat, even to examine them
cards.clinicalNote: Sitting the patient up and dropping their legs over the edge of the bed costs nothing, takes five seconds and pools blood in the legs, away from the flooded lungs. Do it before you draw up a single drug.

cards.title: Heart failure: the drugs and what they do
cards.body:
ACE inhibitors (enalapril, ramipril): first line, reduce afterload and improve survival. Watch for a dry cough and hyperkalaemia.

Beta blockers (metoprolol, carvedilol): improve survival long term. Start LOW, go SLOW. Never start during acute decompensation.

Diuretics (furosemide): relieve symptoms, do NOT improve survival. Monitor potassium.

Digoxin: improves symptoms and rate control. Narrow therapeutic window: 0.5–2 ng/mL.

Spironolactone: potassium SPARING, improves survival. Risk of hyperkalaemia.

cards.title: Digoxin toxicity: the signs to catch
body: Therapeutic range is narrow: 0.5–2.0 ng/mL
Early: anorexia, nausea, vomiting. Almost always the first sign
Visual: yellow or green halos around lights, blurred vision
Cardiac: bradycardia, any new arrhythmia
HYPOKALAEMIA sharply increases the risk, and diuretics cause hypokalaemia
ALWAYS count an apical pulse for one full minute before giving digoxin
Withhold and report if the adult pulse is below 60
cards.clinicalNote: The patient on furosemide and digoxin together is the classic setup. The diuretic drops the potassium, the low potassium makes the heart more sensitive to digoxin, and a dose that was safe last week becomes toxic this week. Check the potassium, not just the digoxin level.

cards.title: Self-check: the apical pulse rule
cards.body:
Your patient's apical pulse is 54 and digoxin is due. What do you do?

Answer: WITHHOLD IT AND REPORT. Digoxin slows conduction, so giving it to a patient already below 60 risks dangerous bradycardia and heart block. Count apically for a full minute, document, and escalate.

cards.title: Hypertension: urgency versus emergency
cards.body:
HYPERTENSIVE URGENCY: BP is very high (often > 180/120) but there is NO evidence of acute organ damage. Lower it gradually, over 24 to 48 hours, with ORAL drugs.

HYPERTENSIVE EMERGENCY: very high BP WITH acute target organ damage.
• Brain: encephalopathy, stroke, headache, seizures
• Heart: chest pain, pulmonary oedema
• Kidney: rising creatinine, haematuria
• Eye: papilloedema, retinal haemorrhage
This needs IV drugs in a monitored setting.

The difference is not the number. It is the ORGAN DAMAGE.
cards.clinicalNote: The temptation with a BP of 220/130 is to bring it down fast. Resist it. Dropping the pressure by more than about 25% in the first hour can starve a brain that has adapted to a high perfusion pressure, and you cause the stroke you were trying to prevent.

cards.title: Measuring BP: the errors that change the answer
body: Cuff too SMALL gives a falsely HIGH reading. Too large reads falsely low
Cuff bladder should cover about 80% of the arm circumference
Arm must be supported at HEART level. A hanging arm reads high
Patient seated, back supported, feet flat, rested for 5 minutes
No caffeine, smoking or exercise for 30 minutes before
Legs crossed raise the reading. Talking raises the reading
Diagnose hypertension on at least two readings on two separate visits

cards.title: Antihypertensives: ABCD
cards.body:
A: ACE inhibitors and ARBs (ramipril, losartan)
B: Beta blockers (atenolol, metoprolol)
C: Calcium channel blockers (amlodipine, nifedipine)
D: Diuretics (thiazides, hydrochlorothiazide)

The side effect that gives each away:
ACE inhibitor → the dry, persistent COUGH (switch to an ARB)
Beta blocker → bradycardia, masks the signs of hypoglycaemia in diabetics
Calcium channel blocker → ankle oedema, flushing, headache
Thiazide → hypokalaemia, raised uric acid, can precipitate gout

cards.title: Hypertension: lifestyle first, and it works
cards.body:
The DASH approach and lifestyle changes are exam favourites because they genuinely reduce BP:

• Salt below 5–6 g a day. This alone can drop systolic BP by several mmHg
• Weight loss: roughly 1 mmHg drop for every 1 kg lost
• DASH diet: rich in fruit, vegetables, low fat dairy, low in saturated fat
• Aerobic exercise 30 minutes most days
• Limit alcohol, stop smoking
• Potassium rich foods, unless the patient has kidney disease

Lifestyle change is prescribed for EVERY stage, not just mild hypertension.

cards.title: Hypertension: the silent organ damage
body: Called the silent killer because it usually has NO symptoms
Brain: stroke, transient ischaemic attack, vascular dementia
Heart: left ventricular hypertrophy, heart failure, coronary disease
Kidney: nephrosclerosis, chronic kidney disease, proteinuria
Eye: hypertensive retinopathy, seen on fundoscopy
Vessels: aortic aneurysm and dissection
Headache and epistaxis are LATE and unreliable signs, never rely on them
cards.clinicalNote: Patients stop their antihypertensives because they feel perfectly well. That is the whole problem: they feel well right up until the stroke. Adherence teaching is the intervention, and it matters more than the choice of drug.

cards.title: Self-check: the ACE cough
cards.body:
A patient on ramipril complains of a dry, tickly cough that will not settle. What is happening and what is done?

Answer: IT IS AN ACE INHIBITOR SIDE EFFECT, from bradykinin build up. It is not an infection. The usual step is switching to an ARB such as losartan, which controls BP without causing the cough.

cards.title: Stroke: ischaemic versus haemorrhagic
cards.body:
ISCHAEMIC (about 85%): a clot blocks a vessel. Onset over minutes. May be preceded by TIAs.

HAEMORRHAGIC (about 15%): a vessel bursts. Sudden, severe headache often described as the worst of the patient's life, vomiting, rapid drop in consciousness, neck stiffness.

WHY IT MATTERS: the treatments are OPPOSITE. Thrombolysis saves an ischaemic stroke and kills a haemorrhagic one.

You CANNOT tell them apart at the bedside with certainty. An urgent non contrast CT head is mandatory BEFORE any clot busting drug.
cards.clinicalNote: Never give aspirin to a stroke patient on the ward because it seems sensible. Until the CT has excluded a bleed, an antiplatelet could be the thing that kills them. Scan first, always.

cards.title: Stroke: BE FAST
cards.body:
B: Balance, sudden loss of balance or coordination
E: Eyes, sudden loss or blurring of vision
F: Face, ask them to smile, look for one sided droop
A: Arms, ask them to raise both, look for one drifting down
S: Speech, slurred, jumbled, or cannot speak
T: Time, note the time of onset and call for help NOW

TIME OF ONSET is the single most important thing you can document. It decides whether thrombolysis is even possible.

cards.title: Stroke: the thrombolysis window and its rules
body: IV thrombolysis (alteplase) window: within 4.5 hours of symptom ONSET
Mechanical thrombectomy can extend the window in selected large vessel strokes
A non contrast CT must exclude haemorrhage FIRST
If the patient woke with symptoms, onset is when they were LAST SEEN WELL
Contraindications: recent surgery, active bleeding, BP above 185/110, recent stroke
After thrombolysis: strict neuro observations, no invasive lines, watch for bleeding
cards.clinicalNote: A patient who woke up with weakness did not have their stroke at breakfast. The clock starts when they were last seen normal, which may have been at bedtime. Getting that history right decides whether they are offered a clot busting drug at all.

cards.title: Stroke: which side, which deficit
cards.body:
The brain controls the OPPOSITE side of the body, so a LEFT brain stroke weakens the RIGHT side.

LEFT brain stroke (right sided weakness):
• APHASIA, the language centre is usually on the left
• Slow, cautious, anxious behaviour
• Difficulty with reading, writing, calculation

RIGHT brain stroke (left sided weakness):
• Speech usually intact, but NEGLECT of the left side
• Impulsive, poor judgement, unaware of their deficit
• They may not eat the left half of the plate, or shave only half their face

The right brain patient is the greater falls risk, because they do not believe anything is wrong.

cards.title: Stroke: preventing the complications that kill
body: SWALLOW SCREEN before ANY food, drink or oral medicine. Aspiration pneumonia is a leading cause of death
Nil by mouth until a trained assessor clears them
Position with the head of the bed at 30 degrees, unless told otherwise
Turn two hourly, protect the affected limb, support the shoulder
Early mobilisation and physiotherapy to prevent contractures and DVT
Approach a neglect patient from their UNAFFECTED side at first, then teach scanning
cards.clinicalNote: The most dangerous thing on a stroke ward is a well meaning relative with a cup of tea. A stroke patient who cannot swallow will not cough to warn you, they will silently aspirate. The swallow screen is not a formality.

cards.title: Self-check: aphasia or dysarthria
cards.body:
Your patient understands you perfectly and knows what they want to say, but the words come out slurred and thick. Aphasia or dysarthria?

Answer: DYSARTHRIA. It is a motor problem with the muscles of speech. Aphasia is a LANGUAGE problem: the patient cannot find, form or comprehend the words themselves. Dysarthric patients can usually write their answer.

cards.title: Asthma versus COPD: the distinguishers
cards.body:
ASTHMA: reversible airway obstruction. Younger, often atopic (eczema, allergic rhinitis). Symptoms are episodic and worse at night or early morning. Triggers: allergens, cold air, exercise, infection.

COPD: largely IRREVERSIBLE obstruction. Older, almost always a smoking history. Symptoms are persistent and progressive, with a chronic productive cough.

The test: spirometry with a bronchodilator. If FEV1 improves substantially after the bronchodilator, it is asthma. If it barely moves, it is COPD.

FEV1/FVC below 0.70 after a bronchodilator confirms COPD.
cards.clinicalNote: A quiet chest in a severe asthma attack is not improvement. It means so little air is moving that there is no longer enough flow to make a wheeze. A silent chest is a pre arrest sign, and it needs help immediately.

cards.title: Acute severe asthma: the danger signs
body: Unable to complete a sentence in one breath
Peak flow below 50% of their personal best
Respiratory rate above 25, heart rate above 110
SILENT CHEST, cyanosis, feeble respiratory effort. This is LIFE THREATENING
Exhaustion, confusion or drowsiness means imminent arrest
A RISING CO2 in an asthmatic is an emergency, they should be blowing it off
cards.clinicalNote: An asthmatic in a severe attack hyperventilates, so their CO2 should be LOW. A normal CO2 is worrying. A HIGH CO2 means they are tiring and about to stop breathing. Never take comfort from a normal blood gas in a struggling asthmatic.

cards.title: Asthma attack: O SHIT
cards.body:
The stepwise management of an acute attack:

O: Oxygen, high flow, target SpO2 94 to 98%
S: Salbutamol, nebulised, back to back if needed
H: Hydrocortisone IV, or oral prednisolone
I: Ipratropium bromide, nebulised, added in severe attacks
T: Theophylline or IV magnesium sulphate, in life threatening cases, on senior advice

Sit the patient UPRIGHT. Never lie an acute asthmatic flat.

cards.title: COPD: the oxygen rule that matters
cards.body:
In most patients we target an SpO2 of 94 to 98%.

In a patient with COPD who RETAINS CO2, the target is 88 to 92%, using CONTROLLED oxygen (a Venturi mask).

Why: in chronic CO2 retainers, the drive to breathe has shifted to depend partly on a low oxygen level (the hypoxic drive). Flooding them with oxygen can blunt that drive and worsen CO2 retention, causing drowsiness and narcosis.

BUT: never withhold oxygen from a hypoxic, dying patient out of fear. Treat the hypoxia, give controlled oxygen, and monitor the blood gases and the level of consciousness.
cards.clinicalNote: A COPD patient who becomes drowsy and flushed on high flow oxygen is not settling comfortably. They are retaining CO2. Check the gases and the conscious level, and step the oxygen down to a controlled device.

cards.title: COPD: living with it, the teaching that helps
body: PURSED LIP BREATHING: breathe in through the nose, out slowly through pursed lips. It stops the small airways collapsing
Tripod position: sitting, leaning forward on the elbows, helps the accessory muscles
Smoking cessation is the ONLY intervention that slows the decline
Pneumococcal and annual influenza vaccination
Pulmonary rehabilitation improves exercise tolerance and quality of life
Eat small frequent high calorie meals: a full stomach splints the diaphragm
Report a change in sputum colour or volume early, it signals infection

cards.title: Self-check: the COPD saturation target
cards.body:
A known CO2 retaining COPD patient has an SpO2 of 90% on room air. The new nurse wants to put them on 60% oxygen. Right or wrong?

Answer: WRONG. Their target is 88 to 92%, and 90% is already within it. High flow oxygen risks blunting the hypoxic drive and worsening CO2 retention. Use controlled oxygen via a Venturi mask only if they fall below target.

cards.title: Pneumonia: the picture and the priorities
cards.body:
Presentation: fever with rigors, productive cough (rusty sputum in classic pneumococcal pneumonia), pleuritic chest pain, breathlessness.

Signs: bronchial breathing, crackles, dullness to percussion over the affected lobe, reduced chest expansion on that side.

CURB-65 predicts severity, one point each:
C: Confusion
U: Urea above 7 mmol/L
R: Respiratory rate 30 or more
B: BP, systolic below 90 or diastolic 60 or less
65: Age 65 or over

Score 0 to 1 can often be treated at home. Score 3 or more means severe pneumonia and likely intensive care review.
cards.clinicalNote: In the elderly, pneumonia often arrives without a fever and without a cough. Sudden confusion or a fall may be the ONLY sign. If an older patient is suddenly confused, examine the chest and check their oxygen saturation.

cards.title: Pneumonia: nursing care that changes outcomes
body: Antibiotics within 4 hours of diagnosis, after cultures where possible
Sit the patient upright to improve lung expansion
Encourage deep breathing and coughing, and use of an incentive spirometer
Adequate hydration keeps secretions loose enough to expectorate
Chest physiotherapy and early mobilisation
Monitor SpO2, respiratory rate and temperature. Rising RR is the earliest warning
Pneumococcal and influenza vaccination prevents the next one

cards.title: Tuberculosis: how it presents and spreads
cards.body:
Spread by AIRBORNE droplet nuclei, which stay suspended in air for hours. Caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, an acid fast bacillus.

Classic symptoms:
• Cough for more than 2 weeks (the case definition for screening)
• Evening rise of temperature
• Drenching NIGHT SWEATS
• Unexplained weight loss and loss of appetite
• Haemoptysis in advanced disease

Diagnosis: sputum for acid fast bacilli, CBNAAT/GeneXpert (which also detects rifampicin resistance), chest X ray, Mantoux test.

Any cough lasting over 2 weeks in India is presumed TB until proven otherwise.
cards.clinicalNote: TB is airborne, not droplet. A surgical mask on the nurse is not enough for an infectious pulmonary case. You need an N95 respirator, and the patient needs a negative pressure room where one exists.

cards.title: TB drugs: RIPE, and what each one damages
cards.body:
R: Rifampicin → turns urine, tears and sweat ORANGE/RED (harmless, but warn them or they stop the drug). Hepatotoxic. Reduces the effect of oral contraceptives.
I: Isoniazid → PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY. Prevented by giving pyridoxine (vitamin B6). Hepatotoxic.
P: Pyrazinamide → raises URIC ACID, can cause gout. Hepatotoxic.
E: Ethambutol → EYES. Optic neuritis, loss of red-green colour vision. Test vision before and during treatment.

Three of the four are hepatotoxic. Monitor liver function and warn the patient about jaundice, nausea and dark urine.

cards.title: TB: infection control and treatment adherence
body: AIRBORNE precautions: N95 respirator for staff, negative pressure room, door closed
The patient wears a SURGICAL mask when leaving the room, staff wear the N95
Teach cough etiquette and safe sputum disposal
Treatment is directly observed (DOT) under the national programme
Never stop treatment early: incomplete courses breed drug resistant TB
Screen and treat household contacts
Report jaundice, dark urine or persistent vomiting at once. It may be drug hepatitis
cards.clinicalNote: Patients feel better after about two weeks and want to stop. That is exactly how multi drug resistant TB is created. Adherence counselling is not paperwork, it is the treatment.

cards.title: Self-check: orange urine on TB treatment
cards.body:
A patient starting TB treatment is frightened because their urine has turned orange. What do you tell them?

Answer: IT IS RIFAMPICIN, AND IT IS HARMLESS. It colours urine, sweat and tears orange or red, and it will stain soft contact lenses permanently. Reassure them and do NOT stop the drug. Warning them in advance prevents them abandoning treatment in panic.

cards.title: Acute kidney injury: pre, intra and post
cards.body:
PRE-RENAL (the commonest): the kidney is fine, but not enough blood reaches it. Hypovolaemia, shock, heart failure, dehydration. REVERSIBLE if perfusion is restored quickly.

INTRA-RENAL (intrinsic): the kidney tissue itself is damaged. Acute tubular necrosis, nephrotoxic drugs, glomerulonephritis, contrast media.

POST-RENAL: an obstruction below the kidney. Enlarged prostate, stones, tumour, blocked catheter. Relieve the obstruction and function often returns.

Always CHECK THE CATHETER before you diagnose kidney failure. A blocked catheter is a post renal AKI you can fix in thirty seconds.
cards.clinicalNote: A patient whose urine output falls to nothing is not always in kidney failure. Flush or replace the catheter first. It is the cheapest, fastest and most commonly missed cause of anuria on any ward.

cards.title: AKI: the numbers and the nursing watch
body: Oliguria: urine output below 0.5 mL/kg/hr, or under 400 mL a day
Anuria: under 100 mL a day
Rising creatinine and urea, falling eGFR
HYPERKALAEMIA is the immediate killer. It causes fatal arrhythmias
Watch for metabolic acidosis and fluid overload (crackles, oedema, breathlessness)
Strict input and output charting, daily weights
Review and stop nephrotoxic drugs: NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, contrast

cards.title: Hyperkalaemia: C BIG K
cards.body:
The emergency management of a dangerously high potassium:

C: Calcium gluconate → protects the HEART. It does not lower the potassium, it stabilises the cardiac membrane. Give it FIRST
B: Beta agonist (nebulised salbutamol) → drives K+ into cells
I: Insulin with dextrose → drives K+ into cells
G: Give the dextrose with the insulin, or you cause hypoglycaemia
K: Kayexalate (a binder) and dialysis → actually REMOVE potassium from the body

Everything except the binder and dialysis merely HIDES the potassium inside cells. It comes back out.

cards.title: CKD: what fails when the kidney fails
cards.body:
The kidney does more than make urine, and CKD shows it:

• ANAEMIA: no erythropoietin. Treated with EPO and iron
• BONE DISEASE: no activated vitamin D → low calcium, high phosphate → the parathyroid overworks (renal osteodystrophy)
• ACIDOSIS: cannot excrete acid
• HYPERTENSION and fluid overload: cannot excrete salt and water
• HYPERKALAEMIA: cannot excrete potassium
• URAEMIA: itching, nausea, confusion, a metallic taste, pericarditis

Diet: restrict protein, potassium, phosphate, sodium and fluid. Phosphate binders are taken WITH meals so they bind the phosphate in the food.
cards.clinicalNote: Phosphate binders given between meals are wasted. They work by binding phosphate in the food itself, so they must be swallowed with the first mouthful. Patients who take them an hour later cannot understand why their phosphate stays high.

cards.title: Dialysis: the nursing essentials
body: NEVER take blood pressure, take blood, or put a cannula in the arm with the AV FISTULA
Check the fistula for a THRILL (feel it) and a BRUISE (hear it) every shift
Weigh the patient before and after every dialysis session. It measures the fluid removed
Watch for disequilibrium syndrome after the first sessions: headache, nausea, confusion
In peritoneal dialysis, CLOUDY outflow means PERITONITIS until proved otherwise
Hold antihypertensives before dialysis if instructed, hypotension is common during it
cards.clinicalNote: Put a blood pressure cuff on a fistula arm and you can clot the fistula. That is months of surgery and access lost. Some wards mark the arm with a wristband, and it is worth doing.

cards.title: Self-check: calcium first, and why
cards.body:
In severe hyperkalaemia with ECG changes, why is IV calcium gluconate given first when it does not lower the potassium at all?

Answer: IT PROTECTS THE HEART. Calcium stabilises the cardiac cell membrane and buys time against fatal arrhythmia while insulin, dextrose and salbutamol shift the potassium and dialysis removes it. It treats the danger, not the number.

cards.title: Cirrhosis: why the complications happen
cards.body:
A scarred liver causes trouble in two ways.

PORTAL HYPERTENSION (blood cannot get through the liver):
• Oesophageal VARICES, which bleed catastrophically
• Ascites
• Splenomegaly
• Caput medusae (dilated abdominal wall veins)

LOSS OF LIVER FUNCTION (the cells no longer work):
• Low albumin → oedema and ascites
• Clotting factors not made → bruising and bleeding, prolonged INR
• Ammonia not detoxified → HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY
• Bilirubin not cleared → jaundice and itching
• Oestrogen not cleared → gynaecomastia, spider naevi, palmar erythema
cards.clinicalNote: The cirrhotic patient who becomes confused has not simply become difficult. Rising ammonia causes encephalopathy, and the first signs are subtle: a change in sleep pattern, mild confusion, then a flapping tremor of the outstretched hands.

cards.title: Hepatic encephalopathy: think ammonia
cards.body:
The four grades run from subtle to fatal:

Grade 1: altered sleep pattern, mild confusion, short attention span
Grade 2: lethargy, disorientation, ASTERIXIS (the flapping tremor)
Grade 3: marked confusion, incoherent, rousable but sleepy
Grade 4: COMA

Treatment lowers ammonia:
LACTULOSE, which traps ammonia in the gut and purges it. Aim for 2 to 3 soft stools a day. If the patient is not passing stool, the drug is not working.
RIFAXIMIN, which kills the ammonia producing gut bacteria.
Restrict protein only in acute severe episodes, not long term.

cards.title: Ruptured oesophageal varices: what to do
body: This is a MASSIVE, life threatening haemorrhage. Call for help immediately
Two large bore cannulas, group and cross match urgently
Resuscitate with fluid and blood. Correct the clotting with vitamin K and plasma
Terlipressin or octreotide to reduce portal pressure, as prescribed
Urgent endoscopy for band ligation, the definitive treatment
Sengstaken-Blakemore balloon tamponade is the temporary rescue when bleeding is uncontrollable
Protect the airway, the risk of aspirating blood is high
cards.clinicalNote: A cirrhotic patient vomiting bright red blood is an emergency in a different league from an ulcer bleed. They are bleeding from a high pressure vein and they cannot clot. Everything must happen at once, and the airway is the first thing you lose.

cards.title: Upper GI bleed: reading the blood
cards.body:
HAEMATEMESIS: vomiting blood.
• Bright red = active, brisk bleeding
• COFFEE GROUND = blood altered by stomach acid, so it has been sitting there

MELAENA: black, tarry, offensively sticky stool. It means blood has been digested, so the bleed is UPPER GI (above the ligament of Treitz). It takes about 50 mL of blood to produce it.

FRESH RED BLOOD PER RECTUM usually means a lower GI bleed, but a very brisk upper bleed can also present this way.

A rising urea with a normal creatinine is a clue: the blood is being digested and absorbed as protein.
cards.clinicalNote: Melaena is unmistakable once you have smelled it, and it is often the first hard evidence that a patient with vague dizziness is actually bleeding internally. Do not dismiss black stool as iron tablets without looking at it.

cards.title: Peptic ulcer: causes, cure and the emergency
body: The two big causes are HELICOBACTER PYLORI and NSAIDs. Not stress or spicy food
Duodenal ulcer pain is RELIEVED by food, and wakes the patient at night
Gastric ulcer pain is WORSENED by food, so the patient loses weight
H. pylori is cured by triple therapy: a PPI plus two antibiotics (clarithromycin, amoxicillin)
PERFORATION: sudden severe pain, board like rigid abdomen, absent bowel sounds. It is a surgical emergency
Air under the diaphragm on an erect chest X ray confirms perforation

cards.title: Self-check: lactulose and the stool count
cards.body:
A confused cirrhotic patient is on lactulose but has not opened their bowels in two days. Is the drug working?

Answer: NO. Lactulose treats encephalopathy by trapping ammonia in the gut and expelling it in stool. The target is 2 to 3 soft stools a day. No stool means the ammonia is not leaving, and the dose needs review.

cards.title: Burns: depth and what it means
cards.body:
SUPERFICIAL (first degree): epidermis only. Red, dry, PAINFUL, blanches. Like sunburn. Heals in days, no scar.

PARTIAL THICKNESS (second degree): into the dermis. BLISTERS, moist, red, VERY PAINFUL. Heals in 2 to 3 weeks, may scar.

FULL THICKNESS (third degree): through the whole dermis. White, leathery, waxy or charred. DRY. PAINLESS, because the nerve endings are destroyed.

The cruel paradox: the WORST burns hurt the LEAST. A painless, leathery burn is not a mild burn, it is a full thickness one and it will need grafting.
cards.clinicalNote: Do not be reassured by a patient who says a burn does not hurt. In burns, absence of pain means the nerve endings have been destroyed. The painful blistered area beside it is actually the less severe injury.

cards.title: Burns: the Rule of Nines
cards.body:
Adult body surface area, each region is 9% or a multiple:

Head and neck: 9%
Each arm: 9% (so both arms = 18%)
Front of trunk: 18%
Back of trunk: 18%
Each leg: 18% (so both legs = 36%)
Perineum: 1%

Total 100%.

In a CHILD the head is proportionally much bigger (about 18%) and the legs smaller (about 14% each), because a child's head is a larger share of their body.

Quick estimate for scattered burns: the patient's own PALM (including fingers) is about 1% of their body surface.

cards.title: Burns: fluid resuscitation and the Parkland formula
body: PARKLAND: 4 mL × body weight in kg × % total burn surface area, of Ringer's lactate, in 24 hours
Give HALF in the first 8 HOURS, counted from the TIME OF THE BURN, not from admission
Give the remaining half over the next 16 hours
Example: 70 kg with 40% burns → 4 × 70 × 40 = 11200 mL. 5600 mL in the first 8 hours
The formula is a STARTING POINT. Titrate to urine output: aim 0.5–1 mL/kg/hr in an adult
Only partial and full thickness burns are counted. Do NOT count superficial redness
cards.clinicalNote: The eight hour clock starts when the patient was burned, not when they reached you. If they arrive three hours later, the first half of that fluid must go in over the remaining five hours, not eight. Getting this wrong under resuscitates them.

cards.title: Burns: the airway is the first thing that kills
cards.body:
Suspect INHALATION INJURY if there are:
• Burns to the face, singed nasal hair or eyebrows
• Soot in the mouth, nose or sputum
• Hoarse voice, stridor, a brassy cough
• A history of being trapped in an enclosed, smoke filled space

The airway can swell shut over hours. Intubate EARLY, while it is still possible. Once the swelling is established, intubation may be impossible and a surgical airway is needed.

Suspect CARBON MONOXIDE poisoning in any enclosed space fire. The pulse oximeter reads FALSELY NORMAL, because it cannot tell carboxyhaemoglobin from oxyhaemoglobin. Give 100% oxygen and measure carboxyhaemoglobin directly.
cards.clinicalNote: A cherry red patient with a perfect SpO2 of 100% who was pulled from a house fire is not fine. The oximeter is lying to you: it counts carbon monoxide as though it were oxygen. Trust the history, give high flow oxygen.

cards.title: Burns: immediate first aid and ward care
body: Stop the burning: remove the source, cool with running COOL (not ice cold) water for 20 minutes
NEVER apply ice, it deepens the injury by causing vasoconstriction
NEVER apply toothpaste, butter, oil or ash. They trap heat and cause infection
Remove rings, bangles and watches BEFORE the swelling starts
Cover with a clean, non adherent dressing or clingfilm. Keep the patient warm
Tetanus prophylaxis. Strict aseptic technique: INFECTION is the leading cause of later death
Do not burst blisters routinely. High protein, high calorie diet for healing

cards.title: Self-check: burns fluid calculation
cards.body:
A 60 kg adult has 30% partial thickness burns. Using Parkland, how much fluid in the FIRST 8 hours?

Answer: 3600 mL. Parkland gives 4 × 60 × 30 = 7200 mL over 24 hours, and HALF of that goes in the first 8 hours from the time of the burn. So 3600 mL, then 3600 mL over the following 16 hours.

cards.title: Sodium: the water problem in disguise
cards.body:
Sodium disorders are usually WATER problems, not salt problems.

HYPONATRAEMIA (below 135 mEq/L): too much water for the salt.
• Causes: SIADH, heart failure, cirrhosis, over infusion of hypotonic fluid, diuretics
• Signs: headache, nausea, confusion, muscle cramps, SEIZURES when severe
• The brain SWELLS. This is why the symptoms are neurological
• CORRECT IT SLOWLY. Too fast causes central pontine myelinolysis, a permanent brain injury

HYPERNATRAEMIA (above 145 mEq/L): not enough water for the salt.
• Causes: dehydration, diabetes insipidus, inability to access water
• Signs: intense THIRST, dry mucosa, restlessness, lethargy, seizures
• The brain SHRINKS.
• Correct slowly too. Too fast causes cerebral oedema
cards.clinicalNote: Both directions of sodium derangement present as confusion, and both are made permanently worse by correcting them too quickly. The rule in an exam and on the ward is the same: slow, controlled correction with frequent monitoring.

cards.title: Potassium: the two ends of the same danger
cards.body:
Both a high and a low potassium can stop the heart.

HYPOkalaemia (below 3.5), think "everything is SLOW and FLAT":
• Muscle weakness, cramps, reduced reflexes
• Constipation, ileus
• ECG: FLATTENED T wave, prominent U WAVE, ST depression
• Causes: diuretics, vomiting, diarrhoea, insulin

HYPERkalaemia (above 5.5), think "TIGHT and PEAKED":
• Muscle weakness, then paralysis
• ECG: TALL PEAKED T waves, wide QRS, flattened P wave, then sine wave and arrest
• Causes: kidney failure, ACE inhibitors, potassium sparing diuretics, crush injury, acidosis

NEVER give potassium as an IV push. It is always DILUTED and INFUSED slowly, on a pump.

cards.title: Calcium and magnesium: the twitchy pair
body: HYPOcalcaemia: TETANY. Numbness around the mouth, carpopedal spasm, twitching
CHVOSTEK sign: tap the facial nerve, the cheek twitches
TROUSSEAU sign: inflate a BP cuff, the hand goes into carpal spasm
Laryngospasm and seizures in severe cases. Keep calcium gluconate available
HYPERcalcaemia: 'stones, bones, abdominal groans and psychic moans'. Weakness, constipation, confusion
HYPOmagnesaemia mimics hypocalcaemia and causes refractory hypokalaemia
You CANNOT correct a low potassium until you correct a low magnesium
cards.clinicalNote: A potassium that stubbornly refuses to rise despite repeated replacement is usually a magnesium problem. Check the magnesium. Until it is corrected, the kidney simply throws the potassium straight back out.

cards.title: IV fluids: which bag and why
cards.body:
ISOTONIC (stays in the vessels, expands circulating volume):
• Normal saline 0.9%: resuscitation, shock. Large volumes cause hyperchloraemic acidosis
• Ringer's lactate: closer to plasma, the fluid of choice in burns and trauma

HYPOTONIC (moves water INTO the cells):
• 0.45% saline, 5% dextrose (which is isotonic in the bag but effectively hypotonic once the glucose is used)
• Used for cellular dehydration. DANGEROUS in head injury: it worsens cerebral oedema

HYPERTONIC (pulls water OUT of the cells, into the vessels):
• 3% saline, 20% mannitol
• Used for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia and raised intracranial pressure
• Given slowly, in a monitored setting, watch for fluid overload
cards.clinicalNote: Never hang plain 5% dextrose in a head injured patient because it is 'just sugar water'. Once the glucose is metabolised, free water is left behind, it moves into the brain, and the intracranial pressure rises.

cards.title: Fluid overload versus dehydration: the bedside signs
body: OVERLOAD: raised JVP, crackles at the lung bases, pitting oedema, breathlessness
OVERLOAD: bounding pulse, rising BP, rapid WEIGHT GAIN, frothy sputum
DEHYDRATION: dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor, sunken eyes
DEHYDRATION: tachycardia, postural drop in BP, concentrated urine, low output
DEHYDRATION: prolonged capillary refill, weak thready pulse, thirst
WEIGHT is the most sensitive measure of fluid change. 1 kg is roughly 1 litre
Urine output below 0.5 mL/kg/hr means the kidney is not being perfused

cards.title: Self-check: never push potassium
cards.body:
A doctor asks you to give 20 mEq of potassium chloride as an IV bolus to a hypokalaemic patient. What do you do?

Answer: REFUSE AND CLARIFY. IV potassium is NEVER given as a push or bolus. It must be diluted and infused slowly via a pump, with cardiac monitoring. An undiluted potassium push causes cardiac arrest, and it is a classic fatal medication error.

cards.title: Cancer warning signs: CAUTION
cards.body:
The seven classic warning signs:

C: Change in bowel or bladder habit
A: A sore that does not heal
U: Unusual bleeding or discharge
T: Thickening or a lump, in the breast or anywhere
I: Indigestion or difficulty swallowing
O: Obvious change in a wart or mole
N: Nagging cough or hoarseness

Any one of these, persisting, deserves investigation, not reassurance.

cards.title: Chemotherapy: the side effects that matter
body: MYELOSUPPRESSION is the most dangerous: low white cells, red cells and platelets
NEUTROPENIA: the patient cannot mount a fever safely. Infection is the leading killer
THROMBOCYTOPENIA: bleeding risk. Avoid IM injections, razors, hard toothbrushes, aspirin
ANAEMIA: fatigue and breathlessness
Nausea and vomiting: give antiemetics BEFORE the chemotherapy, not after
Alopecia: distressing but temporary. Prepare the patient in advance
Mucositis: soft diet, saline mouth rinses, avoid alcohol based mouthwash
EXTRAVASATION of a vesicant drug can destroy tissue. Stop the infusion immediately

cards.title: Neutropenic sepsis: the true emergency
cards.body:
This is the oncology emergency that kills fastest, and a nurse usually spots it first.

Definition: a NEUTROPHIL count below 0.5 × 10⁹/L (or below 1.0 and falling) WITH a temperature of 38 °C or above, OR any sign of sepsis.

Why it is deadly: with no neutrophils, the patient cannot produce pus, cannot localise infection and may not even mount much of a fever. They can deteriorate from mildly unwell to dead within hours.

ACTION: BROAD SPECTRUM IV ANTIBIOTICS WITHIN ONE HOUR. Do not wait for the culture results. Take cultures, then treat.

Any fever in a chemotherapy patient is neutropenic sepsis until proven otherwise.
cards.clinicalNote: A chemotherapy patient who rings the bell saying they feel shivery and unwell, with a temperature of 38.1, is not a routine review. That is the golden hour. Antibiotics within sixty minutes save their life, and every minute of delay measurably raises their risk of dying.

cards.title: Protecting the neutropenic patient
body: Strict HAND HYGIENE. It is the single most effective protection
Single room, protective isolation where available
NO raw or uncooked food, no unpeeled fruit, no salads, no soft cheese
NO fresh flowers or potted plants in the room, they carry fungal spores
Avoid crowds and anyone with an infection, including staff
NO rectal thermometers, suppositories or enemas. They breach the mucosa
Avoid IM injections and invasive procedures where possible
Report ANY temperature at once, however well the patient looks

cards.title: Cancer treatment: what each modality does
cards.body:
SURGERY: removes the bulk of a localised tumour. Curative when the disease has not spread.

CHEMOTHERAPY: systemic. Kills rapidly dividing cells, which is why it also hits bone marrow, hair follicles and gut lining. That is the source of every classic side effect.

RADIOTHERAPY: local. Damages DNA in the treatment field. Skin care in the field is a nursing priority: wash gently with plain water, pat dry, no talc, no perfumed products, no heat or ice, protect from sunlight. Do NOT wash off the marking ink.

PALLIATIVE CARE: neither hastens nor postpones death. It treats symptoms and supports quality of life, and it can run alongside active treatment. It is NOT only for the last days.
cards.clinicalNote: Patients often hear the word palliative and believe treatment has been abandoned. Explaining that palliative care is about controlling symptoms and living as well as possible, and that it can run alongside chemotherapy, changes how they engage with it.

cards.title: Self-check: the one hour rule
cards.body:
A patient 10 days after chemotherapy has a temperature of 38.2 °C and looks well. Neutrophils are 0.3. What is the priority?

Answer: BROAD SPECTRUM IV ANTIBIOTICS WITHIN ONE HOUR. This is neutropenic sepsis. Looking well is meaningless: without neutrophils they cannot mount a normal inflammatory response, and they can crash within hours. Take cultures, but do not delay the antibiotic waiting for them.

cards.title: Pre-operative checklist: what must be done
body: INFORMED CONSENT signed. The SURGEON explains the procedure, the nurse witnesses the signature
Nil by mouth: usually 6 hours for solid food, 2 hours for clear fluids
Confirm the site is MARKED, and complete the surgical safety checklist
Remove jewellery, dentures, prostheses, nail polish and contact lenses
Baseline observations, weight, allergies and blood investigations
Ask about anticoagulants, diabetes medication, steroids and herbal remedies
Empty the bladder immediately before transfer
Teach deep breathing, coughing and leg exercises BEFORE surgery, not after
cards.clinicalNote: Teaching a patient to splint their wound and cough AFTER surgery is teaching them while they are in pain and sedated. Do it the day before, when they can actually take it in. That is what prevents the post op chest infection.

cards.title: Post-op fever: the 5 Ws and their timing
cards.body:
The day of the fever tells you the cause:

WIND (atelectasis, pneumonia): day 1 to 2. The commonest early cause
WATER (urinary tract infection): day 3 to 5, often catheter related
WOUND (wound infection): day 5 to 7
WALKING (deep vein thrombosis): day 5 onwards. Immobility is the enemy
WONDER DRUGS (drug reaction, transfusion reaction): any time

A fever on day one is nearly always the LUNGS. Sit them up, get them breathing deeply, get them moving.

cards.title: Post-op complications: what to watch and when
cards.body:
IMMEDIATE (first hours):
• Airway obstruction, hypoxia, aspiration
• Haemorrhage: check the drains and dressing, watch for tachycardia and a falling BP
• Hypotension, arrhythmia

EARLY (days):
• Atelectasis and chest infection
• Paralytic ileus: silent abdomen, distension, no flatus
• Urinary retention: the patient wants to pass urine and cannot. Palpable bladder
• Wound infection, wound DEHISCENCE (the wound bursts open)

LATE:
• DVT and pulmonary embolism
• Adhesions and incisional hernia

WOUND EVISCERATION is an emergency: cover the protruding bowel with a STERILE SALINE SOAKED dressing, do not push it back, position the patient with knees bent, keep nil by mouth and call the surgeon.
cards.clinicalNote: A patient who says they felt something 'give' when they coughed, and whose dressing is suddenly soaked with pink serous fluid, is telling you their wound is about to burst. That serous leak is the warning sign that comes before evisceration.

cards.title: Preventing DVT after surgery
body: EARLY MOBILISATION is the single best prevention. Get them up
Anti embolism (TED) stockings, correctly sized and correctly fitted
Intermittent pneumatic compression devices
Prophylactic low molecular weight heparin as prescribed
Leg exercises hourly while in bed: ankle pumps, foot circles
Adequate hydration. Dehydrated blood is thicker blood
Signs of DVT: unilateral calf pain, swelling, warmth, redness
NEVER massage a suspected DVT. You can dislodge the clot into the lungs

cards.title: Pulmonary embolism: sudden and lethal
cards.body:
A clot, usually from a leg vein, lodges in the pulmonary circulation.

CLASSIC PRESENTATION: SUDDEN breathlessness, pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, tachypnoea, anxiety and a feeling of impending doom. Haemoptysis and cyanosis in larger emboli. Sudden collapse in a massive PE.

Think of it in any post operative or immobile patient who becomes suddenly and unexpectedly short of breath.

IMMEDIATE ACTION:
• Sit the patient upright, high flow oxygen
• Call for urgent help, do not leave the patient
• IV access, continuous monitoring
• Anticoagulation as prescribed, thrombolysis in massive PE

The defining feature is SUDDENNESS. Pneumonia builds over days, a PE happens in a moment.
cards.clinicalNote: The post op patient who suddenly sits bolt upright, breathless, tachycardic and frightened, with a clear chest, is a pulmonary embolism until proved otherwise. That anxiety and sense of doom is a real clinical sign, not just distress.

cards.title: Self-check: day one fever
cards.body:
A patient spikes a fever 24 hours after abdominal surgery. What is the most likely cause and the first nursing action?

Answer: ATELECTASIS, the 'Wind' of the 5 Ws. Day 1 to 2 fever is nearly always the lungs collapsing from shallow, painful breathing. Sit them up, give adequate analgesia so they CAN breathe deeply, and start deep breathing, coughing and incentive spirometry.

cards.title: Pressure ulcers: the four stages
cards.body:
STAGE 1: intact skin with NON BLANCHING redness. Press it, and it does not turn white. This is the warning, and it is reversible.

STAGE 2: partial thickness loss. A shallow open ulcer, or an intact/ruptured blister. Looks like an abrasion.

STAGE 3: FULL thickness loss. Subcutaneous FAT is visible. Bone, tendon and muscle are NOT exposed. May have undermining and tunnelling.

STAGE 4: full thickness loss with EXPOSED BONE, TENDON OR MUSCLE. Often has slough and eschar.

UNSTAGEABLE: the base is covered in slough or eschar, so the depth cannot be seen. You cannot stage what you cannot see.

DEEP TISSUE INJURY: persistent deep red, maroon or purple discolouration of intact skin. The damage is underneath.
cards.clinicalNote: The blanching test costs one second. Press the red area: if it whitens and refills, the circulation is intact. If it stays red, the tissue is already damaged. That is a stage 1 ulcer, and it is the last point at which you can still prevent everything that follows.

cards.title: Pressure ulcers: prevention that actually works
body: REPOSITION at least 2 hourly in bed, hourly if sitting in a chair
Use a risk assessment tool (Braden or Waterlow) on admission and regularly after
Pressure redistributing mattress and cushions for at risk patients
Keep skin clean and DRY. Moisture and incontinence macerate skin
NEVER MASSAGE a reddened bony prominence. It damages the tissue further
Use the 30 degree tilt, do not lie the patient directly on the hip bone
Avoid dragging the patient up the bed: SHEAR is as damaging as pressure
Nutrition: protein, vitamin C and zinc are needed to build tissue

cards.title: Pressure ulcer risk: SKIN bundle
cards.body:
S: Surface, is the patient on the right mattress or cushion?
K: Keep moving, reposition and encourage movement
I: Incontinence and moisture, keep the skin clean and dry
N: Nutrition and hydration, they cannot heal without protein and fluid

The commonest sites are the bony prominences:
Supine: SACRUM, heels, occiput, elbows
Side lying: greater trochanter (hip), ankles, ears, shoulder
Sitting: ischial tuberosities (the sitting bones)

The sacrum and the heels are where most ulcers happen. Look at them every single day.

cards.title: Wound healing: the phases and what blocks them
cards.body:
PHASES:
1. Haemostasis: bleeding stops, a clot forms. Minutes
2. Inflammation: redness, warmth, swelling, pain. Days 1 to 4. This is NORMAL, it is not automatically infection
3. Proliferation: granulation tissue (red, bumpy, healthy) fills the wound. Days 4 to 21
4. Maturation: collagen remodels and the scar strengthens. Up to a year or more

WHAT DELAYS HEALING:
• Poor nutrition, especially low protein and vitamin C
• Diabetes and poor glycaemic control
• Smoking, which constricts the vessels
• Steroids and chemotherapy
• Infection, poor blood supply, foreign bodies
• Age, obesity, repeated trauma to the site
cards.clinicalNote: Red, bumpy, slightly bleeding tissue in a wound bed is granulation tissue and it is exactly what you want to see. Do not scrub it away or treat it as infection. Yellow slough and black eschar are what needs to go.

cards.title: Wound infection: telling it apart from normal healing
body: Normal inflammation: mild redness and warmth around the edge, settling after day 4
INFECTION: increasing pain, spreading redness, heat, swelling AFTER day 4
PURULENT discharge, an offensive smell
The wound edges separate, granulation looks unhealthy and bleeds easily
Systemic signs: fever, raised white cell count, malaise
Take a wound swab BEFORE cleaning the wound, or you swab the antiseptic
Use aseptic technique. Clean from the CLEANEST area outwards to the dirtiest

cards.title: Self-check: do not massage the redness
cards.body:
You find a non blanching red patch over a patient's sacrum. A colleague starts massaging it to 'get the circulation going'. Correct?

Answer: NO, AND IT CAUSES HARM. Massaging a reddened bony prominence damages already compromised capillaries and deepens the injury. This is a stage 1 pressure ulcer: relieve the pressure, reposition, document, and reassess the mattress and risk score.

cards.title: Compartment syndrome: the 6 Ps
cards.body:
A limb threatening emergency. Pressure builds inside a fascial compartment and strangles the tissue.

P: PAIN, out of all proportion to the injury, and worse on PASSIVE STRETCH of the muscle. This is the EARLIEST and most reliable sign
P: Paraesthesia, pins and needles
P: Pallor
P: Paralysis
P: Pulselessness
P: Poikilothermia (the limb feels cold)

THE TRAP: the pulse is present until very LATE. A palpable pulse does NOT exclude compartment syndrome.

ACTION: report IMMEDIATELY, remove or split any cast or tight dressing, keep the limb at HEART level (not elevated, which reduces arterial inflow), prepare for emergency FASCIOTOMY.

cards.title: Fractures: the immediate nursing care
body: Immobilise the limb, splinting the joint ABOVE and BELOW the fracture
Assess NEUROVASCULAR status distal to the injury: colour, warmth, pulse, sensation, movement
Do this BEFORE and AFTER any splinting, casting or traction
Elevate the limb to reduce swelling, and apply ice in the first 24 to 48 hours
Give analgesia. Pain out of proportion is a red flag, not a request for more painkillers
An OPEN fracture needs the wound covered with a sterile dressing, tetanus cover and antibiotics
Never attempt to push a protruding bone end back under the skin
cards.clinicalNote: The single most important observation after any cast is applied is the neurovascular check. A cast that fitted perfectly at 2 pm can be strangling the limb by 6 pm as the swelling builds. Check, document, and act on what you find.

cards.title: Fat embolism: the fracture complication that looks like nothing else
cards.body:
Fat globules from the marrow of a LONG BONE fracture (especially femur and pelvis) enter the circulation and lodge in the lungs and brain.

Typically 24 to 72 HOURS after the injury, which is why it is missed.

THE CLASSIC TRIAD:
1. Sudden BREATHLESSNESS and hypoxia
2. Sudden CONFUSION or a fall in conscious level
3. A PETECHIAL RASH, characteristically across the chest, axillae and conjunctiva

The petechial rash is the giveaway and it appears in no other post fracture complication.

Management is supportive: oxygen, careful fluids, and early fixation of the fracture prevents it.
cards.clinicalNote: A young man with a femoral fracture who becomes confused and breathless on day two is not simply anxious or over medicated. Look at his chest and armpits for petechiae. Fat embolism is the diagnosis that gets missed because everyone is watching the leg.

cards.title: Traction and cast care
body: TRACTION WEIGHTS MUST HANG FREE. They never rest on the floor or the bed frame
Ropes must sit in the pulley grooves, and knots must not jam against a pulley
Maintain the line of pull. Do not lift the weights, even to reposition the patient
Skeletal traction pin sites: inspect daily for redness, discharge and loosening
A new cast is dry in 24 to 48 hours. Handle a wet plaster cast with the PALMS, not the fingertips, or you dent it
Never insert anything down a cast to scratch an itch
Report a hot spot, a foul smell, or a fresh stain on the cast, all suggest infection underneath

cards.title: Hip fracture and replacement: what must not happen
cards.body:
After a total HIP replacement the prosthesis can DISLOCATE, and the nursing care is entirely about preventing it.

THE THREE FORBIDDEN MOVEMENTS (posterior approach):
1. Do NOT flex the hip beyond 90 degrees. No low chairs, no low toilets, no bending to tie shoes
2. Do NOT cross the legs or the ankles. Use an ABDUCTION PILLOW between the legs
3. Do NOT internally rotate the hip. Keep the toes pointing up

SIGNS OF DISLOCATION: sudden severe pain, the leg looks SHORTENED and ROTATED, the patient cannot move it, there may be an audible pop.

Other priorities: DVT prophylaxis, early mobilisation, neurovascular checks, and preventing pressure damage on the heels.
cards.clinicalNote: The abduction pillow looks like an optional comfort aid to a patient and to relatives. It is not. It is the one thing physically preventing the new hip from dislocating, and it needs explaining, not just placing.

cards.title: Self-check: the pulse in compartment syndrome
cards.body:
A patient in a below knee cast has severe pain on passive stretching of the toes, but you can still feel a good pedal pulse. Can you rule out compartment syndrome?

Answer: NO. Pulselessness is a LATE sign. The pulse can remain palpable while the muscle inside the compartment is dying. Pain out of proportion, and pain on passive stretch, are the EARLIEST signs. Escalate now.

cards.title: The "LASA" Look-Alike Sound-Alike Drugs
cards.body:
Common dangerous confusions:
• Hydralazine vs Hydroxyzine
• Cefazolin vs Ceftazidime
• Furosemide vs Torsemide
• Insulin Humalog vs Humulin
• Digoxin vs Doxepin

Defences: read label THREE times (pulling, preparing, giving), use generic names, "tall man" lettering (HumALOG vs HumULIN), independent double-checks for high-alert drugs.
cards.clinicalNote: You're handed an order for 'hydroxyzine' but the chart context screams cardiac. Stop: hydralazine and hydroxyzine are a classic LASA pair. Read back the indication, not just the name, before you give it.

cards.title: "High-Alert" Categories: APINCH
cards.body:
A: Anticoagulants (heparin, warfarin)
P. Potassium concentrate (NEVER push IV undiluted, fatal!)
I: Insulin
N: Narcotics (opioids)
C. Chemotherapy / Cytotoxics
H. Heparin specifically (often listed twice)

These have the highest harm potential when given wrong. Always double-check.

cards.title: High-alert drugs: the list and the safeguard
body: INSULIN, HEPARIN, potassium chloride, opioids, chemotherapy, anticoagulants, concentrated electrolytes
These cause the most severe harm when given wrongly, even though errors with them are not the most common
They require an INDEPENDENT double check by a second registered nurse
Independent means the second nurse works out the dose themselves, before hearing your answer
Never abbreviate 'units' to U. A handwritten 10U has been read as 100 and killed patients
Never write a trailing zero (10.0 mg reads as 100 mg). Always write a leading zero (0.5 mg, never .5 mg)
IV potassium is NEVER pushed. Diluted and infused on a pump, always
cards.clinicalNote: A missing leading zero is one of the most lethal things in a drug chart. '.5 mg' read quickly becomes '5 mg', a tenfold overdose. Writing 0.5 mg costs one keystroke and prevents it.

cards.title: Heparin versus warfarin: which test, which antidote
cards.body:
HEPARIN (unfractionated, IV):
• Monitored with APTT
• Antidote: PROTAMINE SULPHATE
• Works immediately
• Safe in PREGNANCY (it does not cross the placenta)
• Risk: heparin induced thrombocytopenia, so monitor the platelets

WARFARIN (oral):
• Monitored with INR / PT
• Antidote: VITAMIN K (and prothrombin complex or fresh frozen plasma if urgent)
• Takes DAYS to work, so heparin covers the gap when starting it
• TERATOGENIC. Contraindicated in pregnancy
• Affected by diet: vitamin K rich foods (green leafy vegetables) reduce its effect

Memory hook: heParin → Protamine. warfarin → vitamin K (both have a K sound in the antidote pairing).

LOW MOLECULAR WEIGHT HEPARIN (enoxaparin) needs no routine monitoring, which is why it is used for prophylaxis.

cards.title: Self-check: the INR is 8
cards.body:
A patient on warfarin has an INR of 8 and is bruising. What is happening and what is given?

Answer: THEY ARE DANGEROUSLY OVER ANTICOAGULATED. The target INR for most indications is 2 to 3. An INR of 8 means a high bleeding risk. Withhold the warfarin and give VITAMIN K as prescribed, with prothrombin complex concentrate or fresh frozen plasma if there is active major bleeding.

cards.title: Drug → Antidote (Must-Know)
body: Heparin → Protamine sulphate (monitor aPTT)
Warfarin → Vitamin K (+ FFP/PCC if urgent; monitor INR)
Opioids → Naloxone
Benzodiazepines → Flumazenil
Paracetamol → N-acetylcysteine
Digoxin → Digoxin-specific Fab fragments
Iron → Desferrioxamine
Organophosphates → Atropine + Pralidoxime
Methotrexate → Folinic acid (leucovorin)

cards.title: High-Alert Drugs: A PINCH
cards.body:
A – Anticoagulants (heparin, warfarin)
P – Potassium (NEVER IV push undiluted, fatal)
I – Insulin
N – Narcotics (opioids)
C – Chemotherapy / cytotoxics
H – Heparin

These carry the highest harm if given wrong, independent double-check.

cards.title: The antidotes you must know cold
body: Opioids (morphine) → NALOXONE. Shorter half life than the opioid, so the patient can re sedate
Benzodiazepines (diazepam) → FLUMAZENIL. Can precipitate seizures, use with caution
Paracetamol → N-ACETYLCYSTEINE. Most effective within 8 hours of ingestion
Warfarin → VITAMIN K
Heparin → PROTAMINE SULPHATE
Iron → DESFERRIOXAMINE
Organophosphate poisoning → ATROPINE and pralidoxime
Digoxin → DIGOXIN IMMUNE FAB (digibind)
Methotrexate → FOLINIC ACID (leucovorin), not folic acid
cards.clinicalNote: Paracetamol overdose is deceptive. The patient often feels completely well for the first 24 hours while the liver is being destroyed. Never be reassured by how well they look. Treat on the level and the timing, not the appearance.

cards.title: Organophosphate poisoning: DUMBELS
cards.body:
Common in India from pesticide exposure and deliberate ingestion. The signs are cholinergic excess:

D: Diarrhoea
U: Urination
M: Miosis (PINPOINT pupils)
B: Bronchospasm and Bronchorrhoea (drowning in secretions)
E: Emesis, vomiting
L: Lacrimation, streaming eyes
S: Salivation and Sweating

The patient is soaking wet from every orifice.

TREATMENT: ATROPINE (dries the secretions, and it is titrated until the chest is clear, not until the pupils change) plus PRALIDOXIME. Remove contaminated clothing and wash the skin. WEAR GLOVES, or you will absorb it yourself.

cards.title: Self-check: naloxone wears off
cards.body:
You give naloxone to an over sedated patient on morphine. They wake up and improve. Can you now relax the observations?

Answer: NO. Naloxone has a SHORTER half life than morphine, so as it wears off the patient can slip back into respiratory depression. They need continuous observation and may need repeat doses or an infusion.

cards.title: Pharmacokinetics versus pharmacodynamics
cards.body:
PHARMACOKINETICS is what the BODY does to the DRUG. Four stages, remembered as ADME:

A: ABSORPTION, from the gut, muscle, skin or lung into the blood
D: DISTRIBUTION, carried around the body, often bound to plasma proteins such as albumin
M: METABOLISM, mostly by the LIVER
E: EXCRETION, mostly by the KIDNEY

PHARMACODYNAMICS is what the DRUG does to the BODY: the mechanism of action at the receptor.

WHY IT MATTERS CLINICALLY:
• LIVER disease → the drug is not metabolised → it accumulates → toxicity
• KIDNEY disease → the drug is not excreted → it accumulates → toxicity
• LOW ALBUMIN → more free, unbound, ACTIVE drug in the blood → toxicity at a normal dose

In the elderly, liver and kidney function are both reduced. That is why the rule is 'start low, go slow'.
cards.clinicalNote: An elderly, malnourished patient with a low albumin can become toxic on a textbook normal dose, because the drug that would normally be bound to protein is instead circulating free and active. The dose was right, the patient was not.

cards.title: Routes of administration: speed and use
body: INTRAVENOUS: fastest, 100% bioavailable. No absorption phase. Immediate effect, and no way to take it back
INTRAMUSCULAR: fast, muscle is well perfused
SUBCUTANEOUS: slower, steadier absorption
SUBLINGUAL: fast, and it bypasses the liver's FIRST PASS metabolism. This is why GTN is given under the tongue
ORAL: slowest, most convenient, but subject to first pass metabolism in the liver
RECTAL: useful when the patient is vomiting or unconscious. Partly bypasses first pass
TOPICAL and TRANSDERMAL: slow, sustained. Remove the OLD patch before applying a new one
cards.clinicalNote: The forgotten fentanyl patch is a classic and lethal error. A patient with three patches on because nobody removed the old ones is receiving three times the dose. Always look at the skin, and always remove the old patch.

cards.title: The first pass effect, and why route changes dose
cards.body:
A drug swallowed is absorbed from the gut and goes STRAIGHT TO THE LIVER through the portal vein, BEFORE it reaches the rest of the body.

The liver metabolises a large fraction of it on that first pass. So the oral dose must be much LARGER than the IV dose of the same drug to have the same effect.

Routes that AVOID first pass metabolism:
• Sublingual (under the tongue)
• Intravenous
• Intramuscular and subcutaneous
• Transdermal
• Partly rectal

This is why sublingual GTN relieves angina in seconds, and why the same drug swallowed would be largely destroyed before it ever reached the heart.

cards.title: Half life, steady state and the loading dose
cards.body:
HALF LIFE is the time for the plasma concentration to fall by half.

It takes about 4 to 5 HALF LIVES to reach STEADY STATE (a stable level with regular dosing), and about 4 to 5 half lives to clear a drug after stopping it.

That is why:
• A drug started today may not reach full effect for days
• A drug stopped today may still be acting for days

A LOADING DOSE is a large first dose given to reach the therapeutic level QUICKLY, instead of waiting several half lives. It is then followed by smaller maintenance doses.

Drugs with a NARROW THERAPEUTIC INDEX have a tiny gap between the effective dose and the toxic dose, and they need blood level monitoring:
• Digoxin
• Lithium
• Phenytoin
• Warfarin
• Aminoglycosides such as gentamicin
• Theophylline
cards.clinicalNote: Narrow therapeutic index drugs are the ones where a small dosing error, a change in kidney function, or a drug interaction turns treatment into poisoning. Learn the list, because the exam and the ward both return to it constantly.

cards.title: Adverse effects: naming what you are seeing
body: SIDE EFFECT: a predictable, dose related, unwanted effect. Drowsiness from an antihistamine
ADVERSE DRUG REACTION: harmful and unintended, at a normal dose
ALLERGY: an immune reaction. Rash, urticaria, wheeze, and at worst ANAPHYLAXIS
ANAPHYLAXIS: give IM ADRENALINE 0.5 mg (0.5 mL of 1:1000) into the anterolateral THIGH
IDIOSYNCRATIC: a bizarre, unpredictable reaction unique to that individual
TERATOGENIC: causes fetal malformation. Warfarin, phenytoin, isotretinoin, thalidomide, ACE inhibitors
SYNERGISM: two drugs together produce a greater effect than the sum of each
ANTAGONISM: one drug reduces or blocks the effect of another
cards.clinicalNote: Nausea after codeine is a side effect. A florid rash and wheeze after penicillin is an allergy. Patients and charts confuse the two constantly, and labelling a side effect as an allergy can deny someone the best antibiotic for the rest of their life. Ask what actually happened.

cards.title: Self-check: why sublingual GTN
cards.body:
Why is glyceryl trinitrate given under the tongue rather than swallowed?

Answer: TO AVOID FIRST PASS METABOLISM. Swallowed, it would go through the portal vein to the liver and be largely destroyed before reaching the circulation. Sublingual absorption enters the bloodstream directly, giving relief within minutes.

cards.title: Antibiotic classes and the harm each one does
body: PENICILLINS (amoxicillin): allergy and anaphylaxis. Always ask about penicillin allergy
CEPHALOSPORINS (ceftriaxone): cross reactivity with penicillin allergy in a small percentage
AMINOGLYCOSIDES (gentamicin, amikacin): NEPHROTOXIC and OTOTOXIC. Monitor levels and kidney function
MACROLIDES (azithromycin, erythromycin): GI upset, QT prolongation
FLUOROQUINOLONES (ciprofloxacin): TENDON RUPTURE, especially the Achilles. Avoid in children
TETRACYCLINES (doxycycline): stain developing TEETH. Avoid under 8 years and in pregnancy. Photosensitivity
VANCOMYCIN: RED MAN SYNDROME if infused too fast. Nephrotoxic
METRONIDAZOLE: a severe disulfiram reaction with ALCOHOL. Warn the patient explicitly
cards.clinicalNote: Red man syndrome is not an allergy. It is a histamine release caused by infusing vancomycin too quickly, and the answer is to SLOW THE INFUSION, not to abandon the drug. Mislabelling it as an allergy costs the patient a vital antibiotic.

cards.title: Gentamicin: the two toxicities
cards.body:
Aminoglycosides damage two organs, and both begin with the sound of the drug class:

NEPHROTOXIC: the kidneys. Monitor creatinine and urine output
OTOTOXIC: the ears. Hearing loss and vestibular damage, which can be PERMANENT

Early warning of ototoxicity: TINNITUS, vertigo, hearing loss. Report it immediately, do not wait.

MONITORING:
• TROUGH level (taken just BEFORE the next dose) shows whether the drug is CLEARING. A high trough means accumulation and toxicity
• PEAK level shows whether the dose is high enough to work

The trough is the one that warns you about toxicity, and it is the one the exam asks about.

cards.title: Antibiotic resistance: how nurses drive it or stop it
cards.body:
Resistance is created by how antibiotics are used, and nurses sit at the centre of that.

WHAT DRIVES RESISTANCE:
• Antibiotics for viral infections, where they do nothing at all
• Courses stopped early, leaving the toughest organisms alive to multiply
• Broad spectrum drugs used when a narrow spectrum one would do
• Doses missed or given late, so levels fall below the effective range

WHAT NURSES DO ABOUT IT:
• Take CULTURES BEFORE the first dose, wherever possible. Once antibiotics are in, the culture may grow nothing
• Give doses ON TIME. Antibiotic timing is not flexible in the way many other drugs are
• Complete the course, and teach the patient why
• Hand hygiene, which prevents the spread of the resistant organism you already have
• Question a broad spectrum antibiotic that is still running once the sensitivities are known

MRSA, VRE, ESBL and MDR-TB are all products of this process.
cards.clinicalNote: Taking blood cultures before the first antibiotic dose is one of the highest value things a nurse does. Give the drug first and the culture may grow nothing, and the patient spends the next two weeks on a broad spectrum guess.

cards.title: Giving antibiotics safely
body: ASK ABOUT ALLERGY every single time, and ask what actually happened
Take CULTURES first, if the patient's condition allows
Give the FIRST dose as soon as possible in sepsis. Within one hour is the standard
Some are taken on an EMPTY stomach (most penicillins), others WITH food to reduce nausea
Doxycycline: swallow with plenty of water, sit UPRIGHT for 30 minutes. It causes oesophageal ulceration if it lodges
Metronidazole: NO ALCOHOL during the course and for 48 hours afterwards
Watch for the second infection: thrush and C. difficile follow broad spectrum antibiotics
Never save leftover antibiotics, and never share them

cards.title: Anaphylaxis: the drug reaction that kills in minutes
cards.body:
Anaphylaxis is a severe, rapid, systemic allergic reaction. Penicillin is a classic trigger.

RECOGNISE IT:
• Airway: swelling of the lips, tongue and throat, hoarseness, STRIDOR
• Breathing: wheeze, severe breathlessness, hypoxia
• Circulation: profound HYPOTENSION, tachycardia, collapse
• Skin: urticaria, flushing, angio-oedema. But the skin signs may be ABSENT

IMMEDIATE ACTION:
1. STOP the drug or infusion
2. Call for help. Shout for the arrest team
3. ADRENALINE 0.5 mg IM (0.5 mL of 1:1000) into the ANTEROLATERAL THIGH. Repeat after 5 minutes if there is no improvement
4. Lie the patient FLAT with the legs raised (unless breathing is compromised, then sit them up)
5. High flow oxygen, IV fluid challenge
6. Antihistamines and steroids are SECOND LINE. They do NOT replace adrenaline

ADRENALINE IS THE ONLY DRUG THAT SAVES THEM. Everything else is supportive.
cards.clinicalNote: The commonest fatal error in anaphylaxis is delay: reaching for hydrocortisone and chlorphenamine first because they feel less drastic. Steroids take hours to work. The patient does not have hours. Adrenaline, into the thigh, now.

cards.title: Self-check: the trough level
cards.body:
A gentamicin trough level comes back high. What does that tell you, and what is the risk?

Answer: THE DRUG IS NOT CLEARING and is accumulating between doses. The trough is taken just before the next dose, so a high trough means the kidneys are not eliminating it. The risks are NEPHROTOXICITY and permanent OTOTOXICITY. The dose or the interval needs adjusting.

cards.title: Cardiac drugs: what each class does
body: ACE INHIBITORS (enalapril, ramipril): reduce afterload. Cause a DRY COUGH and HYPERKALAEMIA
ARBs (losartan): same benefit, no cough. Used when an ACE inhibitor is not tolerated
BETA BLOCKERS (metoprolol, atenolol): slow the heart, reduce oxygen demand. MASK HYPOGLYCAEMIA in diabetics
CALCIUM CHANNEL BLOCKERS (amlodipine): vasodilate. Ankle oedema, flushing, headache
NITRATES (GTN): venodilate, reduce preload. HEADACHE is almost universal. Tolerance develops
STATINS (atorvastatin): lower cholesterol. MUSCLE PAIN, and rarely rhabdomyolysis. Taken at NIGHT
DIURETICS (furosemide): reduce fluid. HYPOKALAEMIA. Give in the MORNING, not at bedtime
DIGOXIN: slows and strengthens. Narrow therapeutic index
cards.clinicalNote: Giving furosemide at 10 pm guarantees your patient will be up all night and at high risk of a fall on the way to the toilet. Diuretics belong in the morning, and the second dose no later than early afternoon.

cards.title: Beta blockers: the diabetic trap
cards.body:
Beta blockers MASK the warning signs of HYPOGLYCAEMIA.

The early symptoms of a low blood sugar are mostly adrenergic: tremor, palpitations, anxiety, tachycardia. Beta blockers BLOCK exactly those.

So a diabetic on a beta blocker may go from feeling fine to unconscious with no warning at all.

SWEATING is the one hypoglycaemic sign that is NOT blocked. In a diabetic on a beta blocker, unexplained sweating is a low blood sugar until proven otherwise.

Other beta blocker cautions:
• Never stop abruptly. Rebound tachycardia, hypertension and angina, and it can precipitate an MI
• Caution in ASTHMA. Non selective beta blockers cause bronchospasm
• Bradycardia. Check the pulse before giving

cards.title: GTN: how to teach it and why it must be right
cards.body:
TAKING IT:
• Sit or lie DOWN first. It drops the blood pressure and causes dizziness and fainting
• Place the tablet or spray UNDER THE TONGUE, do not swallow it
• If the pain is not relieved after 5 minutes, take a second dose
• If pain persists 5 minutes after the second dose, call emergency services. Up to 3 doses in total
• A tingling or burning under the tongue means it is still active

STORAGE:
• In the ORIGINAL dark glass bottle, tightly closed
• Away from light, heat and moisture. NOT in a trouser pocket next to the body
• Tablets lose potency and must be replaced regularly

ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATION: sildenafil (Viagra) or similar within 24 to 48 hours. The combination causes catastrophic, sometimes fatal hypotension. ASK THE QUESTION, however awkward it feels.
cards.clinicalNote: The sildenafil question is embarrassing to ask and it saves lives. A middle aged man with chest pain will not volunteer it. If you do not ask, and you give nitrates, you may drop his pressure to a level he does not survive.

cards.title: Nursing checks before cardiac drugs
body: DIGOXIN: count an APICAL pulse for one FULL minute. Withhold and report if below 60 in an adult
BETA BLOCKERS: check the pulse and BP. Withhold if bradycardic or hypotensive, and report
ANTIHYPERTENSIVES: check the BP. Withhold if the patient is already hypotensive
ACE INHIBITORS: check the potassium and kidney function. They cause hyperkalaemia
DIURETICS: check the potassium. Weigh the patient daily, and chart input and output
STATINS: ask about unexplained muscle pain, tenderness or weakness
WARFARIN: check today's INR BEFORE giving the dose. The dose changes with the result
Teach patients to rise SLOWLY from lying to sitting to standing, postural hypotension is common

cards.title: Digoxin toxicity: recognising it before it kills
cards.body:
Therapeutic range: 0.5 to 2.0 ng/mL. There is very little margin.

EARLY SIGNS (almost always gastrointestinal first):
• Anorexia, nausea, vomiting
• Diarrhoea, abdominal pain

VISUAL SIGNS (classic and highly examinable):
• YELLOW or GREEN HALOS around lights
• Blurred vision, colour disturbance

CARDIAC SIGNS:
• Bradycardia, and any new arrhythmia

WHAT MAKES IT WORSE:
• HYPOKALAEMIA. It dramatically increases digoxin's effect on the heart. And diuretics cause hypokalaemia
• Kidney impairment, because digoxin is renally excreted
• Old age, low body weight

ANTIDOTE: digoxin immune Fab.
cards.clinicalNote: The classic setup is the elderly patient on furosemide and digoxin together. The diuretic drops the potassium, the low potassium potentiates the digoxin, and the dose that was safe last month becomes toxic this month. Look at the potassium, not just the digoxin level.

cards.title: Self-check: sweating in a diabetic on atenolol
cards.body:
A diabetic patient on atenolol is suddenly sweating heavily but has no tremor or palpitations. What should you suspect?

Answer: HYPOGLYCAEMIA. Beta blockers block the adrenergic warning signs (tremor, palpitations, tachycardia) but they do NOT block sweating. Check the blood glucose immediately. This patient can go from apparently well to unconscious with almost no warning.

cards.title: Insulin types: onset, peak and duration
body: RAPID acting (lispro, aspart): onset 10–15 min, peak 1–2 hr. Give WITH the meal, food must be in front of them
SHORT acting / REGULAR: onset 30 min, peak 2–4 hr. Give 30 minutes BEFORE the meal
INTERMEDIATE (NPH, cloudy): onset 1–2 hr, peak 6–12 hr. Usually twice daily
LONG acting (glargine, detemir): onset 1–2 hr, essentially NO PEAK, lasts about 24 hr. Once daily
The PEAK is when hypoglycaemia is most likely. Know the peak, and you know when to watch
Only REGULAR insulin can be given INTRAVENOUSLY
Never mix glargine with any other insulin in the same syringe
cards.clinicalNote: Giving rapid acting insulin and then discovering the meal trolley is delayed is a genuine emergency in the making. Rapid insulin goes in when the food is physically in front of the patient, not when you are hoping it arrives.

cards.title: Hypoglycaemia: the 15-15 rule
cards.body:
Blood glucose below 70 mg/dL (about 4 mmol/L) with symptoms.

SYMPTOMS, remember COLD AND CLAMMY MEANS GIVE THEM CANDY:
• Sweating, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, hunger
• Confusion, slurred speech, odd behaviour, then seizure and coma

CONSCIOUS AND ABLE TO SWALLOW, the 15-15 rule:
• Give 15 g of FAST acting carbohydrate (glucose tablets, 100 mL fruit juice, sugary drink, NOT diet)
• WAIT 15 minutes, then RECHECK the glucose
• Repeat if it is still low
• Once it is above 70, give a LONG acting carbohydrate (bread, biscuits, a meal) or it will fall again

UNCONSCIOUS or unable to swallow: NOTHING BY MOUTH. Give IM GLUCAGON or IV DEXTROSE.

COLD and CLAMMY = give them CANDY (hypo). HOT and DRY = sugar HIGH (hyper).

cards.title: Oral antidiabetics: what each does and its danger
cards.body:
METFORMIN (biguanide): first line. Reduces liver glucose output. Does NOT cause hypoglycaemia on its own.
• Side effects: GI upset, metallic taste, diarrhoea
• Rare but fatal: LACTIC ACIDOSIS
• MUST BE STOPPED before IV CONTRAST imaging and surgery, and withheld for 48 hours afterwards until kidney function is confirmed. Contrast can precipitate kidney injury, and metformin then accumulates

SULFONYLUREAS (glibenclamide, gliclazide): stimulate the pancreas to release insulin.
• They CAN and DO cause HYPOGLYCAEMIA, and weight gain

THIAZOLIDINEDIONES (pioglitazone): fluid retention, weight gain, worsen heart failure.

DPP-4 inhibitors and SGLT2 inhibitors: newer. SGLT2 inhibitors cause genital and urinary infections, and a risk of euglycaemic ketoacidosis.
cards.clinicalNote: The metformin and contrast rule is one of the most reliably examined facts in nursing pharmacology, and it is genuinely dangerous when missed. If your patient on metformin is going for a contrast CT, that drug is held. Ask, every time.

cards.title: Sick day rules: teaching that prevents admissions
body: NEVER stop insulin during illness, even if the patient is not eating. Illness RAISES blood glucose
Stopping insulin when unwell is the commonest cause of DKA, and it is entirely preventable
Check blood glucose more frequently, at least 4 hourly
Check for KETONES if glucose is persistently high
Maintain fluid intake: sugar free fluids if glucose is high, sugary fluids if they cannot eat
Replace meals with carbohydrate containing drinks if they cannot eat solid food
Seek help for persistent vomiting, ketones, or glucose that will not come down
cards.clinicalNote: The type 1 diabetic who stops their insulin because they have a stomach bug and are not eating is on a direct path to diabetic ketoacidosis. Illness raises the sugar even without food. That single teaching point prevents admissions.

cards.title: Insulin injection technique: what goes wrong
cards.body:
SITES: abdomen (fastest absorption), upper arm, thigh, buttock (slowest).

ROTATE within one site rather than jumping between sites, so that absorption stays predictable day to day.

LIPOHYPERTROPHY: repeated injection into exactly the same spot causes a lumpy fatty thickening. Insulin injected into that lump is absorbed ERRATICALLY, which is a common hidden cause of unexplained glucose swings. LOOK at the skin, and FEEL for lumps.

OTHER POINTS:
• Inject at 90 degrees, or 45 degrees in a very thin patient
• Do NOT massage the site afterwards
• Do not reuse needles. A blunt needle causes tissue damage
• Cloudy insulin (NPH) must be gently ROLLED, not shaken
