• The pen in use is kept at room temperature. Spares in the fridge. NEVER freeze insulin
cards.clinicalNote: When a patient's glucose is wildly erratic and nobody can work out why, examine the injection sites. Patients favour a comfortable, slightly numb spot, and that lumpy area absorbs insulin unpredictably. The fix is free: rotate.

cards.title: Self-check: metformin and contrast
cards.body:
A patient on metformin is scheduled for a CT with IV contrast tomorrow. What must happen?

Answer: HOLD THE METFORMIN. Contrast can cause kidney injury, and if the kidneys fail, metformin accumulates and can cause LACTIC ACIDOSIS, which is often fatal. It is withheld before the scan and for about 48 hours afterwards, restarted only once kidney function is confirmed as stable.

cards.title: The tablet and liquid formula
cards.body:
The single formula that answers most dosage questions:

DOSE REQUIRED  ÷  DOSE AVAILABLE  ×  QUANTITY

Often remembered as: WANT over HAVE, times the VOLUME.

EXAMPLE (tablets): the order is 500 mg. Stock is 250 mg tablets.
500 ÷ 250 × 1 = 2 tablets.

EXAMPLE (liquid): the order is 125 mg. Stock is 250 mg in 5 mL.
125 ÷ 250 × 5 = 2.5 mL.

THE GOLDEN RULE: both numbers must be in the SAME UNIT before you divide. Convert first, calculate second.

1 g = 1000 mg. 1 mg = 1000 micrograms. 1 L = 1000 mL.

cards.title: IV flow rates: the two formulas
body: DROPS PER MINUTE = (volume in mL × drop factor) ÷ time in MINUTES
Drop factor: a macrodrip set is usually 15 or 20 drops/mL, a MICRODRIP is always 60 drops/mL
Example: 1000 mL over 8 hours with a 20 drop/mL set
mL PER HOUR (for a pump) = total volume ÷ total hours
Example: 1000 mL over 8 hours = 125 mL/hour
ALWAYS convert hours to MINUTES for the drops formula. Forgetting this is the classic error
Round drops to the nearest whole number. You cannot give half a drop

cards.title: Paediatric doses: why they are calculated, never guessed
cards.body:
Children are not small adults. Their dose is calculated on BODY WEIGHT, and sometimes on body surface area.

FORMULA: dose in mg/kg × the child's weight in kg = the total dose.

Example: paracetamol 15 mg/kg for a 12 kg child.
15 × 12 = 180 mg per dose.

THEN ALWAYS CHECK IT AGAINST THE MAXIMUM. A calculated dose that exceeds the adult maximum is wrong, however neat the arithmetic.

SAFETY RULES:
• Weigh the child. Never estimate the weight
• ALWAYS have a second nurse independently check a paediatric calculation
• Be extremely careful with decimal points. A misplaced decimal is a TENFOLD error, and in a small child that is fatal
• Use an oral syringe for liquids, never a household spoon
cards.clinicalNote: The decimal point is the most dangerous character in paediatric nursing. 0.5 mL and 5 mL look similar on a scribbled chart and differ by a factor of ten. In a 3 kg neonate, that difference is the whole margin between a dose and a death.

cards.title: Calculation safety: how errors actually happen
body: Convert units FIRST, always. Mixing mg and micrograms is the commonest error of all
Write a LEADING zero: 0.5 mg, never .5 mg. The missing zero causes tenfold overdoses
NEVER write a TRAILING zero: 5 mg, never 5.0 mg, which reads as 50
Write out 'units' and 'micrograms' in full. Never U, never mcg abbreviated carelessly
SANITY CHECK the answer. Does 8 tablets or 40 mL look plausible? It almost never is
If the answer needs more than 2 tablets or ampoules, stop and recheck
Independent double check for all paediatric doses and all high alert drugs
Use a calculator, then repeat the calculation a second time by a different route

cards.title: Insulin and heparin: units, not millilitres
cards.body:
Insulin and heparin are prescribed in UNITS, not in mL or mg. This is a source of catastrophic error.

INSULIN:
• Standard strength is 100 units/mL (U-100)
• Draw it up ONLY with an INSULIN SYRINGE, which is marked directly in UNITS
• NEVER use a normal 1 mL syringe to measure insulin. If you do, you must convert, and that is exactly where the fatal error creeps in
• An insulin syringe removes the arithmetic entirely, which is the point of it

HEPARIN:
• Comes in several strengths. READ THE VIAL, every single time
• 1000 units/mL and 5000 units/mL look almost identical on a busy trolley
• Confusing them is a fivefold error, and it has killed neonates

BOTH require an INDEPENDENT double check.
cards.clinicalNote: The reason insulin syringes exist is to abolish the calculation. The moment a nurse reaches for a normal syringe and starts converting units into millilitres, an entirely preventable class of fatal error becomes possible.

cards.title: Self-check: the drip rate
cards.body:
An order reads 1000 mL of normal saline over 10 hours. The giving set is 15 drops/mL. What is the rate in drops per minute?

Answer: 25 DROPS PER MINUTE. (1000 × 15) ÷ (10 × 60) = 15000 ÷ 600 = 25. The classic error is dividing by 10 instead of by 600, because the hours were never converted into minutes.

cards.title: India UIP Schedule (Quick View)
cards.body:
BIRTH: BCG + OPV-0 + HepB-0
6 WEEKS: Penta-1 + OPV-1 + Rota-1 + fIPV-1 + PCV-1
10 WEEKS: Penta-2 + OPV-2 + Rota-2
14 WEEKS: Penta-3 + OPV-3 + Rota-3 + fIPV-2 + PCV-2
9 MONTHS: MR-1 + JE-1 (endemic) + PCV booster + Vit A
16–24 M: DPT-1 booster + OPV booster + MR-2 + JE-2
5–6 YR: DPT-2 booster
10 YR & 16 YR: Td

cards.title: Live vs Inactivated Vaccines
cards.body:
LIVE (don't give in pregnancy / severe immunosuppression):
• MMR, MR
• BCG
• OPV
• Varicella
• Yellow fever
• Rotavirus
• Typhoid (oral Ty21a)

INACTIVATED / SUBUNIT (safer):
• DPT, Hib, HepB, IPV
• Influenza (injectable)
• PCV, HPV
• Rabies, JE
• Typhoid (injectable)

cards.title: Immunisation: the rules nurses get asked
body: PENTAVALENT covers five: diphtheria, pertussis, tetanus, hepatitis B, and Haemophilus influenzae type b
BCG is given intradermally, at the left upper arm, dose 0.05 mL under 1 month and 0.1 mL over 1 month
The BCG papule ulcerates and heals into a scar over 6–12 weeks. That is NORMAL, not an abscess
Most childhood vaccines are given INTRAMUSCULAR in the ANTEROLATERAL THIGH (vastus lateralis), never the buttock
OPV is oral, and it is the one that must NOT be given if the child has an immunodeficiency at home
A mild illness, low grade fever or malnutrition are NOT contraindications. Immunise anyway
LIVE vaccines are contraindicated in pregnancy and severe immunosuppression
If a dose is missed, RESUME the schedule. Do NOT restart from the beginning
cards.clinicalNote: Missed doses cause endless confusion and needless restarting. The immune system does not forget. If a child returns two months late, you continue from where they stopped, you never begin the whole course again.

cards.title: Cold chain: what a nurse must protect
body: Standard vaccine storage: +2 to +8 °C in the refrigerator
OPV and measles/MR tolerate freezing and are the most HEAT sensitive
NEVER FREEZE: DPT, TT, Td, Hepatitis B, Pentavalent, PCV, IPV, Rotavirus. Freezing DESTROYS them
A frozen and thawed vaccine looks completely normal, which is exactly what makes it dangerous
SHAKE TEST: a frozen and damaged vaccine sediments quickly. Discard it
Vaccine vial monitor (VVM): if the inner square is as dark as, or darker than, the outer circle, DISCARD
Never store food, drink or anything else in the vaccine refrigerator
Keep the ice packs conditioned. Sweating ice packs prevent accidental freezing in the carrier

cards.title: Self-check: the missed vaccine dose
cards.body:
A child received the first pentavalent dose at 6 weeks but did not return until 6 months of age. Do you restart the course?

Answer: NO, RESUME IT. Continue from where the schedule was interrupted and give the remaining doses at the correct intervals. The immune system retains its priming. Restarting wastes vaccine, wastes visits, and delays protection.

cards.title: General Danger Signs in a Sick Child
cards.body:
Refer URGENTLY if a child:
• Is unable to drink or breastfeed
• Vomits everything
• Has had convulsions
• Is lethargic or unconscious

IMNCI uses a colour triage: PINK = urgent referral, YELLOW = treat at facility, GREEN = home care/advice.
cards.clinicalNote: A mother brings a toddler who 'won't drink and vomits everything'. That single sentence is two IMNCI danger signs. Don't wait for a fever spike, danger signs alone mean urgent referral.

cards.title: Fast Breathing Cut-offs (Pneumonia)
body: <2 months: ≥ 60 breaths/min
2–12 months: ≥ 50 breaths/min
12 months–5 years: ≥ 40 breaths/min
Chest indrawing = severe pneumonia → refer
Count breaths for a FULL minute when the child is calm

cards.title: Fast breathing: the cut-offs that diagnose pneumonia
body: PNEUMONIA in a child is diagnosed at the bedside by COUNTING THE BREATHS for a full minute
Under 2 months: 60 breaths per minute or more is FAST
2 to 12 months: 50 or more is FAST
12 months to 5 years: 40 or more is FAST
CHEST INDRAWING (the lower chest wall moves IN on inspiration) means SEVERE pneumonia. Urgent referral
STRIDOR in a calm child, or grunting, or cyanosis: severe, refer immediately
Count when the child is CALM. A crying or feeding child breathes faster
Fast breathing is the sign that decides treatment. No stethoscope required
cards.clinicalNote: The respiratory rate in a child is not a routine observation to fill a chart. It is the diagnostic test for pneumonia, and it is free. Count for a full minute, with the child calm, and count twice if you are unsure.

cards.title: Assessing dehydration in a child
body: NO dehydration: alert, drinks normally, eyes normal, skin pinch goes back immediately
SOME dehydration: restless and irritable, THIRSTY and drinks eagerly, SUNKEN EYES, skin pinch goes back SLOWLY
SEVERE dehydration: LETHARGIC or unconscious, drinks POORLY or not at all, very sunken eyes, skin pinch goes back VERY SLOWLY (over 2 seconds)
SEVERE also: weak or absent pulse, cold extremities, no urine output
PLAN A (no dehydration): extra fluid at home, ORS, zinc, continue feeding
PLAN B (some dehydration): ORS 75 mL/kg over 4 hours at the facility, then reassess
PLAN C (severe dehydration): IV FLUIDS IMMEDIATELY. Ringer's lactate. This is an emergency
A child who is drinking EAGERLY is dehydrated. A child who is too weak to drink is severely dehydrated
cards.clinicalNote: The child who drinks greedily worries you. The child who refuses to drink terrifies you. Thirst means some dehydration, but the lethargic child who will not even take the cup has lost the ability to save themselves, and needs a drip now.

cards.title: Self-check: counting a child's breaths
cards.body:
A 9 month old has a cough and you count 52 breaths per minute while she is quietly feeding. Is this fast breathing?

Answer: YES, AND IT MEANS PNEUMONIA. For a child aged 2 to 12 months the cut off is 50 or more. But note she was FEEDING, which raises the rate, so recount when she is fully calm. If it remains 50 or above, treat for pneumonia.

cards.title: IMNCI: the four general danger signs
cards.body:
Any ONE of these means URGENT REFERRAL, whatever else you find:

• Unable to DRINK or BREASTFEED
• VOMITS EVERYTHING
• CONVULSIONS
• LETHARGIC or UNCONSCIOUS

Remember them as the child who cannot TAKE IN, cannot KEEP IN, is FITTING, or is FADING.

The IMNCI colour code:
PINK: urgent referral to hospital
YELLOW: treat at the facility, and teach the mother home care
GREEN: home management and advice

Check the danger signs FIRST, on every sick child, before you assess anything else. They override every other finding.

cards.title: Developmental milestones: the ones to know
body: SOCIAL SMILE: 2 months. Its absence by 3 months is a red flag
HEAD CONTROL (no head lag): 3 months
ROLLS OVER: 5–6 months. REACHES for objects, transfers hand to hand: 6 months
SITS WITHOUT SUPPORT: 8 months
CRAWLS: 9 months. STANDS holding on: 9–10 months. PINCER GRASP: 10–12 months
WALKS unaided: 12–15 months. SPEAKS 1–2 words with meaning: 12 months
RUNS, climbs stairs, 2 word sentences: 2 years
WEIGHT: birth weight DOUBLES by 5 months, TRIPLES by 1 year, QUADRUPLES by 2 years
HEIGHT: doubles birth length by 4 years. Head circumference roughly equals chest circumference at 1 year
cards.clinicalNote: The absence of a social smile by three months, or a child who does not sit by ten months, is not something to watch and wait on. Early referral for developmental delay is what allows early intervention, and early intervention is what changes the outcome.

cards.title: Erikson: the developmental stage for each age
cards.body:
Each stage is a conflict the child must resolve, and knowing the age gives you the answer.

INFANT (0–1 yr): TRUST vs mistrust. Consistent, responsive care builds trust. Meet their needs promptly
TODDLER (1–3 yr): AUTONOMY vs shame and doubt. Let them choose. Toilet training belongs here
PRESCHOOL (3–6 yr): INITIATIVE vs guilt. They ask endless questions and play imaginatively. Allow, do not scold
SCHOOL AGE (6–12 yr): INDUSTRY vs inferiority. They want to achieve and to be good at things. Praise real achievement
ADOLESCENT (12–18 yr): IDENTITY vs role confusion. Peer group is everything. Privacy and independence matter
YOUNG ADULT: INTIMACY vs isolation
MIDDLE ADULT: GENERATIVITY vs stagnation
OLD AGE: INTEGRITY vs despair

THE NURSING USE: a hospitalised toddler needs CHOICES to protect autonomy. A hospitalised adolescent needs PRIVACY and their peer group. A hospitalised preschooler needs PLAY.

cards.title: Play, hospitalisation and what each age fears
cards.body:
Illness and hospital frighten children in age specific ways, and knowing the fear tells you the intervention.

INFANT: fears SEPARATION from the caregiver. Keep the parent present. Rooming in.
TODDLER: fears SEPARATION most of all, and loss of control. Expect regression (bedwetting, thumb sucking) and tolerate it. Keep routines.
PRESCHOOLER: fears BODILY INJURY and MUTILATION. They think magically, and may believe illness is a PUNISHMENT for being naughty. Say clearly: 'this is not your fault'. Use a bandage even on a tiny wound, because they fear leaking away.
SCHOOL AGE: fears loss of CONTROL and loss of independence. Explain honestly, involve them, let them help.
ADOLESCENT: fears loss of IDENTITY, altered body image, and separation from PEERS. Respect privacy and confidentiality. Allow friends to visit.

PLAY BY AGE:
• Infant: SOLITARY play
• Toddler: PARALLEL play (alongside, not with, other children)
• Preschool: ASSOCIATIVE play
• School age: COOPERATIVE play, with rules

THERAPEUTIC PLAY is not entertainment. Letting a child give an injection to a doll is how they process what is being done to them.
cards.clinicalNote: A preschooler often believes their illness is a punishment for something they did wrong. They will not say so, and nobody will think to correct it. Telling them plainly that they did nothing to cause this is a genuine nursing intervention.

cards.title: Child safety by age: the injuries that actually happen
body: INFANT: falls from beds and tables, SUFFOCATION, choking on small objects, hot liquid scalds
Never leave an infant unattended on any raised surface, even for a second
TODDLER: POISONING (they put everything in their mouth), drowning (even in a bucket), burns, road traffic
Keep medicines and cleaning fluids LOCKED and out of reach. Kerosene in a soft drink bottle kills children
PRESCHOOL: road traffic, drowning, burns, falls from height
SCHOOL AGE: road traffic, bicycle injuries, sports injuries, drowning
ADOLESCENT: road traffic, risk taking behaviour, substance use, self harm
The commonest choking hazards: peanuts, grapes, small toys, coins, button batteries
cards.clinicalNote: Kerosene stored in a soft drink bottle is one of the commonest and most preventable causes of childhood poisoning in India. Every home visit is a chance to say it out loud, and it is the kind of advice that saves a life you never hear about.

cards.title: Malnutrition: recognising and treating it
cards.body:
SEVERE ACUTE MALNUTRITION (SAM) is defined by ANY ONE of:
• Weight for height below −3 SD
• Mid upper arm circumference (MUAC) BELOW 11.5 cm in a child 6 to 59 months
• BILATERAL PITTING OEDEMA of the feet

MARASMUS: severe wasting. The child is skin and bone, with an 'old man' face, no subcutaneous fat, visible ribs. They are usually ALERT and IRRITABLE, and often ravenously hungry.

KWASHIORKOR: protein deficiency with OEDEMA. A swollen face and feet, a distended belly, sparse discoloured 'flag sign' hair, 'flaky paint' skin, an enlarged fatty liver. The child is APATHETIC, miserable and has NO appetite.

THE TRAP: a kwashiorkor child may look plump because of the oedema. The weight is falsely reassuring. Always check for pitting oedema.

TREATMENT of SAM has a strict order:
1. Treat hypoglycaemia and HYPOTHERMIA first. These kill in the first hours
2. Treat dehydration CAUTIOUSLY (ReSoMal, not standard ORS), and treat infection
3. Correct electrolytes and micronutrients
4. Begin cautious feeding, then catch up growth

DO NOT FEED AGGRESSIVELY AT FIRST. REFEEDING SYNDROME (a dangerous fall in phosphate, potassium and magnesium) can kill a severely malnourished child who is fed too fast.
cards.clinicalNote: The instinct with a starving child is to feed them generously and immediately. That instinct kills. Refeeding syndrome shifts electrolytes into the cells and can stop the heart. Feeding is started slowly and deliberately, and that is not cruelty, it is the treatment.

cards.title: Self-check: the plump malnourished child
cards.body:
A 2 year old has a swollen face and feet and looks reasonably well nourished on the scales. Her hair is sparse and pale and she has no appetite. What is happening?

Answer: KWASHIORKOR. The bilateral pitting OEDEMA is masking severe malnutrition and making the weight falsely reassuring. The apathy, poor appetite and hair changes fit. Bilateral pitting oedema alone is enough to diagnose severe acute malnutrition.

cards.title: The childhood exanthems: telling the rashes apart
body: MEASLES: prodrome of fever, cough, coryza and conjunctivitis (the 3 Cs). KOPLIK SPOTS in the mouth are pathognomonic
Measles rash: red, blotchy, starts BEHIND THE EARS and on the face, then spreads DOWNWARDS. Complications: pneumonia, otitis, encephalitis, blindness. VITAMIN A is given
RUBELLA: mild. A pink rash starting on the face, with tender POSTERIOR CERVICAL and occipital lymph nodes. Devastating to the FETUS in early pregnancy
CHICKENPOX: intensely itchy. A rash in CROPS, so macules, papules, vesicles and crusts appear TOGETHER at the same time
Chickenpox spreads centrally (trunk first). Infectious until every lesion has crusted
MUMPS: painful PAROTID swelling. Complications: orchitis, meningitis, pancreatitis
DIPHTHERIA: a grey adherent MEMBRANE over the tonsils that BLEEDS when scraped. A 'bull neck'. Airway obstruction and myocarditis
cards.clinicalNote: The chickenpox rash is the one that appears in crops, so on the same day you see fresh blisters next to older crusts. Measles is a uniform blotchy sheet that marches downwards from the hairline. That difference alone usually settles it.

cards.title: Croup versus epiglottitis: the one you must not examine
cards.body:
Both cause STRIDOR in a child, and confusing them is dangerous.

CROUP (viral laryngotracheobronchitis):
• GRADUAL onset, over days, usually after a cold
• A BARKING, seal-like cough. Hoarse voice. Stridor worse at night and when upset
• The child is usually not toxic and can lie down
• Treatment: keep them CALM, steroids (dexamethasone), nebulised adrenaline if severe

EPIGLOTTITIS (bacterial, usually Haemophilus influenzae b, now rare thanks to the Hib vaccine):
• SUDDEN onset, over hours. The child looks TOXIC and frightened
• The FOUR Ds: DROOLING, DYSPHAGIA, DYSPHONIA, DISTRESS
• They sit UPRIGHT, leaning forward, in the TRIPOD position, chin thrust out. They will not lie down
• NO cough, or minimal

THE ABSOLUTE RULE IN EPIGLOTTITIS:
DO NOT EXAMINE THE THROAT. DO NOT USE A TONGUE DEPRESSOR. DO NOT LIE THE CHILD DOWN. DO NOT UPSET THEM.
Any of these can trigger complete airway obstruction and death.
Keep the child calm, with the parent, sitting up. Call for senior and anaesthetic help immediately.

cards.title: Febrile seizures: frightening and usually benign
cards.body:
A seizure caused by fever, in a child aged 6 MONTHS to 5 YEARS, with no CNS infection and no other cause.

SIMPLE febrile seizure: generalised, lasts UNDER 15 minutes, does not recur within 24 hours. It does NOT cause brain damage, and it does NOT mean the child has epilepsy.

COMPLEX: focal, lasts OVER 15 minutes, or recurs within 24 hours. Needs investigation.

WHAT TO DO DURING A SEIZURE:
• Stay with the child, and note the TIME it started
• Protect them from injury, move furniture away. Do NOT restrain them
• PUT NOTHING IN THE MOUTH. Not a spoon, not your fingers, not a cloth
• Turn them onto their SIDE once the movements stop, to protect the airway
• Loosen tight clothing, give oxygen if available
• If it lasts more than 5 minutes, give a benzodiazepine as prescribed, and call for help

AFTERWARDS: find the source of the fever. It is the underlying infection that matters, not the seizure.

WHAT TO TELL THE PARENTS: this is common, it is frightening, and it does not cause brain damage. Manage the fever, but understand that antipyretics do NOT reliably prevent a recurrence.
cards.clinicalNote: Parents remember a febrile seizure for the rest of their lives, and they are usually convinced their child nearly died. Reassurance is not padding here. Explaining that this does not cause brain damage and does not mean epilepsy is a real intervention that prevents years of anxiety.

cards.title: Diarrhoea in children: the treatment that saves lives
body: Diarrhoea kills through DEHYDRATION, not through the infection itself
LOW OSMOLARITY ORS is the treatment. It works even in an inflamed gut, via sodium-glucose co-transport
ZINC: 20 mg daily for 14 DAYS (10 mg if under 6 months). Continue the FULL 14 days even after the diarrhoea stops
Zinc shortens the episode AND reduces the chance of another one over the next 2 to 3 months
CONTINUE FEEDING and CONTINUE BREASTFEEDING throughout. Starving the gut prolongs the illness
Do NOT give antidiarrhoeal drugs to children. They are dangerous and do not treat the cause
Antibiotics ONLY for dysentery (blood in the stool) and cholera. Not for routine watery diarrhoea
Prevention: handwashing with soap, safe water, exclusive breastfeeding, rotavirus vaccine, sanitation

cards.title: Worm infestation and anaemia in children
cards.body:
Worms are so common in Indian children that they are a routine public health target, not an exotic diagnosis.

ROUNDWORM (Ascaris): the commonest. Faecal-oral. Can cause intestinal obstruction in heavy infestation, and the worm may be vomited or passed.

HOOKWORM: enters through the BARE SKIN OF THE FOOT from contaminated soil. It sucks blood from the gut wall and is a leading cause of IRON DEFICIENCY ANAEMIA. This is why footwear is a public health intervention.

PINWORM (Enterobius): intense PERIANAL ITCHING, especially at NIGHT. Diagnosed with the adhesive tape test in the morning. Treat the WHOLE FAMILY, or it simply cycles round again.

TAPEWORM: from undercooked pork or beef.

TREATMENT: ALBENDAZOLE 400 mg (200 mg if 1 to 2 years). The National Deworming Day gives it to all children 1 to 19 years, twice a year.

PREVENTION: sanitation, handwashing, WEARING FOOTWEAR, washing vegetables, keeping nails short, safe disposal of faeces.
cards.clinicalNote: Telling a family that their child must wear chappals is not trivial health advice. Hookworm larvae penetrate bare feet from contaminated soil, and the resulting chronic blood loss is a genuine cause of the anaemia and poor growth you are treating.

cards.title: Self-check: the drooling child with stridor
cards.body:
A 4 year old arrives sitting bolt upright, leaning forward, drooling, with stridor and a high fever. A colleague reaches for a torch and a tongue depressor. What do you do?

Answer: STOP THEM. This is EPIGLOTTITIS. Examining the throat, using a tongue depressor, or lying the child down can trigger complete airway obstruction and death. Keep the child calm and upright with the parent, give oxygen if tolerated, and get senior and anaesthetic help immediately.

cards.title: Neonatal sepsis: subtle, and lethal
body: Newborns do NOT present like adults. There may be no fever at all. HYPOTHERMIA is just as ominous
The commonest first sign is that the baby is 'NOT DOING WELL': poor feeding, lethargy, less active
Poor feeding or refusal to suck in a baby who was feeding well is a RED FLAG
Other signs: temperature instability, fast breathing, grunting, chest indrawing, apnoea
Also: vomiting, abdominal distension, jaundice (especially in the first 24 hours), seizures, a bulging fontanelle
EARLY onset (under 72 hours): acquired from the mother's genital tract during birth
LATE onset (after 72 hours): acquired from the environment, hands and equipment
Sepsis progresses very rapidly in a neonate. Treat on suspicion, do not wait for proof
cards.clinicalNote: A mother who says her baby is 'just not feeding like before' is giving you the most important piece of information in the room. In a newborn, a change in feeding is often the only early sign of overwhelming sepsis, and it is routinely dismissed.

cards.title: Keeping the newborn warm: the warm chain
cards.body:
HYPOTHERMIA kills newborns, and it is entirely preventable. A newborn loses heat FOUR ways:

EVAPORATION: from wet skin. DRY THE BABY IMMEDIATELY and remove the wet towel
CONDUCTION: contact with a cold surface. Never place a baby on a cold weighing scale or table. Pre warm the surface
CONVECTION: cold draughts. Close doors and windows, no fan or air conditioner blowing on the baby
RADIATION: heat lost to nearby cold objects, such as a cold outside wall. Keep the cot away from walls and windows

THE WARM CHAIN:
• A warm delivery room (at least 25 °C)
• Immediate drying, and remove the wet cloth
• SKIN TO SKIN contact with the mother
• Cover the head. The head is a large surface area and loses heat fast
• Delay the first bath
• KANGAROO MOTHER CARE for low birth weight babies

Normal newborn temperature: 36.5 to 37.5 °C. Below 36.5 is hypothermia.

cards.title: Neonatal resuscitation: the golden minute
cards.body:
THE THREE QUESTIONS at birth: Is it term? Is it breathing or crying? Does it have good tone?

If all three are YES, the baby stays with the mother for routine care.

If ANY answer is NO, begin the initial steps IMMEDIATELY:

THE GOLDEN MINUTE: within 60 SECONDS of birth, the initial steps and, if needed, ventilation must be under way.

1. WARM (dry, remove the wet linen, warmer)
2. POSITION the head and clear the airway if needed (suction the MOUTH before the NOSE)
3. DRY, and STIMULATE (rub the back, flick the soles)
4. REPOSITION

If the baby is still apnoeic or gasping, or the heart rate is BELOW 100:
• START POSITIVE PRESSURE VENTILATION with a bag and mask. This is the single most important step in neonatal resuscitation

If the heart rate is BELOW 60 after 30 seconds of effective ventilation:
• Add CHEST COMPRESSIONS, at a ratio of 3 compressions to 1 breath

VENTILATION, NOT COMPRESSIONS, is what saves newborns. Most neonatal arrests are RESPIRATORY in origin, and effective bag and mask ventilation resolves the great majority.

Suction the MOUTH first, then the NOSE. Suctioning the nose first can trigger a gasp that aspirates whatever is in the mouth.
cards.clinicalNote: The adult instinct is to reach for chest compressions. In a newborn that is nearly always wrong. The heart has usually stopped because the baby is not breathing, and it will restart once you inflate the lungs. Get the ventilation right first.

cards.title: Low birth weight and preterm care
body: LOW BIRTH WEIGHT: under 2500 g. VERY low: under 1500 g. EXTREMELY low: under 1000 g
PRETERM: born before 37 completed weeks. SMALL FOR GESTATIONAL AGE: below the 10th centile for age
The four great risks: HYPOTHERMIA, HYPOGLYCAEMIA, INFECTION, and FEEDING difficulty
Also: respiratory distress syndrome (surfactant deficiency), jaundice, apnoea, intraventricular haemorrhage
KANGAROO MOTHER CARE: continuous skin to skin, exclusive breast milk, early discharge with follow up
KMC reduces mortality, infection and hypothermia, and it costs nothing. It is a genuinely major intervention
EXPRESSED BREAST MILK is preferred if the baby is too immature to suck. Feed by cup, spoon or tube, never a bottle
Monitor blood glucose. A preterm baby has minimal glycogen stores and becomes hypoglycaemic quickly
cards.clinicalNote: Kangaroo mother care is not a comfort measure or a substitute for a working incubator. It measurably reduces mortality in low birth weight babies, and it can be delivered by a mother in any setting on earth.

cards.title: Respiratory distress in the newborn
cards.body:
SIGNS OF RESPIRATORY DISTRESS:
• Tachypnoea (over 60 breaths per minute)
• GRUNTING (the baby is trying to keep its own alveoli open, this is an ominous sign)
• NASAL FLARING
• CHEST INDRAWING and subcostal or intercostal recession
• Cyanosis

RESPIRATORY DISTRESS SYNDROME (hyaline membrane disease): caused by SURFACTANT deficiency in the PRETERM baby. Without surfactant, the alveoli collapse with every breath. Treated with surfactant and respiratory support. ANTENATAL STEROIDS given to the mother before a preterm delivery dramatically reduce it.

MECONIUM ASPIRATION: the baby has passed meconium in utero (a sign of distress) and inhaled it. The liquor is green and stained.

TRANSIENT TACHYPNOEA OF THE NEWBORN: retained lung fluid, commoner after caesarean section. Usually settles within 24 to 72 hours.

GRUNTING IS NEVER NORMAL. It is the baby generating its own positive end expiratory pressure, and it means they are working hard to stay alive.
cards.clinicalNote: A grunting newborn is a baby in trouble, however pink they look. Grunting is the sound of a baby trying to hold their own alveoli open, and it is one of the most reliable signs that they are about to decompensate.

cards.title: Self-check: heart rate 50 after birth
cards.body:
A newborn is apnoeic with a heart rate of 50. Your colleague immediately begins chest compressions. Is that right?

Answer: NO, NOT YET. The priority is EFFECTIVE VENTILATION with a bag and mask. Most neonatal arrests are respiratory in origin, and the heart rate usually recovers once the lungs are inflated. Compressions are added only if the heart rate remains BELOW 60 after 30 seconds of effective ventilation.

cards.title: Paediatric basic life support: how it differs from adults
body: Children arrest because they STOP BREATHING. Adults arrest because their heart stops. This drives every difference
In a child, give 5 RESCUE BREATHS FIRST, before any compressions
Compression to ventilation ratio: 15:2 for two rescuers, 30:2 for a lone rescuer
Compress ONE THIRD of the depth of the chest
INFANT (under 1 year): two fingers, or two thumbs encircling the chest. Compress just below the nipple line
CHILD (over 1 year): one or two hands on the lower half of the sternum
Pulse check: BRACHIAL artery in an infant, CAROTID or femoral in a child
Rate: 100 to 120 compressions per minute, the same as an adult
Do NOT hyperextend an infant's neck. It kinks and OBSTRUCTS their soft airway. Use the neutral, sniffing position
cards.clinicalNote: Tipping an infant's head right back as you would an adult's actually closes their airway, because the trachea is soft and short and it kinks. The neutral position is what opens it, and it is one of the commonest mistakes made by adult trained staff.

cards.title: Choking in a child: back blows and thrusts
cards.body:
EFFECTIVE cough (can cry, cough, speak): ENCOURAGE THE COUGH. Do nothing else. Do not interfere, and do not slap the back

INEFFECTIVE cough, but still CONSCIOUS:

INFANT (under 1 year):
• 5 BACK BLOWS, head DOWN, between the shoulder blades, with the heel of the hand
• Then 5 CHEST THRUSTS (like compressions, but sharper and slower)
• NEVER give ABDOMINAL thrusts to an infant. Their liver is large and unprotected and you will rupture it

CHILD (over 1 year):
• 5 BACK BLOWS
• Then 5 ABDOMINAL THRUSTS (Heimlich)
• Alternate the two

If they become UNCONSCIOUS: begin CPR, and check the mouth for the object each time you open the airway.

NEVER perform a BLIND FINGER SWEEP. You will push the object deeper and impact it.

cards.title: Poisoning in a child: what to do, and what never to do
cards.body:
Children poison themselves by accident, and the substance is usually a household one.

IMMEDIATE ACTION:
1. Remove the child from the source. Remove contaminated clothing, wash the skin
2. ABC. Airway, breathing, circulation come before any antidote
3. Identify WHAT, HOW MUCH, and WHEN. Bring the container to hospital
4. Contact the poison centre. Give the specific antidote if one exists

DO NOT INDUCE VOMITING. This is the critical point. It is no longer recommended, and it is DANGEROUS.

NEVER induce vomiting after:
• CORROSIVES (acid, alkali, drain cleaner, bleach). It burns the oesophagus a second time on the way back up
• KEROSENE, petrol, or any hydrocarbon. Vomiting causes ASPIRATION into the lungs, and chemical pneumonitis is what actually kills these children
• An unconscious or convulsing child. They will aspirate

Activated charcoal may be used for some ingestions, on medical advice, and it does NOT work for iron, lithium, alcohol or corrosives.

PREVENTION IS THE REAL ANSWER: lock medicines and chemicals away, never store kerosene or pesticide in a drink bottle, keep original labelled containers.
cards.clinicalNote: Kerosene poisoning is the classic Indian paediatric emergency, and the classic fatal error is a well meaning relative making the child vomit. The kerosene goes into the lungs, and the chemical pneumonitis that follows is what kills the child, not the swallowed dose.

cards.title: Fluids and burns in children: why they are different
body: Children have a proportionally LARGER body surface area, so they lose heat and fluid faster
The RULE OF NINES IS DIFFERENT IN CHILDREN: the HEAD is about 18% (not 9%), and each LEG about 14% (not 18%)
This is because a child's head is a far larger share of their body than an adult's
The LUND AND BROWDER chart is more accurate for children, as it adjusts for age
MAINTENANCE FLUID, the 4-2-1 rule per hour: 4 mL/kg for the first 10 kg, 2 mL/kg for the next 10 kg, 1 mL/kg thereafter
Example: a 25 kg child = (4×10) + (2×10) + (1×5) = 40 + 20 + 5 = 65 mL per hour
Children compensate for shock extremely well, and then crash suddenly. A NORMAL BP in a shocked child is late and falsely reassuring
TACHYCARDIA and prolonged CAPILLARY REFILL are the early signs. HYPOTENSION is a PRE-TERMINAL sign in a child
cards.clinicalNote: A child in shock holds their blood pressure up until they have almost nothing left, and then they fall off a cliff. If you wait for hypotension to convince you a child is shocked, you have waited far too long. Trust the tachycardia and the capillary refill.

cards.title: Child abuse: recognising what is not an accident
cards.body:
Nurses are often the first and only people who will notice, and a missed opportunity may be the child's last.

PHYSICAL SIGNS THAT SHOULD RAISE SUSPICION:
• Injuries that do NOT FIT the explanation given, or fit the child's developmental stage poorly (a bruise in a baby who cannot yet roll or crawl)
• Bruises of DIFFERENT AGES, suggesting repeated injury
• Injuries in PROTECTED sites: the back, buttocks, thighs, ears, the inside of the mouth. Accidental bruises fall on bony prominences: shins, knees, forehead
• Patterned marks: a belt, a cord, a hand, a cigarette burn
• Burns with sharply demarcated edges and no splash marks (a forced immersion), or symmetrical stocking and glove burns
• Spiral fractures of a long bone in a child too young to walk

BEHAVIOURAL SIGNS: excessive wariness, frozen watchfulness, flinching, indiscriminate affection, fear of the parent, delayed presentation.

NEGLECT: dirty, hungry, failing to thrive, missed immunisations, untreated conditions.

WHAT THE NURSE DOES:
• DOCUMENT precisely and factually, exactly what you SAW and exactly what was SAID, in quotation marks. Do not speculate on the record
• Do NOT interrogate the child, and do not confront the parent
• REPORT through the safeguarding pathway. This is a legal and professional duty, not a personal judgement call
• The child's safety comes before the family's comfort and before your discomfort
cards.clinicalNote: The single most useful question is whether the injury matches the story and matches what this child can physically do. A baby who cannot yet roll over cannot bruise their own back. That inconsistency is the finding, and it must be documented in the child's words and yours.

cards.title: Self-check: the child who swallowed kerosene
cards.body:
A 3 year old has swallowed kerosene. A relative is trying to make him vomit. What do you do?

Answer: STOP THEM IMMEDIATELY. Vomiting a hydrocarbon such as kerosene causes ASPIRATION into the lungs, and the resulting chemical pneumonitis is what actually kills these children. Never induce vomiting after hydrocarbons or corrosives. Support the airway and breathing, and get the child to hospital.

cards.title: Four Stages of Labour
cards.body:
STAGE 1: Onset of regular contractions → full cervical dilatation (10 cm).
  • Latent phase: 0 → ~6 cm
  • Active phase: 6 → 10 cm (~1 cm/hr primigravida)

STAGE 2: Full dilatation → birth of baby.
  • Cardinal movements happen here

STAGE 3: Birth of baby → expulsion of placenta (typically <30 min)

STAGE 4: 1–2 hr observation post-placenta. Watch for PPH

cards.title: Cardinal Movements (Mechanism of Labour)
cards.body:
Every Decent Family Is Extremely Rich, Earning Excellent Eternal Riches

• Engagement
• Descent
• Flexion
• Internal rotation
• Extension
• Restitution
• External rotation
• Expulsion

These describe how the foetal head navigates the pelvic canal.

cards.title: The Four Stages
cards.body:
FIRST: onset of true labour → full dilatation (10 cm)
  • Latent (0–3 cm) then active (4–10 cm)
SECOND: full dilatation → birth of the baby
THIRD: birth of baby → delivery of placenta (watch for the 3 signs of separation)
FOURTH: ~1 hr after the placenta. Immediate recovery, watch for PPH.

cards.title: Partograph Essentials
body: Plots cervical dilatation against time to spot abnormal/obstructed labour early
Alert line: 1 cm/hr in active phase; crossing it prompts review
Action line: 4 hr right of the alert line → intervention
Also tracks fetal heart rate, contractions, descent, liquor
A simple tool that prevents prolonged/obstructed labour deaths

cards.title: Labour: the numbers that define progress
body: FIRST STAGE: onset of true labour to FULL dilatation (10 cm)
Latent phase: 0–4 cm, slow. Active phase: 4–10 cm, about 1 cm per hour in a primigravida
SECOND STAGE: full dilatation to delivery of the baby. Up to 2 hours in a primi, 1 hour in a multi
THIRD STAGE: delivery of the baby to delivery of the PLACENTA. Usually 5–15 minutes, and abnormal beyond 30
FOURTH STAGE: the first 1–2 hours after delivery. This is when POSTPARTUM HAEMORRHAGE happens
TRUE labour: regular, intensifying contractions, with cervical dilatation and effacement, and a show
FALSE labour: irregular contractions, no cervical change, relieved by walking or rest
cards.clinicalNote: The fourth stage is the one people forget, and it is the most dangerous hour of the whole delivery. Most postpartum haemorrhage happens in the first hour after birth, when the room has emptied and everyone has relaxed.

cards.title: Self-check: true labour or false
cards.body:
A woman at 38 weeks has irregular contractions that ease when she walks around. Her cervix is unchanged from four hours ago. True or false labour?

Answer: FALSE LABOUR. The defining feature of TRUE labour is progressive CERVICAL CHANGE (dilatation and effacement). Contractions alone, however uncomfortable, are not labour. False labour contractions are irregular and are typically relieved by walking or rest.

cards.title: Pregnancy: signs, dates and the essential checks
body: PRESUMPTIVE signs (the woman notices): amenorrhoea, nausea, breast tenderness, fatigue, quickening
PROBABLE signs (the examiner finds): a positive pregnancy test, Hegar's sign, Goodell's sign, Chadwick's sign, ballottement
POSITIVE signs (only these are certain): fetal heart sounds, fetal movements FELT BY THE EXAMINER, ultrasound visualisation of the fetus
NAEGELE'S RULE for the expected date of delivery: first day of the last menstrual period, MINUS 3 months, PLUS 7 days, plus 1 year
Example: LMP 10 January 2026 → EDD 17 October 2026
It assumes a regular 28 day cycle. Adjust it if the cycle is longer or shorter
Pregnancy is 280 days, or 40 weeks, from the first day of the LMP
cards.clinicalNote: Only three signs are POSITIVE proof of pregnancy, and all three involve detecting the FETUS itself, not the mother's symptoms. A positive urine test is only PROBABLE, because other conditions can produce hCG.

cards.title: Antenatal danger signs: teach every woman these
cards.body:
A woman who knows these signs and goes to hospital is the woman who survives. Teach them at every visit.

• BLEEDING from the vagina, at any stage
• SEVERE HEADACHE, BLURRED VISION, or seeing flashing lights
• SWELLING of the FACE and HANDS (not just the ankles)
• EPIGASTRIC pain, or pain under the right ribs
• CONVULSIONS
• REDUCED or ABSENT FETAL MOVEMENTS
• FEVER
• LEAKING of fluid from the vagina
• PERSISTENT vomiting
• Difficulty in breathing

The headache, visual disturbance, facial swelling and epigastric pain cluster together, and together they mean SEVERE PRE-ECLAMPSIA, which is minutes away from a seizure.

cards.title: Anaemia in pregnancy: India's biggest obstetric problem
cards.body:
Anaemia is the leading indirect cause of maternal death in India, and it is almost entirely preventable.

WHO classification in pregnancy (haemoglobin):
• Mild: 10–10.9 g/dL
• Moderate: 7–9.9 g/dL
• Severe: BELOW 7 g/dL
• Very severe: below 4 g/dL, this is a medical emergency

WHY PREGNANCY CAUSES IT: plasma volume rises by about 50% while red cell mass rises less, so there is a physiological dilution. Add poor iron intake, closely spaced pregnancies and hookworm, and the haemoglobin falls.

CONSEQUENCES: preterm birth, low birth weight, postpartum haemorrhage (an anaemic woman tolerates far less blood loss), heart failure, and maternal death.

PREVENTION AND TREATMENT:
• IFA one tablet daily for at least 180 days in pregnancy, and 180 days postpartum
• In anaemia, DOUBLE it to twice daily
• Deworming with albendazole after the first trimester
• Take iron with VITAMIN C (citrus, lemon), which increases absorption
• Avoid tea, coffee, milk and calcium at the same time as iron, they BLOCK absorption
cards.clinicalNote: Women take their iron tablet with their morning tea, and the tannin in the tea blocks most of the absorption. That one habit undoes six months of supplementation, and correcting it costs nothing at all.

cards.title: The physiological changes of pregnancy
body: BLOOD VOLUME rises by about 40–50%. This causes physiological anaemia of pregnancy
CARDIAC OUTPUT rises 30–50%. Heart rate rises by 10–15 beats per minute
BP FALLS in the second trimester, then returns to baseline by term. A rise is abnormal
SUPINE HYPOTENSION: lying flat lets the uterus compress the inferior vena cava. Turn her to the LEFT LATERAL position
Respiratory: the diaphragm is pushed up, tidal volume rises, and mild breathlessness is normal
GI: progesterone relaxes smooth muscle, so heartburn and CONSTIPATION are common
Urinary: frequency, and an increased risk of urinary infection
Blood is HYPERCOAGULABLE, so the risk of clot is raised throughout pregnancy and the puerperium
cards.clinicalNote: A pregnant woman who becomes pale, dizzy and hypotensive the moment you lay her flat for an examination is not fainting. Her uterus is compressing the vena cava and cutting off venous return. Roll her onto her left side and she will recover within seconds.

cards.title: Nutrition and weight gain in pregnancy
cards.body:
EXTRA REQUIREMENTS (ICMR):
• Energy: an extra 350 kcal per day in the second and third trimesters
• Protein: an extra 23 g per day
• Iron and folic acid: as supplements, diet alone is not enough
• Calcium: 1200 mg per day
• FOLIC ACID: 400 mcg daily, ideally started BEFORE conception and continued through the first trimester. It prevents NEURAL TUBE DEFECTS

WEIGHT GAIN: about 10 to 12 kg total in a woman of normal weight. Roughly 1 to 2 kg in the first trimester, then about 0.5 kg per week.

TOO LITTLE weight gain means intrauterine growth restriction and low birth weight.
SUDDEN EXCESSIVE gain, especially with facial and hand swelling, suggests PRE-ECLAMPSIA and is not simply overeating.

AVOID: alcohol, smoking, tobacco in any form, raw or undercooked meat and fish, unpasteurised milk, excess caffeine. Self medication of any kind.
cards.clinicalNote: Folic acid works by preventing the neural tube from failing to close, and that happens in the first 4 weeks, often before a woman even knows she is pregnant. Starting it when she books at 12 weeks is far too late. It has to be preconceptional to do what it is for.

cards.title: Self-check: Naegele's rule
cards.body:
A woman's last menstrual period began on 20 March 2026. What is her expected date of delivery?

Answer: 27 DECEMBER 2026. Naegele's rule: subtract 3 months from March (giving December), add 7 days to the 20th (giving the 27th), and add a year where needed. The rule assumes a regular 28 day cycle.

cards.title: Pre-eclampsia: the disease of the placenta
cards.body:
DEFINITION: new hypertension after 20 WEEKS of gestation, with PROTEINURIA or evidence of organ dysfunction.

GESTATIONAL HYPERTENSION: BP 140/90 or above after 20 weeks, WITHOUT proteinuria.

PRE-ECLAMPSIA: hypertension after 20 weeks WITH proteinuria (300 mg or more in 24 hours, or a dipstick of 1+ or more).

SEVERE FEATURES, any one of which makes it severe:
• BP 160/110 or above
• SEVERE HEADACHE, VISUAL disturbance (blurring, flashing lights)
• EPIGASTRIC or RIGHT UPPER QUADRANT pain (the liver capsule stretching)
• Pulmonary oedema, oliguria
• Low platelets, deranged liver enzymes, rising creatinine

ECLAMPSIA: pre-eclampsia PLUS a SEIZURE.

HELLP SYNDROME: Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets. A severe variant, and it can occur even when the BP is not very high.

THE ONLY CURE IS DELIVERY of the placenta. Everything else buys time.
cards.clinicalNote: Epigastric pain in a pregnant woman is not indigestion until proved otherwise. It is the liver capsule stretching in severe pre-eclampsia, and it is one of the last warnings before a seizure. Take her blood pressure immediately.

cards.title: Magnesium sulphate: the drug and its antidote
cards.body:
MAGNESIUM SULPHATE is the drug of choice to PREVENT and TREAT eclamptic seizures. It is not an antihypertensive.

BEFORE EVERY DOSE, CHECK THREE THINGS:
1. RESPIRATORY RATE must be at least 16 per minute
2. PATELLAR (knee) REFLEX must be PRESENT. Loss of the reflex is the FIRST sign of toxicity
3. URINE OUTPUT must be at least 30 mL per hour (magnesium is excreted by the kidney, so oliguria means it accumulates)

If any one fails, WITHHOLD the dose and report.

SIGNS OF TOXICITY, in the order they appear:
Loss of the patellar reflex → respiratory depression → cardiac arrest.

THE ANTIDOTE IS CALCIUM GLUCONATE. It must be immediately available at the bedside, drawn up, whenever magnesium is running.

Other care in eclampsia: a quiet, dark room, padded rails, suction and oxygen ready, left lateral position, nothing by mouth, strict input and output.

cards.title: Nursing an eclamptic seizure
body: STAY WITH HER. Call for help without leaving the bedside
Protect the airway: turn her to the LEFT LATERAL position, do NOT force anything into her mouth
Do not restrain her. Protect her from injury and pad the cot sides
Give oxygen, and have suction ready for secretions and vomit
MAGNESIUM SULPHATE as prescribed, with calcium gluconate drawn up as the antidote
Control the BP with labetalol, hydralazine or nifedipine as prescribed
Continuous fetal monitoring. Strict fluid balance, hourly urine output
Prepare for DELIVERY, which is the only cure
Seizures can occur up to 48 hours or more POSTPARTUM. Do not relax after delivery
cards.clinicalNote: The most dangerous assumption in obstetrics is that delivery ends the danger. Eclampsia can strike for the first time after the baby is born, and the woman who seizes on the postnatal ward is the one nobody was watching.

cards.title: Antihypertensives in pregnancy: safe and forbidden
cards.body:
SAFE IN PREGNANCY:
• LABETALOL (usually first line)
• METHYLDOPA (long established, very safe, but slow acting)
• NIFEDIPINE
• HYDRALAZINE (used for acute severe hypertension)

ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED:
• ACE INHIBITORS (enalapril, ramipril)
• ANGIOTENSIN RECEPTOR BLOCKERS (losartan)

WHY: they cause fetal kidney failure, oligohydramnios, skull defects and fetal death. They are TERATOGENIC and are among the most important drugs to stop the moment a woman conceives, or ideally before.

OTHER DRUGS TO AVOID IN PREGNANCY:
• WARFARIN (teratogenic. Heparin is used instead, as it does not cross the placenta)
• TETRACYCLINES (stain the fetal teeth and affect bone)
• Live vaccines
• NSAIDs in the third trimester (they close the ductus arteriosus prematurely)

The target BP is controlled, not normal. Dropping it too far reduces placental perfusion and starves the fetus.
cards.clinicalNote: A woman of childbearing age on an ACE inhibitor needs to know, before she conceives, that this drug must be changed. By the time she has a positive test and books a clinic appointment, the fetal kidneys have already been forming for weeks.

cards.title: Who is at risk of pre-eclampsia
body: FIRST pregnancy (primigravida). It is the classic risk factor
Age under 20 or over 35
A previous pregnancy with pre-eclampsia. This is the strongest single predictor
A family history of pre-eclampsia
MULTIPLE pregnancy (twins), and molar pregnancy
Pre-existing hypertension, kidney disease, diabetes, obesity
Autoimmune disease, especially antiphospholipid syndrome
A long interval since the last pregnancy, or a new partner
LOW DOSE ASPIRIN from the end of the first trimester reduces the risk in high risk women

cards.title: Self-check: the absent knee reflex
cards.body:
A woman on a magnesium sulphate infusion has lost her patellar reflex. Respiratory rate is 17. What do you do?

Answer: WITHHOLD THE MAGNESIUM AND REPORT IMMEDIATELY. Loss of the patellar reflex is the EARLIEST sign of magnesium toxicity, and it comes BEFORE respiratory depression and cardiac arrest. Have CALCIUM GLUCONATE, the antidote, ready. A normal respiratory rate does not make it safe to continue.

cards.title: Antepartum haemorrhage: placenta praevia versus abruption
cards.body:
Bleeding after 20 weeks. Telling these two apart decides whether you may examine her vaginally, and getting it wrong can kill her.

PLACENTA PRAEVIA (the placenta lies over or near the cervical os):
• PAINLESS, bright red, recurrent bleeding
• The uterus is SOFT and NON tender
• The fetal condition is usually good, and the presenting part is high or malpresenting
• NEVER PERFORM A VAGINAL EXAMINATION. You can tear the placenta and cause catastrophic haemorrhage

ABRUPTIO PLACENTAE (the placenta separates prematurely from the uterine wall):
• PAINFUL, dark red bleeding. The bleeding may be CONCEALED, so the visible loss can be small while she bleeds internally
• The uterus is TENSE, RIGID, WOODY and TENDER
• Fetal distress or death is common. The mother may go into shock out of proportion to the visible blood
• Risk of disseminated intravascular coagulation

MEMORY HOOK: PRAEVIA is PAINLESS. ABRUPTION is AGONISING.
cards.clinicalNote: The concealed abruption is the one that kills, because the visible bleeding is trivial while litres collect behind the placenta. Judge her by her shock, her rigid uterus and her pain, not by the amount of blood you can see on the pad.

cards.title: Postpartum haemorrhage: the four Ts
cards.body:
PPH is blood loss over 500 mL after a vaginal delivery, or over 1000 mL after a caesarean. It is the LEADING direct cause of maternal death.

THE CAUSES, in order of frequency:

TONE: uterine ATONY. The uterus fails to contract. This causes about 70–80% of all PPH. The uterus feels SOFT and BOGGY
TRAUMA: tears of the cervix, vagina or perineum, uterine rupture, haematoma
TISSUE: retained placenta or membranes
THROMBIN: a clotting disorder, or DIC

FIRST ACTION FOR ATONY: RUB UP A CONTRACTION. Massage the fundus firmly. Then empty the bladder (a full bladder prevents the uterus contracting), give uterotonics (oxytocin, then ergometrine, then misoprostol or carboprost), and call for help.

Bimanual compression and, if needed, surgery.

ACTIVE MANAGEMENT OF THE THIRD STAGE prevents most PPH: prophylactic OXYTOCIN 10 IU IM within one minute of birth, controlled cord traction, and uterine massage.

cards.title: Early pregnancy bleeding: the types of abortion
body: THREATENED: bleeding, cervix CLOSED, the pregnancy may still continue. Rest and observe
INEVITABLE: bleeding, cervix OPEN, the pregnancy cannot be saved
INCOMPLETE: some products passed, some retained. Bleeding continues. Needs evacuation
COMPLETE: everything passed, bleeding settles, the uterus is empty and contracted
MISSED: the fetus has died but is retained. The uterus stops growing, and symptoms of pregnancy fade
SEPTIC: infected products. Fever, offensive discharge, tenderness. A life threatening emergency
The state of the CERVICAL OS is the key to telling threatened from inevitable
ECTOPIC PREGNANCY must always be excluded: amenorrhoea, one sided abdominal pain, then collapse. It is a surgical emergency
cards.clinicalNote: Any woman of childbearing age with abdominal pain and collapse has an ectopic pregnancy until proven otherwise, whether or not she believes she could be pregnant. A ruptured ectopic bleeds into the abdomen and can exsanguinate a healthy young woman within the hour.

cards.title: Managing postpartum haemorrhage: the sequence
cards.body:
This is a drill, and it must be automatic.

1. CALL FOR HELP. Never manage a PPH alone
2. MASSAGE THE FUNDUS. Rub up a contraction. This alone stops many of them
3. EMPTY THE BLADDER. A full bladder physically prevents the uterus from contracting. Catheterise
4. UTEROTONICS: oxytocin, then ergometrine (NOT in hypertension or pre-eclampsia), then misoprostol or carboprost (NOT in asthma)
5. Two large bore IV cannulas, bloods, cross match, fluid resuscitation
6. Monitor: pulse, BP, blood loss, level of consciousness, urine output
7. Examine for TEARS and for RETAINED tissue
8. Bimanual compression, uterine balloon tamponade
9. Theatre if bleeding continues

WEIGH the pads. Visual estimation of blood loss is notoriously and dangerously inaccurate, and it is always an underestimate.

ERGOMETRINE IS CONTRAINDICATED IN HYPERTENSION. CARBOPROST IS CONTRAINDICATED IN ASTHMA. Know both, because you will be handed the ampoule.
cards.clinicalNote: Nurses and doctors underestimate blood loss by roughly half when they guess by eye. The woman who has 'lost about 300 mL' has often lost 600. Weigh the pads and swabs. It is the difference between recognising a haemorrhage and being surprised by a shocked patient.

cards.title: Rh incompatibility and anti-D
body: The risk arises when the mother is Rh NEGATIVE and the baby is Rh POSITIVE
Fetal red cells cross into the maternal circulation, and she makes antibodies against them (sensitisation)
The FIRST baby is usually safe. It is the NEXT Rh positive pregnancy that is attacked
The result is haemolytic disease of the newborn: anaemia, jaundice, hydrops fetalis, and death
ANTI-D IMMUNOGLOBULIN prevents sensitisation by mopping up the fetal cells before she reacts to them
Given routinely at 28 weeks, and again WITHIN 72 HOURS of delivery of an Rh positive baby
Also given after ANY sensitising event: miscarriage, abortion, ectopic, amniocentesis, abdominal trauma, antepartum haemorrhage, external cephalic version
The INDIRECT COOMBS test checks the mother for antibodies. The DIRECT COOMBS test checks the BABY's cells
cards.clinicalNote: The 72 hour window after delivery is absolute. Miss it, and the mother is sensitised for life, and every subsequent Rh positive pregnancy is at risk. It is one of the most consequential deadlines in obstetric nursing.

cards.title: Self-check: the boggy uterus
cards.body:
Thirty minutes after delivery a woman is bleeding heavily. The uterus feels soft and boggy. What is your FIRST action?

Answer: MASSAGE THE FUNDUS to rub up a contraction, and call for help. A soft, boggy uterus means ATONY, which causes most postpartum haemorrhage. Then empty the bladder, because a full bladder physically prevents the uterus contracting, and give uterotonics as prescribed.

cards.title: The postnatal check: what you are looking for
body: Remember BUBBLE-HE: Breasts, Uterus, Bladder, Bowels, Lochia, Episiotomy, Homan's sign, Emotional state
UTERUS: should be firm, central, and involute by about 1 cm (one finger breadth) per day
Immediately after delivery the fundus is at the umbilicus, and by day 10–14 it is no longer palpable abdominally
A uterus that is soft, high or deviated to one side usually means a FULL BLADDER. Empty it and reassess
LOCHIA RUBRA (red) days 1–4, SEROSA (pinkish brown) days 4–10, ALBA (white or yellow) days 10–28
Lochia that goes BACKWARDS in colour, becomes heavy again, or smells offensive is abnormal
TEMPERATURE above 38 °C after the first 24 hours suggests puerperal sepsis
Watch for DVT: calf pain, swelling, warmth. Pregnancy and the puerperium are hypercoagulable
cards.clinicalNote: A fundus that is high and pushed to the right is almost always a full bladder, not a haemorrhage. Catheterise or ask her to void, then feel again. The uterus cannot contract properly around a distended bladder.

cards.title: Breastfeeding: getting it right from the start
cards.body:
INITIATE within the FIRST HOUR of birth. Skin to skin contact, and the baby will often find the breast unaided.

COLOSTRUM is thick and yellow, small in volume and enormously valuable: rich in antibodies and protein. It is the baby's FIRST IMMUNISATION. It must never be discarded as unclean.

EXCLUSIVE breastfeeding for 6 MONTHS: no water, no honey, no ghutti, no formula. Then complementary feeding while breastfeeding continues to 2 years and beyond.

SIGNS OF GOOD ATTACHMENT (remember CHIN):
• Chin touching the breast
• Mouth wide open
• Lower lip turned outwards
• More areola visible ABOVE the mouth than below

SIGNS THE BABY IS GETTING ENOUGH: at least 6 wet nappies a day, regular stools, weight gain after the initial physiological loss, and the baby is settled after feeds.

COMMON PROBLEMS:
• Sore, cracked nipples: nearly always POOR ATTACHMENT. Correct the latch, do not stop feeding
• Engorgement: feed frequently, express a little, warm compress before, cold after
• MASTITIS: a red, painful, hot segment with fever and flu like symptoms. KEEP FEEDING. Continuing to empty the breast is part of the treatment, and the milk is safe for the baby
cards.clinicalNote: Mothers stop feeding from a breast with mastitis because it hurts and because it feels unsafe. It is exactly the wrong response. Stopping lets the milk stagnate and the infection worsen, and it can progress to an abscess. Keep the breast empty.

cards.title: APGAR: scoring the newborn
cards.body:
Assessed at 1 MINUTE and 5 MINUTES. Each of five items scores 0, 1 or 2.

A: APPEARANCE (colour). 0 = blue or pale, 1 = body pink with blue extremities, 2 = completely pink
P: PULSE (heart rate). 0 = absent, 1 = below 100, 2 = above 100
G: GRIMACE (reflex irritability). 0 = none, 1 = grimace, 2 = cry, cough or sneeze
A: ACTIVITY (muscle tone). 0 = limp, 1 = some flexion, 2 = active movement
R: RESPIRATION. 0 = absent, 1 = slow or irregular, 2 = good strong cry

INTERPRETATION:
7–10: normal
4–6: moderately depressed, needs some resuscitation
0–3: severely depressed, needs immediate active resuscitation

THE POINT: APGAR describes the baby's condition. It does NOT decide whether to start resuscitation. You do not wait one minute to score a baby who is not breathing. You resuscitate immediately.

cards.title: Newborn jaundice: physiological or dangerous
body: PHYSIOLOGICAL jaundice appears AFTER 24 hours, peaks at day 3–5, and settles by 1–2 weeks. It is common and usually harmless
PATHOLOGICAL jaundice appears WITHIN THE FIRST 24 HOURS. This is ALWAYS abnormal and always urgent
Jaundice in the first 24 hours suggests haemolysis: Rh or ABO incompatibility, or sepsis
Also pathological: a rapidly rising bilirubin, jaundice lasting beyond 2 weeks, or a very high level
The danger is KERNICTERUS: bilirubin crosses into the brain and causes permanent damage, deafness and cerebral palsy
Signs of kernicterus: lethargy, poor feeding, a high pitched cry, arching of the back, seizures
TREATMENT: phototherapy, and exchange transfusion in severe cases
During phototherapy: cover the EYES, leave as much skin exposed as possible, monitor temperature, ensure adequate fluid, and turn the baby regularly
cards.clinicalNote: The timing is the whole diagnosis. Jaundice on day three is usually physiological and needs watching. Jaundice in the first twenty four hours is haemolysis until proved otherwise, and it must be escalated immediately.

cards.title: Puerperal sepsis and postnatal mental health
cards.body:
PUERPERAL SEPSIS: infection of the genital tract after delivery. A leading cause of maternal death, and largely preventable.

SIGNS: fever above 38 °C after the first 24 hours, offensive lochia, lower abdominal pain, a tender and poorly involuting uterus, tachycardia, general malaise.

PREVENTION: aseptic technique in labour, hand hygiene, minimising vaginal examinations, clean delivery practices, and perineal hygiene.

POSTNATAL MENTAL HEALTH, and the distinction matters:

• BABY BLUES: very common (up to 80%). Days 3–5. Tearfulness, mood swings, anxiety. SELF LIMITING, needs reassurance and support only

• POSTNATAL DEPRESSION: 10–15%. Onset over weeks. Persistent low mood, hopelessness, inability to enjoy the baby, guilt, poor sleep even when the baby sleeps, thoughts of self harm. Needs treatment: therapy, and sometimes medication

• PUERPERAL PSYCHOSIS: rare (about 1 in 500 to 1000) but a PSYCHIATRIC EMERGENCY. Onset is often abrupt, within the first 2 weeks. Delusions, hallucinations, confusion, mania, and a real risk of harm to the mother or the baby. Needs urgent psychiatric admission
cards.clinicalNote: A mother who cannot sleep even when her baby is sleeping soundly is telling you something important. Exhaustion is universal in the postnatal period. Being unable to sleep when finally given the chance is a symptom, not fatigue.

cards.title: Self-check: jaundice at 12 hours
cards.body:
A newborn is visibly jaundiced at 12 hours of age. The mother says her first baby was jaundiced too and it settled. Is this reassuring?

Answer: NO. THIS IS PATHOLOGICAL. Jaundice appearing WITHIN THE FIRST 24 HOURS is never physiological, and it suggests haemolysis (Rh or ABO incompatibility) or sepsis. It needs urgent bilirubin measurement and investigation, because of the risk of kernicterus.

cards.title: The menstrual cycle: the phases and the hormones
body: A 28 day cycle is the model. Day 1 is the FIRST day of bleeding
MENSTRUAL phase: days 1–5. The endometrium is shed
PROLIFERATIVE (follicular) phase: days 6–14. OESTROGEN dominates and rebuilds the endometrium
OVULATION: about day 14, triggered by the LH SURGE. The ovum survives about 24 hours
SECRETORY (luteal) phase: days 15–28. The corpus luteum makes PROGESTERONE, which maintains the endometrium
If there is no fertilisation, the corpus luteum degenerates, progesterone falls, and menstruation begins
The LUTEAL phase is the fixed one, at about 14 days. It is the FOLLICULAR phase that varies between women
So in a 35 day cycle, ovulation is around day 21, not day 14. This is why 'day 14' is a dangerous assumption
cards.clinicalNote: The idea that everyone ovulates on day 14 has caused a great many unplanned pregnancies. The second half of the cycle is fixed at about 14 days, so ovulation is 14 days BEFORE the next period, not 14 days after the last one.

cards.title: Menstrual disorders: naming them correctly
cards.body:
AMENORRHOEA: absence of menstruation.
• PRIMARY: no periods by age 16. Causes include Turner syndrome, imperforate hymen, absent uterus
• SECONDARY: periods stop for 3 or more months in a woman who had them. The COMMONEST CAUSE IS PREGNANCY, and it must be excluded first, always

DYSMENORRHOEA: painful periods.
• PRIMARY: no underlying disease, caused by prostaglandins. Treated with NSAIDs, which work by blocking prostaglandin production, and with heat
• SECONDARY: caused by disease. Endometriosis, fibroids, PID, IUCD

MENORRHAGIA: heavy bleeding. Causes anaemia. Fibroids, adenomyosis, coagulopathy.
METRORRHAGIA: bleeding BETWEEN periods.
OLIGOMENORRHOEA: infrequent periods, more than 35 days apart. Classic in PCOS.

POSTMENOPAUSAL BLEEDING IS CANCER UNTIL PROVED OTHERWISE. Any bleeding after 12 months of amenorrhoea in a postmenopausal woman must be investigated, without exception and without delay.
cards.clinicalNote: Postmenopausal bleeding is the single red flag in gynaecology that must never be reassured away. It is endometrial carcinoma until an investigation says otherwise, and it is one of the cancers where early diagnosis genuinely changes the outcome.

cards.title: Menopause: what falling oestrogen does
cards.body:
Menopause is confirmed after 12 CONSECUTIVE MONTHS of amenorrhoea. Average age is around 45 to 50.

The symptoms are all consequences of falling OESTROGEN:

VASOMOTOR: HOT FLUSHES, night sweats, palpitations
UROGENITAL: vaginal dryness, itching, painful intercourse, urinary frequency and urgency, recurrent UTI
PSYCHOLOGICAL: mood swings, irritability, poor sleep, difficulty concentrating
SKELETAL: OSTEOPOROSIS. Bone loss accelerates sharply after menopause, and fracture risk rises
CARDIOVASCULAR: the protective effect of oestrogen is lost, so cardiac risk rises to match that of men

NURSING ADVICE: calcium and vitamin D, WEIGHT BEARING exercise, stop smoking, limit alcohol and caffeine, layered clothing for flushes, vaginal lubricants.

Hormone replacement therapy relieves symptoms but carries risks (clot, stroke, breast cancer), so it is an individual decision, at the lowest effective dose for the shortest necessary time.

cards.title: Cervical and breast cancer: screening and signs
body: CERVICAL cancer is caused by persistent HPV infection, mainly types 16 and 18
It is one of the few cancers that is both PREVENTABLE (HPV vaccine) and SCREENABLE (Pap smear)
PAP SMEAR: from age 21, or 3 years after first intercourse. Every 3 years, or with HPV co-testing every 5
Do NOT take a Pap during menstruation. No douching, intercourse or vaginal medication for 48 hours before
Symptoms: POSTCOITAL bleeding, intermenstrual bleeding, offensive discharge. Early disease has NO symptoms
VIA (visual inspection with acetic acid) is the low cost screening method used in India
BREAST self examination: monthly, about a week after the period starts, when the breasts are least lumpy
Breast red flags: a hard fixed painless lump, skin dimpling (peau d'orange), nipple retraction, bloody discharge, an axillary lump
cards.clinicalNote: The classic cancerous breast lump is HARD, IRREGULAR, FIXED and PAINLESS, and the painlessness is exactly why women delay. A tender, mobile, smooth lump that changes with the cycle is far more likely to be benign, but any new lump is examined, not reassured over the phone.

cards.title: Pelvic inflammatory disease and infertility
cards.body:
PID is infection of the upper genital tract: uterus, tubes and ovaries. Usually from a sexually transmitted infection, most often chlamydia or gonorrhoea, ascending from the cervix.

SIGNS: lower abdominal pain, deep pain on intercourse, offensive discharge, fever, CERVICAL EXCITATION (severe pain when the cervix is moved on examination), abnormal bleeding.

WHY IT MATTERS SO MUCH: it SCARS THE FALLOPIAN TUBES, and that causes:
• INFERTILITY
• ECTOPIC pregnancy (the scarred tube traps the embryo)
• Chronic pelvic pain

Each episode of PID roughly doubles the risk of infertility. It is a disease where prompt treatment genuinely preserves a woman's future.

TREATMENT: prompt antibiotics, and TREAT THE PARTNER, or she will simply be reinfected. Abstain during treatment.

PREVENTION: condoms, screening, early treatment of sexually transmitted infection.
cards.clinicalNote: Treating the woman and not her partner is a guarantee of reinfection, and each reinfection scars the tubes further. Partner notification feels intrusive and difficult, and it is the part of the treatment that protects her fertility.

cards.title: Self-check: bleeding after menopause
cards.body:
A 58 year old woman, 6 years postmenopausal, reports light vaginal spotting. She says it is 'nothing much'. What is your response?

Answer: URGENT REFERRAL. POSTMENOPAUSAL BLEEDING IS ENDOMETRIAL CANCER UNTIL PROVED OTHERWISE. There is no amount of bleeding after the menopause that is normal, and the volume is irrelevant. It requires investigation, not reassurance.

cards.title: Mortality Indicators (India approx.)
cards.body:
IMR (Infant Mortality Rate): ~28 per 1000 live births
NMR (Neonatal): ~20 per 1000 live births
U5MR (Under-5): ~32 per 1000 live births
MMR (Maternal): ~97 per 100,000 live births

Key definition trick. IMR/NMR/U5MR use /1000 LIVE BIRTHS. MMR uses /100,000 LIVE BIRTHS. Crude death rate uses /1000 POPULATION.

cards.title: Levels of Prevention: Quick Mapping
cards.body:
PRIMORDIAL → eliminate the risk factor (e.g., healthy lifestyle in whole society)
PRIMARY → prevent disease before onset (immunisation, sanitation)
SECONDARY → early detection in asymptomatic (Pap smear, BP screening)
TERTIARY → limit disability after disease (rehab, dialysis)

cards.title: The core health indicators and their formulas
body: INFANT MORTALITY RATE: deaths under 1 year per 1000 LIVE BIRTHS in a year
NEONATAL MORTALITY RATE: deaths in the first 28 days per 1000 live births
MATERNAL MORTALITY RATIO: maternal deaths per 1,00,000 LIVE BIRTHS
UNDER-5 MORTALITY RATE: deaths under 5 years per 1000 live births
CRUDE BIRTH RATE: live births per 1000 MID YEAR POPULATION
CRUDE DEATH RATE: deaths per 1000 mid year population
TOTAL FERTILITY RATE: average children a woman would bear in her lifetime. Replacement level is 2.1
The IMR is regarded as the single most sensitive indicator of a country's overall health and development
cards.clinicalNote: Notice the denominators, because that is what examiners test. Mortality rates for infants use LIVE BIRTHS. Crude birth and death rates use the MID YEAR POPULATION. Maternal mortality is per 1,00,000, not per 1000.

cards.title: Levels of prevention: primordial to tertiary
cards.body:
PRIMORDIAL: stop the RISK FACTOR from ever appearing. Discouraging children from ever taking up smoking

PRIMARY: stop the DISEASE from occurring in a healthy person. IMMUNISATION, health education, safe water

SECONDARY: EARLY DETECTION and prompt treatment, so the disease does not progress. SCREENING (Pap smear, mammography, blood pressure checks)

TERTIARY: LIMIT DISABILITY and rehabilitate once the disease is established. Physiotherapy after a stroke, a prosthetic limb

The examinable line: a screening test is SECONDARY prevention, not primary. Immunisation is PRIMARY.

cards.title: Self-check: which level of prevention
cards.body:
A community Pap smear camp for cervical cancer screening. Which level of prevention is this?

Answer: SECONDARY PREVENTION. Screening detects disease EARLY in people who already have it but do not yet know. Primary prevention would be the HPV vaccine, which stops the disease occurring at all.

cards.title: Key Programmes & Their Focus
cards.body:
RNTCP / NTEP – Tuberculosis (DOTS)
NVBDCP – Vector-borne (malaria, dengue, filaria)
NLEP – Leprosy
RCH / RMNCH+A – Reproductive & child health
UIP – Universal Immunisation
NPCDCS – Non-communicable diseases (diabetes, cancer, stroke)
NMHP – Mental health

cards.title: Quick Facts
body: DOTS = Directly Observed Treatment, Short-course, cornerstone of TB control
India aims to ELIMINATE TB ahead of the global 2030 SDG target
ASHA = Accredited Social Health Activist, the village-level link worker
Cold chain keeps vaccines viable from manufacturer to child
Sub-centre is the first/most peripheral contact point in rural health

cards.title: The national programmes and what they target
body: NTEP (formerly RNTCP): tuberculosis. Free diagnosis and treatment, directly observed therapy
NVBDCP: vector borne diseases. Malaria, dengue, chikungunya, filaria, kala azar, Japanese encephalitis
NACP: HIV and AIDS. Prevention, testing, free antiretroviral therapy
NPCB: blindness control. Cataract surgery is the largest component
NLEP: leprosy eradication, using multidrug therapy
RMNCH+A: reproductive, maternal, newborn, child and adolescent health
NPCDCS: non communicable diseases. Cancer, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, stroke
JANANI SURAKSHA YOJANA: cash incentive promoting INSTITUTIONAL delivery
AYUSHMAN BHARAT: health and wellness centres, plus PM-JAY health insurance

cards.title: The health worker hierarchy in rural India
cards.body:
Know who serves how many people. This is asked constantly.

ASHA (Accredited Social Health Activist): about 1 per 1000 population. A community volunteer, the vital link to the health system

ANM (Auxiliary Nurse Midwife): at the SUB CENTRE

SUB CENTRE: 5000 population in the plains, 3000 in hilly, tribal and difficult areas

PRIMARY HEALTH CENTRE (PHC): 30,000 in the plains, 20,000 in hilly and tribal areas. Has a medical officer

COMMUNITY HEALTH CENTRE (CHC): 1,20,000 in the plains, 80,000 in hilly and tribal areas. Has specialists and is a 30 bed referral unit

The pattern to remember: the difficult-terrain figure is always LOWER, because people are harder to reach.

cards.title: Self-check: the sub centre population
cards.body:
What population does one sub centre serve in a plains area, and in a hilly or tribal area?

Answer: 5000 IN THE PLAINS, 3000 IN HILLY, TRIBAL AND DIFFICULT AREAS. The figure is always lower in difficult terrain, because the population is scattered and harder to reach. The same logic applies to the PHC (30,000 and 20,000) and the CHC (1,20,000 and 80,000).

cards.title: Incidence and prevalence: the difference that matters
cards.body:
INCIDENCE: the number of NEW cases in a defined period. It measures the RISK of getting the disease.

PREVALENCE: the number of EXISTING cases (old and new) at a point in time. It measures the BURDEN of the disease.

PREVALENCE = INCIDENCE × DURATION.

This relationship explains a lot:
• A disease that kills quickly (rabies) has a high incidence but a LOW prevalence, because cases do not persist
• A chronic incurable disease (diabetes) has a modest incidence but a HIGH prevalence, because cases accumulate
• A new treatment that keeps people alive longer without curing them will RAISE prevalence, even while incidence falls

Incidence is used for ACUTE disease and to study causation. Prevalence is used for CHRONIC disease and to plan services.
cards.clinicalNote: If a treatment improves survival without curing anyone, prevalence goes UP, and to the untrained eye that looks like the health system is failing. Understanding the difference stops you drawing exactly the wrong conclusion from the data.

cards.title: Sensitivity and specificity: reading a screening test
body: SENSITIVITY: the ability to correctly identify those WITH the disease. It rules disease OUT when negative
SPECIFICITY: the ability to correctly identify those WITHOUT it. It rules disease IN when positive
A highly SENSITIVE test has few FALSE NEGATIVES. Good for SCREENING: you do not want to miss anyone
A highly SPECIFIC test has few FALSE POSITIVES. Good for CONFIRMING a diagnosis
Screening tests are sensitive. Confirmatory tests are specific
Memory hook: SnNOUT (a Sensitive test, when Negative, rules OUT). SpPIN (a Specific test, when Positive, rules IN)
A screening test is NOT a diagnosis. A positive screen must always be confirmed

cards.title: The epidemiological triad
cards.body:
Disease arises from the interaction of THREE things:

AGENT: the organism or cause (bacterium, virus, toxin, deficiency)
HOST: the person (age, immunity, nutrition, genetics, behaviour)
ENVIRONMENT: the surroundings (water, sanitation, housing, vectors, crowding)

Break ANY of the three and you break the disease.

This is why public health works on the environment (clean water, sanitation), the host (immunisation, nutrition) and the agent (antibiotics, vector control) all at once.

Add TIME and you have the classic epidemiological question set: what is the agent, who is the host, where is the environment, and when did it happen?

cards.title: Types of epidemiological study
cards.body:
DESCRIPTIVE: describes TIME, PLACE and PERSON. Who is getting it, where, and when? It generates the hypothesis.

ANALYTICAL: tests the hypothesis.
• CASE CONTROL: start with the DISEASE, look BACKWARDS for the exposure. Retrospective. Good for RARE diseases. Quick and cheap. Gives an ODDS RATIO
• COHORT: start with the EXPOSURE, follow FORWARDS to see who gets the disease. Prospective. Expensive and slow. Gives RELATIVE RISK and true incidence

EXPERIMENTAL: the investigator ASSIGNS the exposure.
• RANDOMISED CONTROLLED TRIAL: the gold standard for proving that a treatment works

Memory hook: Case control looks BACK from the disease. Cohort looks FORWARD from the exposure.
cards.clinicalNote: The reason a case control study is used for rare disease is arithmetic. To study a disease affecting one person in ten thousand by following a cohort forward, you would need to enrol and follow an enormous population for years. Starting from the cases that already exist is the only practical route.

cards.title: Outbreak investigation: the steps
body: 1. Confirm the DIAGNOSIS and confirm that an OUTBREAK is genuinely occurring
2. Define a CASE. You cannot count what you have not defined
3. Search for and COUNT the cases
4. Describe by TIME (the epidemic curve), PLACE (a spot map) and PERSON
5. Form a HYPOTHESIS about the source and the mode of spread
6. TEST the hypothesis, usually with a case control study
7. CONTROL measures: isolate cases, treat, immunise, protect the water, control the vector
8. Report, and communicate to the public and the authorities
A POINT SOURCE epidemic curve rises and falls sharply from a single exposure. A PROPAGATED curve has successive waves as it spreads person to person

cards.title: Self-check: SnNOUT
cards.body:
You want a test to SCREEN a whole population for a serious disease. Do you prioritise sensitivity or specificity, and why?

Answer: SENSITIVITY. A screening test must not MISS anyone with the disease, so it needs few false negatives. False positives are acceptable, because everyone who screens positive goes on to a more SPECIFIC confirmatory test. SnNOUT: a sensitive test, when negative, rules the disease out.

cards.title: The national immunisation schedule
body: BIRTH: BCG, OPV-0, Hepatitis B birth dose
6 WEEKS: OPV-1, Pentavalent-1, Rotavirus-1, fIPV-1, PCV-1
10 WEEKS: OPV-2, Pentavalent-2, Rotavirus-2
