14 WEEKS: OPV-3, Pentavalent-3, Rotavirus-3, fIPV-2, PCV-2
9–12 MONTHS: Measles-Rubella 1st dose, PCV booster, JE-1 (in endemic districts), Vitamin A 1st dose
16–24 MONTHS: MR 2nd dose, DPT booster-1, OPV booster, JE-2
5–6 YEARS: DPT booster-2
10 YEARS and 16 YEARS: Td
PENTAVALENT covers five: diphtheria, pertussis, tetanus, hepatitis B and Haemophilus influenzae b
cards.clinicalNote: The birth doses (BCG, OPV zero, hepatitis B) are the ones most often missed for babies born at home, and they are exactly the doses that need to be given as early as possible. Any contact with a newborn is an opportunity to check them.

cards.title: Cold chain: the temperatures that must not slip
cards.body:
The cold chain is the system that keeps vaccines potent from manufacture to the child's arm.

+2 to +8 °C: the standard refrigerator range for MOST vaccines
−15 to −25 °C: deep freezer, for OPV and measles at higher storage levels

WHICH VACCINES MUST NEVER FREEZE:
The 'T series' and the liquid ones: DPT, TT, Td, Hepatitis B, PCV, Pentavalent, IPV, Rotavirus.
Freezing DESTROYS them, and a frozen vaccine looks completely normal.

WHICH TOLERATE FREEZING: OPV and measles/MR are the most heat sensitive and are the ones stored in the freezer compartment.

IN THE ILR (refrigerator): the most heat sensitive vaccines go in the COLDEST part (the basket at the bottom), and the freeze sensitive ones go AWAY from the freezing plate.

SHAKE TEST: if you suspect a vaccine has frozen, shake it and watch for sedimentation. A frozen and thawed vaccine settles fast and is discarded.

cards.title: Types of immunity and types of vaccine
cards.body:
ACTIVE immunity: the body makes its OWN antibodies. Slow to develop, but LONG lasting.
• Natural: having the infection
• Artificial: VACCINATION

PASSIVE immunity: ready made antibodies are GIVEN. Immediate protection, but SHORT lived.
• Natural: maternal antibodies across the placenta and in breast milk
• Artificial: immunoglobulin, antitoxin, antivenom

VACCINE TYPES:
• LIVE ATTENUATED (BCG, OPV, measles, MMR, varicella): strong, lasting immunity from one or two doses. CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy and in the immunocompromised
• KILLED / INACTIVATED (IPV, rabies, hepatitis A): safer, but need boosters
• TOXOID (tetanus, diphtheria): the inactivated toxin, not the organism
• SUBUNIT / CONJUGATE (Hepatitis B, Hib, PCV): a fragment of the organism

A post exposure situation often needs BOTH: the vaccine for lasting immunity, and immunoglobulin for immediate cover while the vaccine takes effect.
cards.clinicalNote: Live vaccines are the ones with real contraindications: pregnancy, immunosuppression, and high dose steroids. Getting that list wrong in either direction either harms a vulnerable patient or denies a healthy one protection they need.

cards.title: Adverse events following immunisation
body: MINOR and EXPECTED: mild fever, soreness and swelling at the site, irritability, a small nodule
Manage with paracetamol, a cold compress and reassurance. These are NOT a reason to stop the schedule
BCG: a papule appears in 2–3 weeks, ulcerates, then heals into a scar over 6–12 weeks. THIS IS NORMAL
A BCG lesion is not an abscess and must not be squeezed, dressed with antiseptic or treated as infection
SEVERE: anaphylaxis, high fever with seizures, persistent inconvincible screaming, a large local abscess
ANAPHYLAXIS: adrenaline must always be available wherever vaccines are given
REPORT all serious adverse events. It is a legal and public health responsibility
Contraindications are FEW. A mild illness, a low grade fever, or malnutrition are NOT reasons to delay
cards.clinicalNote: Far more children are harmed by a missed vaccination than by a mild fever after one. Parents and staff both over-diagnose contraindications. A snuffly, slightly warm child should still be immunised.

cards.title: Herd immunity and why coverage matters
cards.body:
When enough of a population is immune, the organism cannot find enough susceptible people to keep spreading. The chain of transmission breaks, and even the UNVACCINATED are protected.

This is what protects the people who CANNOT be vaccinated:
• Newborns too young for the vaccine
• The immunocompromised, and those on chemotherapy
• People with a genuine contraindication

The threshold depends on how infectious the disease is. MEASLES is one of the most infectious diseases known, and needs coverage of around 95% to interrupt transmission. That is why measles is the FIRST disease to come back when coverage slips.

Herd immunity only works for diseases spread PERSON TO PERSON. It does not protect against tetanus, because tetanus comes from the soil, not from other people. Every individual needs their own tetanus protection.
cards.clinicalNote: Tetanus is the exception that reveals the principle. No amount of community coverage protects you, because you catch it from a contaminated wound, not from your neighbour. Herd immunity is about breaking person to person chains.

cards.title: Self-check: the frozen vaccine
cards.body:
A vial of hepatitis B vaccine has accidentally been frozen and thawed. It looks completely normal. Can it be used?

Answer: NO. DISCARD IT. Hepatitis B is a freeze sensitive vaccine, and freezing destroys its potency, though the vial looks unchanged. Use the SHAKE TEST if in doubt: a frozen and thawed vaccine sediments rapidly. Giving it means the child is unprotected while everyone believes they are covered.

cards.title: Contraceptive methods and their key facts
body: BARRIER (condom): the ONLY method that also prevents sexually transmitted infections and HIV
COMBINED ORAL PILL: contains oestrogen and progesterone. Contraindicated in smokers over 35, and in those with a history of clot, migraine with aura, or breast cancer
PROGESTERONE ONLY PILL: safe in BREASTFEEDING, and where oestrogen is contraindicated
INJECTABLE (DMPA): every 3 months. Delayed return of fertility, and it reduces bone density
IUCD (Copper-T 380A): effective for up to 10 years. Increases menstrual bleeding and cramping
LNG-IUS: releases progesterone, and it DECREASES bleeding
IMPLANT: 3 years, highly effective
STERILISATION: permanent. Tubectomy for women, VASECTOMY for men, which is simpler and safer
EMERGENCY PILL: within 72 hours, and the sooner the better. It is NOT an abortion pill
cards.clinicalNote: Vasectomy is simpler, cheaper and safer than tubectomy, and it is far less often chosen. That gap is cultural, not medical, and a nurse who can discuss it plainly and without embarrassment does real good.

cards.title: IUCD warning signs: PAINS
cards.body:
Teach every IUCD user to return if she notices:

P: Period late, or any sign of pregnancy. Abnormal spotting or bleeding
A: Abdominal pain, or pain on intercourse
I: Infection, an abnormal or foul smelling discharge
N: Not feeling well, fever, chills
S: String missing, shorter or longer than before

Teach her to CHECK THE STRING after every period. A missing string may mean the device has been expelled, or has perforated and migrated.

The copper IUCD does NOT protect against sexually transmitted infection, and PID is a real risk in someone with multiple partners.

cards.title: Combined pill: the rules that make it work
cards.body:
HOW IT WORKS: it suppresses ovulation, thickens cervical mucus and thins the endometrium.

A MISSED PILL:
• ONE pill missed: take it as soon as you remember, even if that means two in one day. Continue as normal. No extra protection needed
• TWO OR MORE missed: take the most recent one, continue the pack, and use CONDOMS for the next 7 days. Emergency contraception may be needed if there was sex in the preceding week

WHAT REDUCES ITS EFFECTIVENESS:
• VOMITING or severe DIARRHOEA within a few hours of taking it, the pill may not have been absorbed
• Enzyme inducing drugs, especially RIFAMPICIN and some anticonvulsants

DANGER SIGNS, remember ACHES:
A: Abdominal pain (severe)
C: Chest pain, breathlessness
H: Headache (severe)
E: Eye problems, blurred vision, loss of vision
S: Severe leg pain or swelling in the calf

Each of these suggests a CLOT, and the pill must be stopped and the patient seen urgently.
cards.clinicalNote: The rifampicin interaction is easy to miss and it has caused many unplanned pregnancies. A woman starting TB treatment must be told plainly that her pill may no longer work, and offered an alternative method.

cards.title: Counselling: what makes family planning work
body: The choice belongs to the COUPLE. Your job is to inform, not to persuade
PRIVACY and CONFIDENTIALITY are essential, or people will not speak honestly
Explain how each method works, its effectiveness, side effects and how to use it
Explain what to do when something goes wrong, before it goes wrong
Only CONDOMS protect against sexually transmitted infection, so dual protection may be needed
Discuss return of fertility. This is the commonest unspoken fear
Correct the myths directly. Fear of weakness, of permanent infertility and of loss of sexual function drives most refusals
Follow up matters. Most discontinuation happens because of unmanaged side effects nobody warned them about

cards.title: Spacing, and why it saves lives
cards.body:
Birth spacing is not merely a convenience. It is one of the highest impact interventions in maternal and child health.

RECOMMENDED:
• At least 24 months between a live birth and the next conception
• At least 6 months after a miscarriage or abortion
• Delay the first pregnancy until at least 20 years of age

WHY:
• Pregnancies close together deplete the mother's iron and nutrient stores, so she becomes anaemic, and anaemia is a leading contributor to maternal death
• Short spacing raises the risk of PRETERM birth, LOW BIRTH WEIGHT and infant death
• Teenage pregnancy carries a much higher risk of obstructed labour, eclampsia, fistula and death

LACTATIONAL AMENORRHOEA is a genuine method, but only under three strict conditions ALL at once: the baby is under 6 months, breastfeeding is EXCLUSIVE and on demand, and menstruation has NOT returned. If any one condition fails, another method is needed.
cards.clinicalNote: Many women believe that breastfeeding alone protects them completely, indefinitely. It protects only while all three conditions hold, and the moment periods return, or formula is introduced, that protection is gone. Saying so explicitly prevents an unplanned pregnancy.

cards.title: Self-check: which method while breastfeeding
cards.body:
A woman who is exclusively breastfeeding a 2 month old wants contraception. Is the combined oral pill appropriate?

Answer: NO. The oestrogen in the combined pill can reduce milk supply. The PROGESTERONE ONLY PILL is the appropriate oral choice while breastfeeding, and it does not affect lactation. An IUCD or an implant would also be suitable.

cards.title: Incubation periods worth memorising
body: Measles: 10–14 days. Infectious 4 days BEFORE to 4 days AFTER the rash appears
Chickenpox: 14–16 days. Infectious 1–2 days before the rash until all lesions crust
Mumps: 16–18 days
Rubella: 14–21 days. Dangerous in the FIRST TRIMESTER of pregnancy
Diphtheria: 2–5 days
Pertussis: 7–10 days
Tetanus: 3–21 days. A shorter incubation means a worse prognosis
Hepatitis A: 15–45 days, faecal-oral. Hepatitis B: 45–180 days, blood and body fluid
Rabies: 1–3 months, but it varies widely. Once symptoms appear, it is almost 100% fatal
cards.clinicalNote: Measles is infectious for four days BEFORE the rash appears, which is precisely why it spreads so efficiently. By the time you can see it and isolate the child, they have already exposed the entire waiting room.

cards.title: Rabies: the wound is the treatment
cards.body:
Rabies is nearly 100% fatal once symptoms begin, and nearly 100% PREVENTABLE if the wound is managed properly.

CATEGORY 1: touching or feeding an animal, licks on INTACT skin → NO treatment needed
CATEGORY 2: nibbling of uncovered skin, minor scratches WITHOUT bleeding → wound care + VACCINE
CATEGORY 3: single or multiple transdermal bites, scratches WITH bleeding, licks on BROKEN skin, any bat contact, any mucous membrane contact → wound care + VACCINE + RABIES IMMUNOGLOBULIN

WOUND CARE IS THE FIRST AND MOST IMPORTANT STEP:
WASH with soap and RUNNING WATER for at least 15 MINUTES. This alone removes and kills much of the virus.
Then apply an antiseptic (povidone iodine or alcohol).

DO NOT suture the wound. DO NOT apply irritants, chilli, turmeric or mud.
DO NOT delay: give the immunoglobulin infiltrated INTO and around the wound.

cards.title: Malaria and dengue: telling them apart
cards.body:
Both cause fever in the same season and the same places, and confusing them is dangerous.

MALARIA (Plasmodium, spread by the female ANOPHELES mosquito, which bites at NIGHT):
• Fever with CHILLS and RIGORS, in a periodic pattern, followed by drenching sweats
• Anaemia, splenomegaly
• Diagnosis: peripheral blood smear, or a rapid diagnostic test
• Falciparum is the dangerous species: cerebral malaria, kidney failure, death

DENGUE (a virus, spread by the AEDES mosquito, which bites in the DAYTIME and breeds in CLEAN, stored water):
• High fever, SEVERE headache, RETRO-ORBITAL pain (behind the eyes), severe joint and muscle pain
• A rash. It is called breakbone fever for a reason
• The danger is the FALL in PLATELETS and PLASMA LEAKAGE, leading to dengue shock syndrome
• WARNING SIGNS: severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, bleeding, lethargy, a rapidly falling platelet count
• NEVER GIVE ASPIRIN OR NSAIDs in dengue. They worsen bleeding. Use PARACETAMOL only
cards.clinicalNote: The aspirin rule in dengue is a genuine killer when broken. Platelets are already falling, and an antiplatelet drug on top of that turns a survivable illness into a haemorrhage. Paracetamol only, and say it to the family, because they will otherwise buy a painkiller themselves.

cards.title: Vector control: breaking the chain
body: AEDES (dengue, chikungunya): breeds in CLEAN, STORED water. Coolers, tyres, flowerpots, overhead tanks
Aedes bites in the DAYTIME, so bed nets alone do not protect you
ANOPHELES (malaria): breeds in clean, slow moving natural water. Bites at NIGHT, so insecticide treated bed nets work well
CULEX (filaria, Japanese encephalitis): breeds in DIRTY, polluted, stagnant water and drains
Source reduction is the most effective single measure: empty, cover and clean all water containers WEEKLY
Insecticide treated bed nets, indoor residual spraying, larvicides
Personal protection: full sleeves, repellents, screened windows
Community participation is essential. A single untreated container reinfests a whole street

cards.title: Isolation, quarantine and notification
cards.body:
ISOLATION: separating the SICK, so they do not infect others. It lasts as long as they are infectious.

QUARANTINE: restricting the movement of the WELL BUT EXPOSED, for the maximum incubation period of the disease, to see whether they develop it.

The difference is examined constantly: isolate the ill, quarantine the exposed.

NOTIFIABLE DISEASES must be reported to the public health authority by law, so that contacts can be traced and an outbreak contained. They include cholera, plague, yellow fever, TB, measles, diphtheria, pertussis, polio, rabies, dengue, malaria, hepatitis, HIV and COVID-19.

CONTACT TRACING: identify everyone exposed, then observe, test, treat or immunise them as appropriate. It is the core public health tool that turns single cases into contained ones.

DISINFECTION:
• CONCURRENT: carried out while the patient is still infectious, throughout the illness
• TERMINAL: carried out after they recover, die or are discharged
cards.clinicalNote: Notification feels like paperwork on a busy shift and it is the actual mechanism by which outbreaks are stopped. The case you do not notify is the outbreak nobody investigates until twenty people are sick.

cards.title: Self-check: fever with a falling platelet count
cards.body:
A patient with dengue has a headache and asks for the aspirin they take at home. What do you tell them?

Answer: NO ASPIRIN, AND NO NSAIDs. Dengue causes the platelet count to fall and the vessels to leak, and aspirin inhibits platelets on top of that, dramatically increasing the risk of serious haemorrhage. PARACETAMOL only, and the family must be told explicitly, because they will otherwise buy a painkiller themselves.

cards.title: Safe water: purification and its standards
body: Water borne diseases: cholera, typhoid, hepatitis A and E, dysentery, polio, giardiasis
HOUSEHOLD purification: boiling (a ROLLING boil for 5–10 minutes is the most reliable), chlorination, filtration, SODIS (solar disinfection)
CHLORINATION: contact time of at least 1 hour. Residual free chlorine should be 0.5 mg/L at the point of delivery
The residual chlorine is what protects the water on its way to the tap, not just in the tank
Bleaching powder should contain about 33% available chlorine when fresh, and it deteriorates in storage
LARGE SCALE purification: storage, filtration (slow sand or rapid sand), then chlorination
Coliform count is the indicator of faecal contamination. E. coli in drinking water means faecal pollution
SAFE minimum: about 40–50 litres per person per day for domestic use

cards.title: The faecal-oral route: the six Fs
cards.body:
Faecal-oral disease travels by six routes, and each one is a place to intervene:

FINGERS (unwashed hands)
FLIES
FLUIDS (contaminated water)
FOOD
FOMITES (contaminated objects and surfaces)
FIELDS / FLOORS (soil contaminated by open defecation)

BREAK ANY OF THEM AND YOU BREAK THE DISEASE.

The two highest impact interventions are HANDWASHING WITH SOAP and SANITATION (safe disposal of human excreta). Together they prevent more childhood diarrhoea than any drug.

A SANITARY LATRINE must be at least 15 METRES away from and DOWNHILL of any water source, so seepage cannot contaminate the well.

cards.title: Waste management: the colour coded bins
cards.body:
Biomedical waste is segregated AT THE POINT OF GENERATION, by the person who creates it. That is you, at the bedside, not a cleaner later.

YELLOW: human and animal anatomical waste, soiled waste (dressings, cotton contaminated with blood), expired medicines, chemical waste, laboratory waste. Treated by INCINERATION or deep burial.

RED: contaminated recyclable plastic. Tubing, IV sets, catheters, urine bags, syringes WITHOUT needles, gloves. Treated by autoclaving or microwaving, then recycled.

WHITE (translucent, puncture proof): SHARPS. Needles, blades, scalpels. Never recap a needle. Never fill beyond three quarters.

BLUE (puncture proof, cardboard): broken or discarded GLASS, and metallic implants.

THE RULES:
• NEVER RECAP a used needle. Most needlestick injuries happen during recapping
• Never overfill a sharps container
• Segregate at the bedside, not at the disposal point
• Never mix categories: one wrongly binned item contaminates the whole bag
cards.clinicalNote: Almost every needlestick injury in a hospital is preventable, and most happen at the moment a tired nurse tries to put the cap back on a used needle. The needle goes directly into the sharps box, uncapped, straight from the patient.

cards.title: Nutrition and environment: the deficiency diseases
body: IODINE deficiency: goitre, cretinism, still the commonest preventable cause of intellectual disability. Prevented by IODISED SALT
Iodised salt must be stored in a DRY, CLOSED container, away from heat, and added AFTER cooking. Iodine evaporates
FLUORIDE: too little causes dental caries, too much causes DENTAL and SKELETAL FLUOROSIS. The safe level is about 0.5–1.0 mg/L
ARSENIC in groundwater causes skin lesions and cancers. A major problem in parts of West Bengal and Bihar
Indoor AIR POLLUTION from biomass cooking fuel causes COPD and pneumonia, and it affects women and children most
Improved cookstoves and clean fuel are public health interventions, not merely comfort
cards.clinicalNote: Telling a family to use iodised salt is not enough. If they add it at the start of cooking, or store it in an open jar next to the stove, most of the iodine is gone before it reaches the child. It is added at the end, and stored closed and dry.

cards.title: Housing, sanitation and disease
cards.body:
Overcrowding and poor housing are direct causes of disease, not merely a backdrop.

OVERCROWDING drives airborne spread: TUBERCULOSIS, measles, meningitis, respiratory infection. The standard is at least 50 square feet of floor space per person, with adequate ventilation and cross ventilation.

POOR SANITATION drives the faecal-oral diseases: cholera, typhoid, hepatitis, worm infestation, diarrhoea. Open defecation contaminates soil and water, and hookworm enters through the bare skin of the foot.

POOR VENTILATION and biomass smoke drive COPD, and increase TB transmission.

UNSAFE WATER STORAGE breeds Aedes mosquitoes and causes dengue.

This is why community health nursing is not simply clinical care delivered outdoors. A nurse who treats a child's diarrhoea but never looks at the family's water source will treat that same child again next month.
cards.clinicalNote: The home visit is where community nursing actually happens. You see the water storage, the latrine, the cooking smoke and the crowding, and every one of those explains a diagnosis you would otherwise treat over and over without ever curing.

cards.title: Self-check: residual chlorine
cards.body:
Why does treated drinking water need a RESIDUAL free chlorine level of 0.5 mg/L, when it has already been disinfected?

Answer: TO PROTECT IT ON THE WAY TO THE TAP. Disinfection at the plant kills what is in the water then. The residual chlorine keeps working through the distribution pipes and household storage, where recontamination from leaks and dirty containers actually happens.

cards.title: Antenatal care under the national programme
body: At least 4 antenatal visits are the minimum standard, and 8 contacts are now recommended
1st visit: as EARLY as possible, within the first trimester. Register the pregnancy
Weight, BP, haemoglobin, urine for protein and sugar, blood group, VDRL, HIV, fundal height, fetal heart
IFA: one tablet daily (100 mg elemental iron, 500 mcg folic acid) for at least 180 days
In ANAEMIA, the IFA dose is DOUBLED to twice daily
CALCIUM supplementation from the second trimester
TETANUS: Td, two doses one month apart, or one booster if she was immunised in the last 3 years
Danger signs to teach: bleeding, severe headache, blurred vision, swelling of face and hands, reduced fetal movement, fever, leaking fluid
cards.clinicalNote: The single most useful thing an antenatal visit produces is not a number in a book. It is a woman who knows the danger signs and knows where to go. A woman who recognises the headache of pre-eclampsia at home is the one who reaches hospital in time.

cards.title: IMNCI danger signs in a sick child
cards.body:
Any ONE of these GENERAL DANGER SIGNS means URGENT referral, whatever else is going on:

• Unable to DRINK or BREASTFEED
• VOMITS EVERYTHING
• CONVULSIONS
• LETHARGIC or UNCONSCIOUS

The IMNCI colour code:
PINK: urgent referral to hospital
YELLOW: treat at the health facility, and teach the mother home care
GREEN: home management and advice

FAST BREATHING is the key sign of PNEUMONIA in a child, and the cut off changes with age:
• Under 2 months: 60 breaths per minute or more
• 2 to 12 months: 50 or more
• 12 months to 5 years: 40 or more

CHEST INDRAWING means SEVERE pneumonia and urgent referral.

cards.title: Diarrhoea in a child: ORS and zinc
cards.body:
Diarrhoea kills children through DEHYDRATION, not through the infection itself, and it is almost entirely preventable with a sachet that costs a few rupees.

ORS (low osmolarity): the treatment. It replaces water and electrolytes and works by the sodium-glucose co-transport mechanism, which keeps working even in a badly inflamed gut.

ZINC: 20 mg daily for 14 DAYS (10 mg if under 6 months). It shortens the episode, reduces its severity, and reduces the chance of another episode in the next 2 to 3 months. The full 14 days matters, even after the diarrhoea has stopped.

CONTINUE FEEDING. Continue BREASTFEEDING throughout. Withholding food prolongs the illness and worsens malnutrition. This is one of the most damaging myths in child health.

DO NOT give antidiarrhoeals or antibiotics routinely. Antibiotics are only for dysentery (blood in the stool) and cholera.

ASSESSING DEHYDRATION: sunken eyes, absence of tears, dry mouth, thirst, skin pinch that goes back slowly, lethargy, reduced urine output.
cards.clinicalNote: The instruction to keep feeding is the one families resist most, because every instinct says to rest the gut. Explaining that a starved gut heals more slowly, and that the child will lose weight they cannot afford, changes the outcome more than any prescription.

cards.title: Newborn care: the first hour and the first month
body: Dry the baby immediately, and keep them WARM. Hypothermia kills newborns quickly
SKIN TO SKIN contact with the mother, and initiate BREASTFEEDING WITHIN THE FIRST HOUR
Delayed cord clamping (1 to 3 minutes) improves the baby's iron stores
COLOSTRUM is not dirty and must not be discarded. It is the first immunisation the baby ever receives
EXCLUSIVE breastfeeding for 6 months. No water, no honey, no ghutti, nothing else
KANGAROO MOTHER CARE for low birth weight babies: skin to skin, continuous, with exclusive breastfeeding
Cord care: keep it clean and DRY. Nothing is applied to it
Danger signs: poor feeding, lethargy, fever or hypothermia, fast breathing, chest indrawing, convulsions, jaundice in the first 24 hours
cards.clinicalNote: In many families colostrum is discarded as unclean, and the baby is given honey or water instead. That single practice removes the child's entire passive immunity and introduces infection at the same moment. It has to be addressed directly and respectfully.

cards.title: The community health nurse: what the role really is
cards.body:
Community health nursing is not hospital nursing performed outdoors. The unit of care is the FAMILY and the COMMUNITY, not the individual patient in a bed.

THE ROLES:
• CARE PROVIDER: antenatal care, immunisation, treatment of minor illness, follow up
• EDUCATOR: the largest and most powerful part of the role
• COUNSELLOR: family planning, nutrition, adolescent health
• ADVOCATE: for the poor, for women, for those excluded from services
• MANAGER and RECORD KEEPER: the register is what makes follow up possible
• RESEARCHER and surveyor: surveys, surveillance, outbreak detection

THE HOME VISIT is the central tool. It shows you what no clinic consultation can: the water source, the latrine, the cooking smoke, the crowding, who eats last, and who decides.

PRINCIPLES OF A HOME VISIT: have a clear purpose, respect the family's privacy and customs, involve the family in planning, and record what you found and what you did.
cards.clinicalNote: You will treat the same child's diarrhoea three times before you think to look at where the family stores their drinking water. The home visit is what turns repeated treatment into an actual cure, and it is the reason the community nurse exists.

cards.title: Self-check: zinc for how long
cards.body:
A 2 year old with acute diarrhoea is given ORS. The diarrhoea stops after 3 days. How long should the zinc continue?

Answer: THE FULL 14 DAYS, 20 mg daily, even though the diarrhoea has stopped. Zinc continues to repair the gut lining and reduces the risk of a further episode over the next 2 to 3 months. Stopping when symptoms stop loses most of the benefit.

cards.title: Therapeutic vs Non-Therapeutic Responses
cards.body:
THERAPEUTIC:
• Active listening
• Open-ended questions
• Reflection ("You feel scared right now")
• Silence (let them speak)
• Validation ("Your feelings make sense")

AVOID:
• False reassurance ("Everything will be fine")
• Giving advice ("You should…")
• "Why" questions (feel accusatory)
• Changing the subject
• Minimising ("It's not that bad")
• Approval/disapproval

cards.title: Suicide Risk: SAD PERSONS Scale
cards.body:
S: Sex (male higher)
A. Age (<19 or >45)
D. Depression
P. Previous attempts
E. Ethanol/drug abuse
R. Rational thinking loss
S. Social support lacking
O. Organised plan
N. No spouse
S. Sickness (chronic illness)

0–4 low, 5–6 moderate, 7–10 high risk. ASK directly: it does not plant ideas.

cards.title: Therapeutic techniques, and the blocks that ruin them
body: USE: open ended questions, silence, reflection, clarification, summarising, offering self, broad openings
USE: 'You seem worried. Tell me more about that.' It opens the door and hands them the key
BLOCK: giving FALSE REASSURANCE. 'Don't worry, everything will be fine.' It closes the conversation and it may be a lie
BLOCK: giving ADVICE. 'If I were you, I would...' It takes the decision away from them
BLOCK: asking WHY. 'Why do you feel like that?' It sounds like an accusation and it demands a justification
BLOCK: changing the subject, minimising, being defensive, disapproving, stereotyped responses
BLOCK: agreeing or disagreeing outright. It makes your approval the point of the conversation
SILENCE is a technique, not a failure. It gives the patient room to find the words
cards.clinicalNote: False reassurance is the most common therapeutic block, and it comes from a good place: you cannot bear their distress. But 'everything will be fine' tells the patient their fear is unwelcome, and they stop talking. 'That sounds frightening. Tell me more' keeps them with you.

cards.title: Therapeutic relationship: the four phases
cards.body:
PRE-INTERACTION: before you meet them. Examine your OWN feelings, fears and prejudices. This is the phase everyone skips, and it is where your unexamined reaction to a patient begins

ORIENTATION (introductory): build TRUST and RAPPORT. Set the CONTRACT: who you are, what you will do, how long, and the limits of CONFIDENTIALITY. Boundaries are set HERE, not later

WORKING: the longest phase. The real work happens. Explore problems, develop insight, practise new coping. Expect RESISTANCE, it is part of the process

TERMINATION: planned from the BEGINNING, not sprung at the end. Summarise progress, evaluate goals, and allow the patient to express feelings about the ending. Expect a return of symptoms and anger. That is normal grief at a loss, not a relapse

THE RULE: the relationship is THERAPEUTIC, not social. It exists for the patient's benefit, not yours.

cards.title: Self-check: the reassuring response
cards.body:
A patient says 'I am terrified of this operation, I think I am going to die.' Which reply is therapeutic: 'Don't worry, this surgeon is excellent', or 'You are frightened you might not survive this. Tell me more'?

Answer: THE SECOND. The first is FALSE REASSURANCE. It dismisses the fear, closes the conversation and promises something you cannot guarantee. The second reflects the feeling back, shows you heard it, and invites them to keep talking.

cards.title: Recognise Them in a Vignette
cards.body:
Denial – refusing to accept reality
Repression – unconscious blocking of painful thoughts
Projection – attributing your feelings to others
Displacement – redirecting feelings to a safer target
Rationalisation – logical excuses for behaviour
Regression – reverting to earlier behaviour under stress
Reaction formation – acting opposite to true feelings
Sublimation (mature) – channelling impulses into acceptable activity

cards.title: Exam Tips
body: Sublimation is the only one considered consistently "mature/healthy"
Displacement vs projection: displacement REDIRECTS the feeling; projection ASSIGNS it to someone else
Regression is common in hospitalised children (bed-wetting, thumb-sucking)
Identify by the behaviour described, not the label the patient uses

cards.title: Defence mechanisms: naming what you are seeing
body: DENIAL: refusing to accept reality. The alcoholic who insists he can stop any time
PROJECTION: attributing your own unacceptable feelings to someone else. 'The nurses all hate me' when he hates them
DISPLACEMENT: shifting the feeling onto a SAFER target. Shouted at by the boss, he shouts at his wife
RATIONALISATION: inventing a logical sounding excuse. 'I failed because the paper was unfair'
REGRESSION: reverting to an earlier developmental stage. A toilet trained child starts bedwetting in hospital
REACTION FORMATION: behaving as the OPPOSITE of what you feel. Being excessively kind to someone you resent
SUBLIMATION: channelling an unacceptable urge into a socially useful one. Aggression becomes competitive sport. This is the MATURE, HEALTHY defence
REPRESSION: unconscious burial of a memory. SUPPRESSION is the same thing done DELIBERATELY
cards.clinicalNote: Defence mechanisms are not pathology in themselves. Everyone uses them, and they protect us from unbearable feeling. They become a problem only when they are rigid, when they replace reality entirely, and when they stop the person functioning.

cards.title: Anxiety: the four levels and what you can teach at each
cards.body:
MILD: alertness is INCREASED. The person notices more, learns well, and can problem solve. This is useful anxiety, the kind before an exam

MODERATE: the perceptual field NARROWS. They focus on the immediate concern and miss the periphery. They can still learn, WITH DIRECTION

SEVERE: the field is greatly reduced. They focus on a small detail, cannot think or problem solve, and cannot learn. They need direction and reassurance, not information

PANIC: a complete loss of control. Disorganised, may be unable to speak or move, or may run. There is a risk of harm to themselves and others. They CANNOT process anything you say

THE NURSING RULE: you can only TEACH at MILD or MODERATE anxiety.

In SEVERE anxiety or PANIC, do not explain and do not educate. STAY WITH THEM. Remain calm. Speak in short, simple sentences. Reduce the stimuli. Do not leave them alone. Teach later, when the anxiety has come down.

cards.title: Self-check: teaching an anxious patient
cards.body:
A patient in severe anxiety before surgery is pacing and cannot follow what you are saying. Do you continue the pre-operative teaching?

Answer: NO. Learning is not possible at severe anxiety. Stay with them, remain calm, use short simple sentences, and reduce the stimulation. Bring the anxiety down first. Teach when they reach a mild or moderate level, where the information can actually be absorbed.

cards.title: Schizophrenia: positive and negative symptoms
cards.body:
POSITIVE symptoms are things ADDED that should not be there. They respond well to medication.
• DELUSIONS: fixed, false beliefs that cannot be corrected by reason (persecution, grandeur, reference, control)
• HALLUCINATIONS: perception without a stimulus. AUDITORY is by far the commonest in schizophrenia
• Disorganised speech and thought: loose associations, neologisms, word salad, flight of ideas
• Disorganised or bizarre behaviour, catatonia

NEGATIVE symptoms are things TAKEN AWAY. They respond POORLY to medication, and they cause most of the long term disability.
• Blunted or FLAT AFFECT
• ALOGIA: poverty of speech
• AVOLITION: no motivation or drive
• ANHEDONIA: no pleasure in anything
• Social withdrawal

The family is often relieved when the voices stop and then baffled that the person still sits and does nothing. That apathy is the illness too, and it is the harder half.
cards.clinicalNote: Relatives cope with the delusions and forgive the hallucinations, and then lose patience with the negative symptoms, because those look like laziness. Explaining that avolition is a symptom, not a character flaw, changes how a family treats someone for years.

cards.title: Responding to hallucinations: do not argue, do not agree
cards.body:
The patient says the voices are telling him the staff are poisoning his food.

DO NOT ARGUE: 'There are no voices, you are imagining it.' You will lose, and he will stop trusting you.
DO NOT AGREE or pretend to hear them: 'Yes, I hear them too.' You are reinforcing the psychosis and lying to him.

THE THERAPEUTIC RESPONSE, in three parts:
1. ACKNOWLEDGE HIS EXPERIENCE, without confirming the content: 'I can see this is frightening for you.'
2. STATE YOUR REALITY, calmly, without arguing: 'I do not hear any voices.'
3. REFOCUS to the here and now: 'Let us walk to the day room together.'

ALWAYS ASK ABOUT COMMAND HALLUCINATIONS: 'What are the voices telling you to do?'

A voice commanding self harm or harm to others is a psychiatric EMERGENCY, and you cannot know unless you ask. Nurses avoid the question because it feels intrusive. It is the most important question in the room.

cards.title: Antipsychotics: the side effects that make patients stop
body: TYPICAL (haloperidol, chlorpromazine): effective on positive symptoms, but high risk of EXTRAPYRAMIDAL side effects
ATYPICAL (risperidone, olanzapine, clozapine): fewer movement effects, but METABOLIC ones. Weight gain, diabetes, dyslipidaemia
EXTRAPYRAMIDAL SIDE EFFECTS: acute DYSTONIA, AKATHISIA, PSEUDOPARKINSONISM, TARDIVE DYSKINESIA
ACUTE DYSTONIA: a sudden painful muscle spasm, often of the neck, jaw or eyes (oculogyric crisis). Frightening. Treated with IM anticholinergic (promethazine, benztropine). It responds within minutes
AKATHISIA: an intense inner restlessness, an inability to sit still. Often mistaken for agitation and wrongly treated with MORE antipsychotic
TARDIVE DYSKINESIA: involuntary lip smacking, tongue and face movements. Appears LATE, and it may be IRREVERSIBLE
CLOZAPINE: reserved for treatment resistant cases. Causes AGRANULOCYTOSIS. Mandatory regular blood counts, and a sore throat or fever must be reported at once
Anticholinergic effects: dry mouth, blurred vision, constipation, urinary retention. Postural hypotension is common
cards.clinicalNote: Akathisia is misread constantly. The patient paces, cannot settle, and looks agitated, so the answer seems to be more antipsychotic, which makes it worse. Ask them whether the restlessness is INSIDE them. That question distinguishes akathisia from psychotic agitation.

cards.title: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome: the emergency
cards.body:
A rare but potentially FATAL reaction to antipsychotic drugs. Recognising it is the nurse's job, because the first signs appear on the ward, not in a clinic.

THE CARDINAL FEATURES, remember FEVER:
• FEVER, high, sometimes above 40 °C
• Encephalopathy: altered consciousness, confusion, stupor
• Vitals unstable: fluctuating BP, tachycardia, tachypnoea, sweating
• Elevated CREATINE KINASE (from muscle breakdown), and raised white cells
• RIGIDITY: severe, generalised 'lead pipe' muscle rigidity

WHAT HAPPENS: the rigid muscles break down (rhabdomyolysis), releasing myoglobin, which causes ACUTE KIDNEY FAILURE.

IMMEDIATE ACTION:
1. STOP THE ANTIPSYCHOTIC IMMEDIATELY
2. Call for urgent medical help
3. Cool the patient aggressively
4. IV fluids, to protect the kidneys
5. Monitor vital signs, urine output and conscious level continuously
6. Dantrolene and bromocriptine may be prescribed

SERotonin SYNDROME is the mirror image, caused by serotonergic drugs (SSRIs). It also has fever and altered mental state, but the muscles show HYPERREFLEXIA, CLONUS and tremor rather than lead pipe rigidity, and the onset is faster.
cards.clinicalNote: A patient on haloperidol who becomes rigid, confused and febrile is not developing an infection. Reaching for antibiotics and continuing the antipsychotic is the mistake that kills them. Stop the drug first, and escalate.

cards.title: Nursing the psychotic patient
body: SAFETY FIRST, for the patient, for others, and for you. Know where the exit is, and never let the patient be between you and the door
Use SHORT, SIMPLE, CONCRETE sentences. A psychotic patient cannot process complexity
Be CONSISTENT and honest. Do not make promises you cannot keep. Trust is everything
Do not whisper or laugh where the patient can see you but not hear you. It feeds paranoia
With PARANOID patients: offer food in SEALED containers, or let them choose their own plate, if they fear poisoning
Avoid touching a paranoid patient without warning. It can be perceived as an attack
Reduce environmental stimulation. A loud, chaotic ward makes psychosis worse
Assess for SUICIDE and for violence risk on every shift. Psychosis carries a high suicide rate
Monitor food, fluid, sleep, elimination and hygiene. A floridly psychotic person may neglect all of them

cards.title: Self-check: the voices are telling him something
cards.body:
A patient tells you he is hearing voices. What is the single most important question you must ask?

Answer: 'WHAT ARE THE VOICES TELLING YOU TO DO?' You are screening for COMMAND HALLUCINATIONS. A voice commanding self harm or harm to others is a psychiatric emergency, and you will never know unless you ask directly.

cards.title: Depression: what to look for beyond sadness
body: CORE symptoms: persistently LOW MOOD, ANHEDONIA (loss of pleasure), and reduced ENERGY, for at least 2 weeks
BIOLOGICAL symptoms: early morning WAKENING, mood WORSE IN THE MORNING, loss of appetite and weight, loss of libido, constipation
PSYCHOLOGICAL: hopelessness, worthlessness, excessive guilt, poor concentration, indecisiveness
In severe depression there may be PSYCHOTIC features: delusions of guilt, poverty, or nihilism
In older adults depression often presents as PHYSICAL complaints or as apparent memory loss (pseudodementia)
In men and in some cultures it presents as IRRITABILITY, anger or somatic pain rather than sadness
ALWAYS ASK ABOUT SUICIDE. Directly. Asking does NOT plant the idea
The MOST DANGEROUS TIME is when the depression begins to LIFT, because the energy to act returns before the hopelessness goes
cards.clinicalNote: The patient who has been mute and immobile for weeks and suddenly seems brighter and more active is the patient you watch most closely. Severe depression can be so profound that it removes even the energy to act on suicidal thoughts, and treatment returns that energy first.

cards.title: Suicide risk: SAD PERSONS
cards.body:
S: Sex, male (more likely to complete, though women attempt more often)
A: Age, under 19 or over 45
D: Depression

P: Previous attempt. This is the STRONGEST single predictor
E: Ethanol or drug misuse
R: Rational thinking loss, psychosis
S: Social supports lacking, isolation
O: Organised PLAN. A specific plan, with means and a time, is very high risk
N: No spouse or partner
S: Sickness, chronic or terminal illness

ASK DIRECTLY. Escalate the questions:
'Have you had thoughts of ending your life?'
'Have you thought about HOW you would do it?'
'Do you have the means to do it?'
'Have you made a plan? Have you set a time?'

ASKING ABOUT SUICIDE DOES NOT PLANT THE IDEA. This myth costs lives. Most patients are relieved to be asked, because they have been carrying it alone.

A PLAN plus the MEANS plus a TIME is a psychiatric emergency.

cards.title: Nursing the suicidal patient
cards.body:
IMMEDIATE SAFETY:
• Constant, or close, OBSERVATION as assessed. Do not leave a high risk patient alone
• REMOVE THE MEANS: belts, cords, shoelaces, sharps, glass, plastic bags, medications, lighters
• Search belongings as per policy. Be respectful, and explain why
• Never agree to keep a suicidal plan SECRET. A 'no suicide contract' is NOT a safety plan, and it is not evidence of safety

THERAPEUTIC:
• Stay with them. Presence itself is an intervention
• Let them TALK about it. Do not change the subject, and do not appear shocked
• Do NOT be falsely cheerful or minimise: 'You have so much to live for.' It tells them you have not understood
• Instil hope realistically: this feeling is a symptom, it is treatable, and it will change
• Involve the family, and teach them what to watch for

HIGH RISK MOMENTS: shift changes, night, weekends, mealtimes, and just after admission or discharge. These are when staffing is thinnest and observation lapses.
cards.clinicalNote: If a patient asks you to keep a secret before they tell you something, you must say clearly, before they speak, that you cannot promise that. Anything about their safety will be shared with the team. Promising secrecy and then breaking it destroys the trust of every future patient they meet.

cards.title: Mania: recognising it and nursing it
body: ELEVATED, expansive or IRRITABLE mood. Grandiosity, inflated self esteem
Decreased need for SLEEP. They may go days on almost none and feel fine
PRESSURED SPEECH: rapid, loud, hard to interrupt. FLIGHT OF IDEAS
DISTRACTIBILITY, increased goal directed activity, psychomotor agitation
RISK TAKING: spending sprees, sexual indiscretion, reckless driving, giving away money
They do NOT recognise they are ill. Insight is absent, which is why they resist treatment
NURSING: a CALM, LOW STIMULUS environment. Reduce noise, light and activity
FINGER FOODS and high calorie drinks. They are burning energy and are too busy to sit and eat, so they lose weight and become dehydrated
Set FIRM, CONSISTENT limits without arguing. Do not be drawn into their jokes or their arguments
Protect them from the consequences of their behaviour: money, phone, and their dignity
cards.clinicalNote: A manic patient can become dangerously exhausted and dehydrated while apparently full of energy, because they are physically incapable of sitting still long enough to eat or drink. Finger foods they can carry with them is not a comfort measure, it is how you keep them alive.

cards.title: Lithium and the antidepressants
cards.body:
LITHIUM is the classic mood stabiliser, and it has a NARROW THERAPEUTIC INDEX.
• Therapeutic level: 0.6 to 1.2 mEq/L. TOXIC above 1.5
• EARLY TOXICITY: coarse tremor, nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, drowsiness, slurred speech
• SEVERE TOXICITY: ataxia, confusion, seizures, coma, death
• MAINTAIN SALT AND FLUID INTAKE. Lithium is handled like sodium by the kidney, so DEHYDRATION, a low salt diet, vomiting, diarrhoea, or sweating in hot weather all raise the lithium level and cause toxicity
• NSAIDs, diuretics and ACE inhibitors also raise lithium levels
• Monitor levels regularly, plus thyroid and kidney function. It causes hypothyroidism

ANTIDEPRESSANTS:
• SSRIs (fluoxetine, sertraline): first line. GI upset, sexual dysfunction, hyponatraemia
• They take 2 to 4 WEEKS to lift the mood. Explain this, or the patient stops taking them
• Never stop abruptly: discontinuation syndrome
• TRICYCLICS (amitriptyline): anticholinergic effects, cardiotoxic in overdose, which matters greatly in a suicidal patient
• MAOIs: the CHEESE REACTION. Tyramine rich foods (aged cheese, red wine, pickled and fermented foods, yeast extract) cause a HYPERTENSIVE CRISIS
• SEROTONIN SYNDROME if serotonergic drugs are combined: fever, agitation, tremor, hyperreflexia, clonus
cards.clinicalNote: A patient on lithium who develops a stomach bug, or works outdoors through an Indian summer, can become toxic without changing their dose at all. Fluid loss concentrates the lithium. Teaching them to maintain salt and water, and to seek help when vomiting, is the safety net.

cards.title: Self-check: when the depression lifts
cards.body:
A severely depressed patient who has been withdrawn for weeks suddenly appears brighter, more energetic and calmer. The team is pleased. What should concern you?

Answer: THIS MAY BE THE HIGHEST RISK MOMENT FOR SUICIDE. Severe depression can remove even the energy to act. As treatment starts working, the ENERGY returns before the HOPELESSNESS lifts. A sudden calm may also mean they have made a decision. Increase observation and ask directly.

cards.title: The anxiety disorders: telling them apart
body: GENERALISED ANXIETY DISORDER: excessive, uncontrollable worry about many things, most days, for 6 months or more
PANIC DISORDER: sudden, unexpected attacks of intense fear, peaking within minutes. Palpitations, sweating, chest pain, choking, a fear of dying or going mad
Panic attacks are often mistaken for a heart attack, and the patient genuinely believes they are dying
PHOBIA: intense, irrational fear of a specific object or situation, with avoidance
AGORAPHOBIA: fear of places from which escape is difficult. Patients can become housebound
OCD: OBSESSIONS (intrusive, unwanted, distressing thoughts) and COMPULSIONS (repetitive acts done to relieve the anxiety)
The patient with OCD KNOWS the thoughts are irrational, which is what distinguishes it from delusion, and that insight is part of the torment
PTSD: after a traumatic event. Flashbacks, nightmares, avoidance, hyperarousal, emotional numbing
cards.clinicalNote: Never stop a patient performing a compulsive ritual abruptly. The ritual is what is holding their anxiety down, and removing it without a replacement causes overwhelming panic. Ritual reduction is done gradually, with the patient's agreement and with therapy.

cards.title: Panic attack: what to do in the moment
cards.body:
The patient is terrified, hyperventilating, and convinced they are dying.

1. STAY WITH THEM. Do NOT leave them alone. Your calm presence is the treatment
2. Remain CALM yourself. Your anxiety is contagious, and so is your calm
3. Speak in SHORT, SIMPLE sentences. They cannot process anything complex
4. Move them to a QUIET, less stimulating place if you can
5. Guide their BREATHING. Slow, deep breathing, breathing WITH them. Count out loud
6. Reassure them that this WILL PASS, and that they are safe and not dying
7. Do NOT teach, explain or reason during the attack. Do that afterwards
8. Medication (a benzodiazepine) only if prescribed and if needed

AFTERWARDS, when the anxiety is mild or moderate, teach: recognising triggers, breathing exercises, relaxation, grounding techniques, and cognitive behavioural strategies.

Remember: you can only TEACH at MILD or MODERATE anxiety. Never during panic.

cards.title: Alcohol withdrawal: the one that kills
cards.body:
Alcohol withdrawal is more dangerous than the intoxication, and the timeline is predictable.

6 to 12 HOURS: tremor, sweating, anxiety, nausea, headache, tachycardia, hypertension
12 to 24 HOURS: alcoholic HALLUCINOSIS (usually visual, and the patient KNOWS they are not real)
24 to 48 HOURS: WITHDRAWAL SEIZURES, generalised tonic clonic
48 to 72 HOURS AND BEYOND: DELIRIUM TREMENS

DELIRIUM TREMENS is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY with a real mortality:
• Severe CONFUSION and clouding of consciousness
• Vivid, terrifying VISUAL and tactile HALLUCINATIONS (classically insects or small animals)
• Severe AUTONOMIC instability: fever, drenching sweats, tachycardia, hypertension
• Gross tremor, agitation, disorientation

MANAGEMENT:
• BENZODIAZEPINES (chlordiazepoxide, diazepam) on a reducing regimen. This is the core treatment and it PREVENTS seizures and DTs
• THIAMINE (vitamin B1), and it must be given BEFORE any glucose
• Fluids, electrolytes, a calm and well lit environment, and close observation

WERNICKE'S ENCEPHALOPATHY: thiamine deficiency. The classic triad is CONFUSION, ATAXIA and OPHTHALMOPLEGIA. If untreated it becomes KORSAKOFF'S PSYCHOSIS, with irreversible memory loss and CONFABULATION.
cards.clinicalNote: Giving glucose to a malnourished alcoholic before thiamine can precipitate Wernicke's encephalopathy, because the glucose consumes the last of their thiamine. THIAMINE FIRST, THEN GLUCOSE. It is one of the most important orders in medicine and it is disarmingly easy to get backwards.

cards.title: Substance use: what nurses must recognise
body: OPIOID intoxication: PINPOINT pupils, respiratory depression, drowsiness. Antidote NALOXONE
OPIOID withdrawal: intensely unpleasant but NOT usually fatal. Dilated pupils, yawning, runny nose, cramps, diarrhoea, gooseflesh, muscle aches
ALCOHOL withdrawal CAN be fatal. Opioid withdrawal usually is not. This is the key distinction
STIMULANTS (cocaine, amphetamine): dilated pupils, hypertension, tachycardia, agitation, paranoia, hyperthermia
CANNABIS: red eyes, increased appetite, impaired coordination, and it can precipitate psychosis
TOLERANCE: needing more for the same effect. DEPENDENCE: physical or psychological need. ADDICTION: compulsive use despite harm
CAGE questions for alcohol: Cut down? Annoyed by criticism? Guilty? Eye opener in the morning?
Approach WITHOUT judgement. Shame drives concealment, and concealment kills

cards.title: Restraint, seclusion and de-escalation
cards.body:
DE-ESCALATION COMES FIRST, ALWAYS. Restraint is a failure of everything before it.

DE-ESCALATION:
• Stay CALM. Speak slowly and quietly. Your calm is contagious
• Keep a SAFE DISTANCE and a non threatening posture. Hands visible, no sudden movements
• Never let the patient get between you and the DOOR, and never let yourself be cornered
• LISTEN. Acknowledge their anger and their grievance: 'I can see you are furious. Tell me what happened.'
• Offer realistic CHOICES. Restoring some control reduces aggression
• Remove the audience. People escalate in front of a crowd
• Set clear, calm limits on the BEHAVIOUR, not on the person

IF RESTRAINT BECOMES NECESSARY:
• The LEAST restrictive option, for the SHORTEST possible time
• Never as punishment, never for staff convenience, never out of anger
• A doctor's order, time limited, and frequently reviewed
• Continuous or very frequent observation
• Check circulation, offer fluid, toileting and repositioning at least 2 hourly
• DEBRIEF afterwards, with the patient and with the team. Ask what could have been done differently

Restraint traumatises patients and staff. Every episode is a failure to be learned from.
cards.clinicalNote: The single most useful de-escalation skill is listening to the grievance. Most ward aggression begins with someone feeling unheard, powerless and disrespected. Ten minutes of genuine listening prevents far more violence than any restraint policy.

cards.title: Self-check: thiamine or glucose first
cards.body:
A malnourished, confused alcoholic patient is admitted with a low blood sugar. Which do you give first, glucose or thiamine?

Answer: THIAMINE FIRST, THEN GLUCOSE. Glucose metabolism consumes thiamine, and in an already depleted patient giving glucose alone can precipitate WERNICKE'S ENCEPHALOPATHY, which may progress to irreversible Korsakoff's psychosis. In a genuine emergency they may be given together, but thiamine is never omitted.

cards.title: The mental state examination
cards.body:
The MSE is a snapshot of the patient's mental state RIGHT NOW, and it is recorded in a fixed order:

APPEARANCE and BEHAVIOUR: dress, grooming, eye contact, psychomotor activity, rapport, attitude
SPEECH: rate, volume, tone, quantity. Pressured? Poverty of speech? Mute?
MOOD and AFFECT: MOOD is what the patient SAYS they feel (subjective). AFFECT is what YOU OBSERVE (objective). Flat, blunted, labile, INCONGRUENT?
THOUGHT: FORM (how they think: flight of ideas, loose associations, thought block) and CONTENT (what they think: delusions, obsessions, suicidal ideas)
PERCEPTION: hallucinations (which modality?), illusions, depersonalisation
COGNITION: level of consciousness, orientation to time, place and person, attention, memory
INSIGHT: do they know they are ill, and do they accept they need treatment?
JUDGEMENT: can they make safe, reasoned decisions?

MOOD versus AFFECT is examined constantly. Mood is the CLIMATE (what they report over time). Affect is the WEATHER (what you see right now).

cards.title: Delirium versus dementia: the distinction that matters most
body: DELIRIUM: ACUTE onset, over hours to days. Dementia: INSIDIOUS, over months to years
DELIRIUM: consciousness is CLOUDED and FLUCTUATES through the day, classically worse at night (sundowning)
DEMENTIA: consciousness is CLEAR until very late. The decline is steady, not fluctuating
DELIRIUM: attention is grossly impaired. They cannot hold a thread. Hallucinations (often visual) are common
DELIRIUM IS REVERSIBLE, and it is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY. It has a CAUSE, and you must find it
Common causes of delirium: infection (especially UTI and chest), hypoxia, drugs, dehydration, constipation, urinary retention, pain, electrolyte disturbance, alcohol withdrawal
In an older person, delirium is often the FIRST and ONLY sign of a serious physical illness
DEMENTIA is progressive and largely irreversible. But a person with dementia can ALSO develop delirium, and often does
cards.clinicalNote: An older person who suddenly becomes confused has a physical illness until proved otherwise. The most common answer is a urinary or chest infection, and the second most common is a full bladder or a loaded bowel. Sedating them and calling it dementia is how the underlying sepsis gets missed.

cards.title: Caring for the person with dementia
cards.body:
COMMUNICATION:
• Approach from the FRONT, at eye level. Say who you are, every time
• ONE instruction at a time, in short simple sentences. Allow time to respond
• Use their name. Maintain eye contact. A calm, warm tone matters more than the words
• Do NOT argue with, correct or reality-orient a person in the later stages. It causes distress and achieves nothing
• VALIDATION: enter their reality and respond to the FEELING behind it. If she is looking for her mother, do not say 'your mother died forty years ago'. Ask about her mother

ENVIRONMENT:
• ROUTINE, ROUTINE, ROUTINE. Predictability reduces anxiety
• Familiar objects, photographs, good lighting, reduced noise
• Clear signage, a visible clock and calendar
• Safety: wandering, falls, fire, and unsafe use of the kitchen

SUNDOWNING: increased confusion and agitation in the late afternoon and evening. Increase the lighting before dusk, reduce stimulation, keep the routine, and avoid daytime napping.

SUPPORT THE CARER. Carer burnout is enormous, and it is the reason most admissions happen.
cards.clinicalNote: Telling a woman with advanced dementia that her mother is dead means she hears that news, with full grief, for the first time, every time you say it. Validation is not lying to her. It is choosing not to inflict a bereavement over and over.

cards.title: Electroconvulsive therapy: the nursing care
body: Used mainly for SEVERE depression that has not responded to treatment, especially with high suicide risk, refusal to eat or drink, or catatonia
It works FASTER than antidepressants, which is why it is used when someone may not survive the wait
INFORMED CONSENT is required. The patient must understand what it is and be able to refuse
BEFORE: nil by mouth for at least 6 hours, remove dentures, jewellery, hairpins and nail polish. Empty the bladder. Baseline observations
It is done under a GENERAL ANAESTHETIC with a MUSCLE RELAXANT, so the convulsion is modified and the patient does not thrash
AFTER: recovery position, monitor airway and vital signs, stay with them until fully awake
Expect CONFUSION and a HEADACHE afterwards. Reorient them gently and repeatedly, they will not remember your first explanation
The main lasting side effect is MEMORY LOSS, usually of events around the treatment period. It is usually temporary
Do not leave them alone until they are fully alert and oriented

cards.title: Mental health law and the rights of the patient
cards.body:
The core principle: a person with mental illness retains their RIGHTS, their DIGNITY and, wherever possible, their AUTONOMY.

The Mental Healthcare Act 2017 (India) sets out:
• The RIGHT to access mental healthcare
• The RIGHT to be treated with DIGNITY, and protection from cruel or degrading treatment
• The RIGHT to CONFIDENTIALITY
• The RIGHT to legal aid, and to make a complaint
• The RIGHT to community living, and not to be segregated unnecessarily
• ADVANCE DIRECTIVES: a person may state in advance how they wish to be treated if they become unwell
• A NOMINATED REPRESENTATIVE may be appointed to act for them
• It DECRIMINALISED attempted suicide. A person who attempts suicide is presumed to be under severe stress and must be offered CARE, not punishment
• It regulates the use of ECT, which may not be given without anaesthesia and muscle relaxants, and is prohibited for minors without approval
• Seclusion and solitary confinement are PROHIBITED. Restraint is permitted only in narrow circumstances

ADMISSION may be voluntary (the great majority) or, in defined circumstances, supported admission. Even then, the least restrictive option and the person's expressed wishes must be considered.
cards.clinicalNote: Attempted suicide is no longer a crime, and that change matters clinically as well as legally. A patient who fears arrest will hide their intent from you, and hidden intent is what kills people. Knowing the law lets you reassure them honestly.

cards.title: Self-check: sudden confusion in an older patient
cards.body:
An 80 year old with mild dementia becomes acutely confused overnight, seeing things that are not there, and is much worse at night. Is this simply her dementia progressing?

Answer: NO. THIS IS DELIRIUM SUPERIMPOSED ON DEMENTIA. The ACUTE onset, the FLUCTUATION, the visual hallucinations and the worsening at night all point to delirium, which is reversible and has a physical cause. Look for infection, pain, constipation, urinary retention, dehydration or a new drug.

cards.title: Which Method For Which Item?
cards.body:
AUTOCLAVE (121°C, 15 psi, 15 min): surgical instruments, dressings, fluids, glassware
DRY HEAT (160°C / 2 hr): glassware, oils, powders, sharp instruments (won't blunt)
ETHYLENE OXIDE: heat-sensitive items (endoscopes, plastics)
FORMALDEHYDE / GLUTARALDEHYDE: heat-sensitive equipment, surfaces
RADIATION (gamma): single-use plastics, sutures, commercial items
FILTRATION: heat-sensitive liquids (serum, vaccines)

cards.title: Sterilisation: the methods and their exact settings
body: AUTOCLAVE (moist heat under pressure): 121 °C at 15 psi for 15–20 minutes, or 134 °C for 3–4 minutes
The autoclave is the most reliable and most widely used method. It kills SPORES
HOT AIR OVEN (dry heat): 160 °C for 2 hours. For glassware, powders, oils and instruments that rust
Dry heat needs a HIGHER temperature and a LONGER time than moist heat, because dry air transfers heat poorly
BOILING (100 °C for 10–20 min) is DISINFECTION, not sterilisation. It does NOT reliably kill spores
ETHYLENE OXIDE gas: for heat sensitive items, plastics, endoscopes. Toxic, needs long aeration
GAMMA RADIATION: industrial, for pre-packed disposables such as syringes and gloves
FILTRATION: for heat labile fluids such as serum and some antibiotics
cards.clinicalNote: The distinction that matters at the bedside: sterilisation kills EVERYTHING including spores. Disinfection kills most organisms but NOT spores. Boiling an instrument feels thorough and it does not sterilise it.

cards.title: Spaulding: how clean does it need to be
cards.body:
The level of decontamination depends on where the item goes.

CRITICAL items enter STERILE tissue or the vascular system.
• Surgical instruments, implants, catheters, needles
• They must be STERILISED. Nothing less will do

SEMI-CRITICAL items touch MUCOUS MEMBRANES or broken skin.
• Endoscopes, respiratory equipment, vaginal specula
• They need HIGH LEVEL DISINFECTION at minimum

NON-CRITICAL items touch INTACT SKIN only.
• BP cuffs, stethoscopes, bedpans, bed rails
• LOW or intermediate level disinfection is enough

Intact skin is an excellent barrier, which is why a stethoscope does not need autoclaving. But it is a superb vehicle for carrying organisms from one patient to the next, which is why it must still be wiped between patients.

AUTOCLAVE CONTROLS: the Bowie-Dick test checks air removal. Chemical indicator tape shows it was PROCESSED, not that it was STERILE. A BIOLOGICAL indicator (Geobacillus spores) is the only true proof.

cards.title: Self-check: the autoclave tape turned black
cards.body:
The indicator tape on an autoclaved pack has changed colour. Does that prove the contents are sterile?

Answer: NO. Chemical indicator tape only proves the pack has been THROUGH the process and reached a certain temperature. It does NOT prove sterility. Only a BIOLOGICAL indicator, using resistant bacterial spores, actually proves that sterilisation was achieved.

cards.title: Disinfectants: choosing the right one
cards.body:
Not every job needs the same agent, and using the wrong one is either useless or destructive.

ALCOHOL (60–90%): fast, for skin and small surfaces. Does NOT kill spores. It does not work on visibly soiled surfaces, because organic matter blocks it

CHLORINE (sodium hypochlorite, bleach): cheap and broad spectrum. It DOES kill spores including C. difficile. The standard for blood spills (usually 0.5% or 1%). Corrodes metal, and it is inactivated by organic matter, so clean first

GLUTARALDEHYDE: high level disinfection for endoscopes. Toxic and irritant, needs good ventilation

HYDROGEN PEROXIDE: surfaces and wounds

POVIDONE IODINE and CHLORHEXIDINE: antiseptics for SKIN and tissue. Chlorhexidine has a longer residual action, which is why it is preferred for line insertion and surgical scrub

THE KEY DISTINCTION:
ANTISEPTIC goes on LIVING tissue (skin, wounds).
DISINFECTANT goes on INANIMATE objects (surfaces, instruments).
They are not interchangeable. A disinfectant strong enough for a floor will damage tissue.

ALWAYS CLEAN BEFORE YOU DISINFECT. Organic matter (blood, pus, faeces) inactivates most disinfectants, so disinfecting a dirty surface achieves very little.
cards.clinicalNote: Alcohol gel on visibly soiled hands does almost nothing, because the organic matter shields the organisms from it. That is the same reason you clean a surface before disinfecting it. Dirt is not just untidy, it is chemically protective.

cards.title: Airborne: "My Chicken Tastes Tasty"
cards.body:
AIRBORNE (droplet nuclei <5 µm, negative-pressure room + N95):
  • Measles
  • Tuberculosis
  • Varicella (chickenpox) / disseminated zoster

DROPLET (>5 µm, surgical mask, ~1 m): influenza, pertussis, meningococcus, mumps, rubella
CONTACT (gown + gloves): MRSA, C. difficile, scabies

C. difficile and norovirus need SOAP-AND-WATER (alcohol rub doesn't kill spores).

cards.title: Standard Precautions
body: Apply to ALL patients regardless of diagnosis
Hand hygiene is the single most effective measure
PPE chosen by anticipated exposure
Don: gown → mask → goggles → gloves; Doff: gloves → goggles → gown → mask
Most contaminated item comes off first; hand hygiene between steps

cards.title: The chain of infection: six links, six chances to break it
cards.body:
Infection needs all six links. Break ANY ONE and the infection does not happen. This is the entire theory of infection control in one idea.

1. INFECTIOUS AGENT: the organism. Broken by antibiotics, disinfection, sterilisation
2. RESERVOIR: where it lives and multiplies (people, water, equipment, food). Broken by cleaning, waste disposal, treating carriers
3. PORTAL OF EXIT: how it leaves (secretions, blood, faeces, droplets). Broken by covering coughs, safe waste handling
4. MODE OF TRANSMISSION: how it travels (contact, droplet, airborne, vehicle, vector). Broken by HAND HYGIENE, PPE, isolation. THIS IS THE WEAKEST LINK, and the one nurses break most often
5. PORTAL OF ENTRY: how it gets in (broken skin, mucosa, catheters, cannulas). Broken by aseptic technique, wound care, early removal of devices
6. SUSCEPTIBLE HOST: the person. Broken by IMMUNISATION, nutrition, treating the underlying illness

HAND HYGIENE is the single most effective intervention in all of infection control, because it breaks the strongest and most frequent mode of transmission.
cards.clinicalNote: Every infection control policy you will ever read is simply an attempt to break one of these six links. Understanding that turns a long list of rules into a single idea you can reason from at the bedside.

cards.title: PPE: the order that protects you
cards.body:
PUTTING IT ON (donning), from the inside out:
1. Hand hygiene
2. GOWN
3. MASK or respirator
4. GOGGLES or face shield
5. GLOVES (last, over the gown cuffs)

TAKING IT OFF (doffing), dirtiest first:
1. GLOVES (they are the FILTHIEST thing you are wearing)
2. Hand hygiene
3. GOGGLES or face shield
4. GOWN
5. MASK or respirator (LAST, because you are still breathing the room's air until you leave)
6. Hand hygiene again

Most self contamination happens during REMOVAL, not during care, which is why doffing is examined far more often than donning.

Remove the mask LAST, and remove it OUTSIDE the room where possible. Never touch the front of the mask, it is contaminated. Handle it by the ties or the loops.

cards.title: Self-check: which comes off first
cards.body:
You are leaving the room of a patient on contact precautions. What is the FIRST item of PPE you remove, and why?

Answer: THE GLOVES. They are the most heavily contaminated item you are wearing, having touched the patient and the environment directly. Removing them first prevents you spreading that contamination onto your face, hair and clothing while removing everything else.

cards.title: The classes of microorganism and what treats them
body: BACTERIA: have a cell wall. Treated with ANTIBIOTICS. Gram positive (thick wall, stains PURPLE) or gram negative (stains PINK)
VIRUSES: need a living host cell to replicate. Antibiotics do NOTHING. Treated with antivirals, prevented by vaccines
FUNGI: yeasts and moulds. Candida, Aspergillus, dermatophytes. Treated with ANTIFUNGALS
PARASITES: protozoa (malaria, amoeba, Giardia) and helminths (worms). Treated with antiparasitics and anthelmintics
PRIONS: abnormal proteins. Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease. They resist normal sterilisation, which is why they are feared
Gram POSITIVE examples: Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, Clostridium
Gram NEGATIVE examples: E. coli, Salmonella, Pseudomonas, Neisseria, Klebsiella
Mycobacterium tuberculosis is ACID FAST, stained with Ziehl-Neelsen, not by the routine Gram stain
cards.clinicalNote: The single most common and most damaging misunderstanding in the whole of microbiology is giving an antibiotic for a viral illness. It cannot help, it exposes the patient to side effects, and it breeds resistance for everyone else.

cards.title: The organisms behind the classic infections
cards.body:
Know the bug, and the management follows.

STAPHYLOCOCCUS AUREUS: skin and wound infection, abscess, boils, food poisoning (fast onset, from a toxin), osteomyelitis. MRSA is the resistant strain
STREPTOCOCCUS PYOGENES: sore throat, scarlet fever, impetigo. Its late complications are RHEUMATIC FEVER and post-streptococcal GLOMERULONEPHRITIS
CLOSTRIDIUM TETANI: tetanus, from soil entering a wound. Spore forming
CLOSTRIDIUM DIFFICILE: antibiotic associated diarrhoea and colitis. SPORE forming, so alcohol gel does NOT kill it
E. COLI: the commonest cause of urinary tract infection
SALMONELLA TYPHI: typhoid. Step ladder fever, rose spots, relative bradycardia
VIBRIO CHOLERAE: cholera. Painless, profuse RICE WATER stools and catastrophic dehydration
MYCOBACTERIUM TUBERCULOSIS: TB. Acid fast, airborne
NEISSERIA MENINGITIDIS: meningococcal meningitis. A non blanching purpuric rash. Droplet precautions

The reason strep throat is treated is not the sore throat. It is to prevent rheumatic fever damaging the heart valves weeks later.

cards.title: Specimen collection: getting the answer right
cards.body:
A culture is only as good as the specimen. A badly collected sample produces a wrong answer, and the patient is treated for the wrong thing.

THE UNIVERSAL RULES:
• Collect BEFORE antibiotics are started, wherever the patient's condition allows
• Use the correct container, and the correct transport medium
• LABEL AT THE BEDSIDE, in front of the patient, with two identifiers
• Send it PROMPTLY. A specimen left on a desk overgrows with contaminants
• Never contaminate the outside of the container

SPECIFICS:
• BLOOD CULTURE: strict ASEPSIS, clean the skin and the bottle top. Take from TWO different sites. Take BEFORE antibiotics. Take during a fever spike if you can
• URINE: MIDSTREAM clean catch. The first part of the stream washes out urethral contaminants and is discarded. Send within an hour, or refrigerate
• SPUTUM: a DEEP COUGH specimen, not saliva. Best EARLY MORNING. Rinse the mouth with water first. For TB, take two samples
• WOUND SWAB: swab the wound BEFORE cleaning it, from the depth of the wound and not the surrounding skin
• STOOL: for C. difficile, the specimen must take the shape of the container. A formed stool is not tested

The commonest error in the whole list is swabbing a wound AFTER cleaning it with antiseptic. You then culture the antiseptic.
cards.clinicalNote: Taking a blood culture after the first dose of antibiotics is a wasted needle and a wasted opportunity. The organism may never grow, and the patient spends two weeks on a broad spectrum guess instead of a targeted drug.

cards.title: Hospital acquired infection: the four that dominate
body: Also called nosocomial infection. Acquired 48 hours or more after admission, and not incubating on arrival
URINARY TRACT INFECTION, mostly CATHETER associated. The commonest of all. Prevention: do not catheterise, and remove it early
SURGICAL SITE INFECTION. Prevention: asepsis, glucose control, normothermia, appropriate prophylactic antibiotics
PNEUMONIA, especially VENTILATOR associated. Prevention: head of bed 30–45 degrees, oral care with chlorhexidine, sedation holds, early weaning
BLOODSTREAM infection, mostly LINE associated. Prevention: aseptic insertion bundle, daily review of need, remove the line as soon as possible
The common thread in all four: an INVASIVE DEVICE. Every device is a highway past the body's defences
The single best prevention across all of them is HAND HYGIENE, followed by removing the device as early as possible
MRSA, VRE, C. difficile and multi drug resistant gram negatives are the classic hospital organisms
cards.clinicalNote: Every catheter, cannula and tube is a hole through the patient's defences, and it stays a hole for as long as it remains in. The most effective infection control question on any ward round is simply: does this patient still need this device today?

cards.title: Immunity: how the body fights back
cards.body:
INNATE (non specific, present from birth, immediate):
• Physical barriers: skin, mucous membranes, cilia, stomach acid, tears, normal flora
• Inflammatory response, phagocytes (neutrophils, macrophages), natural killer cells, fever
• It does not remember. It responds identically every time

ADAPTIVE (specific, acquired, slower but it REMEMBERS):
• HUMORAL: B lymphocytes make ANTIBODIES against a specific antigen
• CELL MEDIATED: T lymphocytes. Helper T cells (CD4) coordinate. Cytotoxic T cells (CD8) kill infected cells
• MEMORY cells are why a second exposure produces a faster, stronger response. That is the entire basis of vaccination

THE ANTIBODIES:
• IgG: the most abundant. It CROSSES THE PLACENTA and protects the newborn. It dominates the SECONDARY response
• IgM: the FIRST to appear in an acute infection. Large. Its presence means a RECENT or current infection
• IgA: in secretions, tears, saliva and BREAST MILK. It protects mucous membranes
• IgE: allergy and parasites
• IgD: on B cell surfaces

HIV destroys CD4 helper T cells, which is why it dismantles the whole adaptive response and leaves the patient open to opportunistic infection.
cards.clinicalNote: IgM means new, IgG means old or immune. That single distinction lets you read most serology reports: IgM positive suggests current infection, IgG positive alone suggests past infection or successful vaccination.

cards.title: Self-check: swab before or after cleaning
cards.body:
You are asked to take a wound swab from an infected surgical wound. Do you clean the wound first?

Answer: NO. SWAB BEFORE CLEANING. If you clean with antiseptic first, you kill the very organisms you are trying to grow, and the culture may come back falsely negative. Swab from the DEPTH of the wound, not the surrounding skin, then clean and dress it.

cards.title: HIV: transmission and what does NOT transmit it
cards.body:
HIV attacks CD4 helper T cells, the coordinators of the immune system. As they fall, the patient becomes vulnerable to OPPORTUNISTIC infections.

TRANSMISSION:
• Unprotected SEXUAL contact
• BLOOD: transfusion, contaminated needles, sharing injecting equipment, needlestick injury
• MOTHER TO CHILD: in pregnancy, during delivery, and through BREAST MILK

IT IS NOT TRANSMITTED BY:
• Casual contact, hugging, shaking hands, sharing utensils, food or toilets
• Coughing, sneezing, mosquitoes, sweat or tears

That second list matters as much as the first. Stigma keeps people from testing, and untested people transmit.

STAGING BY CD4 COUNT:
• Normal CD4: 500 to 1500 cells/mm³
• Below 200: AIDS defining. High risk of Pneumocystis pneumonia, and prophylaxis is started
• Common opportunistic infections: TUBERCULOSIS (by far the commonest in India), Pneumocystis pneumonia, candidiasis, cryptococcal meningitis, toxoplasmosis, Kaposi's sarcoma

WINDOW PERIOD: the time between infection and the antibody test becoming positive. During it the person is INFECTIOUS but tests NEGATIVE. This is why a single early negative test cannot be trusted after a genuine exposure.

PMTCT (prevention of mother to child transmission): antiretrovirals in pregnancy, safe delivery, and infant prophylaxis reduce transmission from around 30% to under 5%.
cards.clinicalNote: Fear of casual transmission is what drives HIV stigma, and stigma is what stops people testing. A nurse who eats with, touches and cares for an HIV positive patient normally, in front of others, is doing genuine public health work.

cards.title: Needlestick injury: what to do, in order
cards.body:
It has just happened. Do these things in this order, and do not panic.

1. DO NOT SQUEEZE the wound. Squeezing damages tissue and may drive material deeper
2. WASH immediately with SOAP AND RUNNING WATER. Encourage gentle bleeding, do not scrub
3. For a mucous membrane or eye splash: irrigate copiously with water or saline
4. DO NOT use bleach, alcohol or antiseptic on the wound. Do not suck it
5. REPORT IMMEDIATELY to the supervisor and occupational health. Do not go home and think about it
6. Assess the SOURCE patient's status: HIV, hepatitis B, hepatitis C
7. POST EXPOSURE PROPHYLAXIS (PEP) for HIV must be started as soon as possible, IDEALLY WITHIN 2 HOURS, and certainly within 72 hours. It runs for 28 days
8. Hepatitis B: immunoglobulin and vaccine if you are not immune
9. Baseline blood tests, then follow up testing
10. Document and complete an incident report

THE PREVENTION: never RECAP a needle. Most needlestick injuries happen during recapping. Dispose of sharps at the point of use, in a puncture proof container, never more than three quarters full.

cards.title: The hepatitis viruses: routes and prevention
body: HEPATITIS A: FAECAL-ORAL. Contaminated food and water. Acute only, it does NOT become chronic. Vaccine available
HEPATITIS E: FAECAL-ORAL, water borne. Usually self limiting, but it is DANGEROUS IN PREGNANCY, with a high mortality
HEPATITIS B: BLOOD and BODY FLUIDS, sexual, and mother to child. CAN become chronic, leading to cirrhosis and liver cancer
Hepatitis B is highly infectious, far more so than HIV from the same needlestick. There IS a vaccine, and every health worker must have it
HEPATITIS C: mainly BLOOD (transfusion, injecting drug use). Often becomes CHRONIC and silent for years. NO vaccine, but it is now curable with antivirals
HEPATITIS D: needs hepatitis B to exist, it cannot infect alone
Memory hook: A and E come from what you EAT (faecal-oral). B, C and D come from BLOOD
Signs of hepatitis: jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, nausea, right upper quadrant pain, fatigue
