cards.clinicalNote: The hepatitis B vaccine is the single most important thing a nursing student can do for their own safety, and it is often left half finished. The course is three doses, and an incomplete course leaves you unprotected against the most infectious blood borne virus you will meet.

cards.title: Standard precautions: the principle that protects everyone
cards.body:
THE CORE IDEA: treat the BLOOD and BODY FLUIDS of EVERY patient as though they were infectious, regardless of their diagnosis.

WHY: because you do not know who is infected. Many people with HIV, hepatitis B and hepatitis C do not know it themselves. A policy of taking precautions only with 'known' cases protects nobody, because the unknown cases are the majority.

STANDARD PRECAUTIONS INCLUDE:
• HAND HYGIENE, before and after every patient contact
• GLOVES whenever contact with blood, body fluid, mucosa or broken skin is anticipated
• GOWN, MASK and EYE PROTECTION whenever splashing is possible
• SAFE SHARPS HANDLING. Never recap. Dispose at the point of use
• Safe handling of contaminated linen and waste
• Cleaning and disinfection of equipment and surfaces
• Respiratory hygiene and cough etiquette

They apply to ALL patients, ALL the time. Additional transmission based precautions (contact, droplet, airborne) are then added on top for specific organisms.

GLOVES ARE NOT A SUBSTITUTE FOR HAND HYGIENE. Hands are washed BEFORE putting them on and AFTER taking them off. Gloves have micro perforations, and hands get contaminated during removal.
cards.clinicalNote: The whole point of standard precautions is that they remove the need to guess. The moment you start deciding who looks risky, you are wrong, and you are also discriminating. Everyone gets the same care and the same caution.

cards.title: Caring for the patient with HIV: the nursing priorities
body: CONFIDENTIALITY is absolute. Disclosure without consent causes real, lasting harm and is a breach of duty
Standard precautions are sufficient. No special isolation is needed for HIV alone
PREVENT INFECTION: the patient is immunosuppressed. Protect them FROM you and from other patients
Monitor for opportunistic infection: TB above all in India. Any cough, fever or weight loss must be investigated
ADHERENCE to antiretroviral therapy must be near perfect. Poor adherence breeds resistance and the regimen fails
Antiretrovirals are LIFELONG and are not a cure. They suppress the virus, and an undetectable viral load means untransmittable
NUTRITION: they have higher energy and protein needs, and often a poor appetite and oral thrush
Watch for drug side effects, and for TB and HIV drug interactions, which are significant
Address the STIGMA directly. Isolation, shame and depression are as damaging as the virus

cards.title: Self-check: the needlestick, first action
cards.body:
You sustain a needlestick from a patient of unknown HIV status. A colleague tells you to squeeze the finger hard to make it bleed. Correct?

Answer: NO. DO NOT SQUEEZE. It damages tissue and may drive contaminated material deeper. WASH with soap and running water, allow it to bleed gently, report immediately, and get post exposure prophylaxis assessed. PEP works best within 2 hours, and certainly within 72.

cards.title: Renal Diet Cheat Sheet
cards.body:
PRE-DIALYSIS CKD: limit protein (~0.6–0.8 g/kg), restrict K+, phosphate, sodium, fluid

ON HAEMODIALYSIS: INCREASE protein (1.2 g/kg) because dialysis removes it. Still restrict K+, phosphate, sodium, fluid.

FOODS HIGH IN K+ to avoid: bananas, oranges, tomato, coconut water, leafy greens, potatoes (soak before cooking helps)
FOODS HIGH IN PHOSPHATE: dairy, nuts, processed foods, cola

Fluid limit on dialysis: ~1L/day + previous day's urine output

cards.title: Therapeutic diets: which restriction, which disease
body: DIABETES: consistent carbohydrate at each meal, high fibre, low glycaemic index. Regular timing matters as much as content
HYPERTENSION: LOW SODIUM (under 5–6 g salt a day). DASH pattern: fruit, vegetables, low fat dairy
HEART FAILURE: low sodium AND fluid restriction. Daily weights are the monitor
CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE: restrict PROTEIN, POTASSIUM, PHOSPHATE, SODIUM and FLUID
DIALYSIS patients need MORE protein, not less, because dialysis removes it. This reverses the pre-dialysis rule
LIVER FAILURE with encephalopathy: restrict protein only in acute severe episodes, not long term
GOUT: reduce purines (red meat, organ meat, seafood, alcohol, especially beer)
COELIAC: strictly NO gluten. Wheat, barley, rye. Lifelong, and even small amounts matter
LOW RESIDUE before bowel surgery or colonoscopy. HIGH FIBRE for constipation and diverticular disease
cards.clinicalNote: The protein rule in kidney disease reverses the moment dialysis starts, and this catches people out constantly. Before dialysis you restrict protein to spare the kidney. On dialysis you increase it, because the treatment itself strips protein out.

cards.title: Potassium and phosphate: what a renal patient must avoid
cards.body:
HIGH POTASSIUM FOODS to limit in kidney disease (hyperkalaemia stops the heart):
• BANANA, orange, kiwi, dried fruit
• POTATO, tomato, spinach, and other green leafy vegetables
• Coconut water, fruit juices
• Dal and pulses, nuts
• SALT SUBSTITUTES. This is the hidden killer: 'low sodium' salt is usually POTASSIUM chloride, and a renal patient who switches to it can develop fatal hyperkalaemia

TIP FOR PATIENTS: potatoes and vegetables can be cut small, soaked, and boiled in plenty of water which is then discarded (leaching). It removes a useful part of the potassium.

HIGH PHOSPHATE FOODS to limit:
• Dairy: milk, cheese, paneer, curd
• Nuts, seeds, whole grains
• Cola drinks, processed food with phosphate additives

PHOSPHATE BINDERS ARE TAKEN WITH THE FIRST MOUTHFUL OF FOOD. They work by binding phosphate IN the meal. Taken an hour later, they do nothing at all, and patients do this constantly.

cards.title: Self-check: the low sodium salt substitute
cards.body:
A patient with chronic kidney disease proudly tells you he has switched to 'low sodium salt' to protect his kidneys. What is your concern?

Answer: MOST SALT SUBSTITUTES ARE POTASSIUM CHLORIDE. In kidney disease the kidneys cannot excrete potassium, so this can cause life threatening HYPERKALAEMIA and cardiac arrest. He has swapped one risk for a far more immediate one, and he must stop.

cards.title: Diet and the diabetic patient
cards.body:
The aim is a steady blood glucose, not a joyless diet. What matters is CONSISTENCY, TIMING and QUALITY of carbohydrate, more than its total absence.

PRINCIPLES:
• Keep the carbohydrate amount CONSISTENT at each meal, and keep meal TIMES regular. Erratic meals with fixed insulin is what causes hypoglycaemia
• Prefer LOW GLYCAEMIC INDEX foods: whole grains, millets, pulses, vegetables. They release glucose slowly
• Avoid high glycaemic index foods: refined flour, polished rice, sugary drinks, sweets, fruit juice
• HIGH FIBRE slows glucose absorption and improves control
• Do NOT skip meals, especially if on insulin or a sulfonylurea
• Small, frequent meals are better than two large ones
• Fruit is allowed, in portions, as whole fruit. Fruit JUICE is not: the fibre is gone and the sugar hits fast

SICK DAY RULE: never stop insulin during illness, even if not eating. Illness RAISES blood glucose. Replace meals with carbohydrate containing fluids and monitor more often.

ALCOHOL can cause DELAYED hypoglycaemia, hours later, because it blocks the liver from releasing glucose. If they drink, it must be with food.
cards.clinicalNote: A diabetic on insulin who skips a meal because they were busy, or because they were kept nil by mouth for a scan nobody cancelled the insulin for, is the commonest cause of hypoglycaemia on a ward. The food and the insulin have to move together.

cards.title: Fat- vs Water-Soluble
body: Fat-soluble = "ADEK" (A, D, E, K), stored in body, toxicity possible
Water-soluble = B-complex + C, not stored, deficiency appears faster
Vitamin A: night blindness, Bitot's spots
Vitamin D: rickets (child) / osteomalacia (adult)
Vitamin K: bleeding (clotting factors II, VII, IX, X)

cards.title: B-Complex Deficiencies
body: B1 (Thiamine): beriberi, Wernicke encephalopathy
B2 (Riboflavin): angular stomatitis, glossitis
B3 (Niacin): pellagra: the 3 Ds (Dermatitis, Diarrhoea, Dementia)
B6 (Pyridoxine): peripheral neuropathy (esp. with isoniazid)
B9/B12: megaloblastic anaemia; B12 also causes neuro signs
Vitamin C: scurvy (bleeding gums, poor wound healing)

cards.title: Vitamin deficiencies and their signature signs
body: VITAMIN A: NIGHT BLINDNESS, then Bitot's spots, xerophthalmia, and eventually irreversible blindness. Also raises infection and measles mortality
VITAMIN B1 (thiamine): BERIBERI. Wet beriberi is heart failure, dry beriberi is neuropathy. WERNICKE'S in alcoholics
VITAMIN B3 (niacin): PELLAGRA. The 3 Ds: Dermatitis (on sun exposed skin), Diarrhoea, Dementia. A 4th D is Death
VITAMIN B9 (folate): megaloblastic anaemia, and NEURAL TUBE DEFECTS in the fetus
VITAMIN B12: megaloblastic anaemia PLUS neurological damage (subacute combined degeneration). Common in strict vegetarians
VITAMIN C: SCURVY. Bleeding gums, poor wound healing, perifollicular haemorrhage, corkscrew hairs
VITAMIN D: RICKETS in children (bowed legs, rachitic rosary), OSTEOMALACIA in adults
VITAMIN K: bleeding. Newborns get a routine vitamin K injection to prevent haemorrhagic disease
IRON: microcytic hypochromic anaemia, koilonychia (spoon nails), pica, glossitis
cards.clinicalNote: Folate and B12 both cause a megaloblastic anaemia, but only B12 deficiency damages the nerves. Treating a B12 deficiency with folate alone corrects the blood count while the spinal cord degeneration continues silently, and that damage can become permanent.

cards.title: Fat soluble versus water soluble: ADEK
cards.body:
FAT SOLUBLE: A, D, E, K. Remember ADEK.
• They are STORED in the liver and fat, so deficiency takes a long time to appear
• They can accumulate to TOXIC levels. Overdose is a real risk, especially vitamin A and D
• They need FAT and BILE for absorption, so fat malabsorption (coeliac, cystic fibrosis, biliary obstruction) causes deficiency of all four

WATER SOLUBLE: the B group and vitamin C.
• NOT stored (except B12, which is stored in the liver for years)
• Excess is excreted in the urine, so toxicity is rare
• They must be replaced REGULARLY, and deficiency appears relatively quickly
• They are LOST IN COOKING WATER and destroyed by heat, so overcooking and discarding the water wastes them

PRACTICAL TEACHING: steam or cook vegetables in minimal water, and use that water in the dish. Do not boil and drain, or you pour the vitamins down the sink.

cards.title: Self-check: night blindness in a child
cards.body:
A 3 year old cannot see well at dusk and has dry, dull conjunctivae with foamy patches. What is the deficiency and why is it urgent?

Answer: VITAMIN A DEFICIENCY, with Bitot's spots. It is urgent because it progresses from reversible night blindness to xerophthalmia, corneal ulceration and IRREVERSIBLE BLINDNESS. It also sharply increases the risk of dying from measles and diarrhoea. Vitamin A is given immediately.

cards.title: The macronutrients: what each provides
body: CARBOHYDRATE: 4 kcal per gram. The main energy source. Should be 50–60% of total energy
PROTEIN: 4 kcal per gram. For growth and repair, not primarily for energy. About 10–15% of energy
FAT: 9 kcal per gram, MORE THAN DOUBLE the others. This is why fat drives weight gain. About 20–30% of energy
ALCOHOL: 7 kcal per gram, and it provides no useful nutrients at all
COMPLETE proteins contain all essential amino acids: egg, milk, meat, fish. Egg is the reference protein
INCOMPLETE proteins (most plant proteins) lack one or more. Cereals lack lysine, pulses lack methionine
COMBINING cereal and pulse (dal and rice, roti and dal) gives a COMPLETE protein. This is why the traditional Indian meal works
ESSENTIAL nutrients cannot be made by the body and must come from food
cards.clinicalNote: The dal and rice combination is not a cultural accident. Cereals are low in lysine and pulses are low in methionine, and each supplies what the other lacks. Explaining this gives a vegetarian family real confidence about protein without any expensive food.

cards.title: The balanced diet: the food groups
cards.body:
A balanced diet supplies all nutrients in the right proportion. Build every meal from these groups:

ENERGY GIVING: cereals, millets, roots, tubers, fats and sugars
BODY BUILDING: pulses, dal, milk, egg, meat, fish, nuts
PROTECTIVE: fruits and vegetables, supplying vitamins, minerals and fibre

A SIMPLE PLATE RULE for patients who cannot read a chart:
• Half the plate: VEGETABLES and fruit
• A quarter: CEREAL (rice, roti)
• A quarter: PROTEIN (dal, egg, fish, meat, paneer)
• Plus a little milk or curd

ADVICE THAT WORKS IN PRACTICE:
• Use a VARIETY of colours. Different colours carry different micronutrients
• Prefer WHOLE grains to polished ones. Polishing removes the B vitamins and fibre
• Reduce salt, sugar and fried food
• Do not discard cooking water, the water soluble vitamins are in it
• Iodised salt, added AFTER cooking and stored in a closed dry container

cards.title: Assessing nutritional status
cards.body:
Remember the ABCD of nutritional assessment.

A: ANTHROPOMETRY, the measurements
• Weight, height, BMI
• MID UPPER ARM CIRCUMFERENCE (MUAC), which needs no scales and is superb in the field
• Skinfold thickness, waist circumference

B: BIOCHEMICAL
• Haemoglobin, serum albumin, serum protein, transferrin
• ALBUMIN has a long half life (about 20 days), so it reflects LONG TERM status and changes slowly
• PREALBUMIN has a short half life and responds quickly, so it is better for monitoring recent change

C: CLINICAL, the physical signs
• Hair, skin, eyes, mouth, nails, oedema. Deficiency writes itself on the body

D: DIETARY
• 24 hour recall, food frequency questionnaire, food diary

BODY MASS INDEX = weight in kg ÷ (height in metres)²
• Underweight: below 18.5
• Normal: 18.5–24.9
• Overweight: 25–29.9
• Obese: 30 and above
• Asian populations develop metabolic risk at LOWER BMI, so lower cut offs (23 for overweight, 25 for obese) are often used in India

MUAC in a child 6 to 59 months:
• Below 11.5 cm: SEVERE acute malnutrition
• 11.5 to 12.5 cm: MODERATE acute malnutrition
cards.clinicalNote: A MUAC tape costs almost nothing, needs no electricity, no scales and no arithmetic, and it identifies the children at highest risk of dying. In a field setting it outperforms far more sophisticated tools.

cards.title: Nutrition through the life cycle
body: PREGNANCY: an extra 350 kcal per day and an extra 23 g protein in the 2nd and 3rd trimesters. Iron, folic acid and calcium supplements
LACTATION: an extra 600 kcal per day and an extra 19 g protein for the first 6 months. Fluid needs rise sharply
INFANT: EXCLUSIVE breastfeeding for 6 months. Nothing else, not even water
COMPLEMENTARY FEEDING starts AT 6 months, while breastfeeding continues to 2 years and beyond
Start with soft, mashed, energy dense food. Increase thickness and variety gradually. Add oil or ghee for energy density
ADOLESCENT: rapid growth, high iron needs (girls especially, because of menstruation). Calcium for peak bone mass
ELDERLY: energy needs FALL, but protein, calcium and micronutrient needs do NOT. Nutrient dense food matters
Elderly problems: poor dentition, reduced taste and thirst, constipation, isolation, poverty, difficulty shopping and cooking
cards.clinicalNote: Delaying complementary feeding past six months is as harmful as starting it too early. Breast milk alone can no longer meet a growing child's energy and iron needs after six months, and the gap that opens is where stunting begins.

cards.title: Food safety and food borne illness
cards.body:
FOOD POISONING TYPES:
• STAPHYLOCOCCUS AUREUS: a preformed TOXIN. Onset is FAST, 1 to 6 hours. Vomiting dominates. From food handled by someone with a skin infection, then left unrefrigerated
• SALMONELLA: onset 12 to 48 hours. Fever and diarrhoea. From poultry, eggs, meat
• CLOSTRIDIUM BOTULINUM: BOTULISM. From improperly canned or preserved food. It causes DESCENDING paralysis, blurred vision, difficulty swallowing and speaking, and death from respiratory failure. A medical emergency
• CLOSTRIDIUM PERFRINGENS: from meat kept warm for long periods, as in mass catering

THE FAST ONSET ONES ARE TOXINS. The SLOWER ONSET ones are the organism itself multiplying.

THE DANGER ZONE: 5 °C to 60 °C. Bacteria multiply fastest in this range. Keep hot food HOT (above 60 °C) and cold food COLD (below 5 °C).

SAFE PRACTICE:
• Wash hands before handling food
• Separate raw and cooked food. Never use the same board or knife without washing
• Cook thoroughly, especially poultry, eggs and meat
• Refrigerate promptly, do not leave cooked food at room temperature for hours
• Reheat thoroughly and only ONCE
• Never let a person with diarrhoea, vomiting or a skin infection handle food
cards.clinicalNote: A rapid onset of vomiting within a couple of hours of a meal points to a preformed toxin, usually staphylococcal, which means the food was contaminated by a handler and then left in the warm. Onset a day later is more likely the organism itself, such as salmonella.

cards.title: Self-check: fat has how many calories
cards.body:
Why does reducing fat lower energy intake so effectively, compared to reducing carbohydrate?

Answer: FAT PROVIDES 9 kcal PER GRAM, more than double the 4 kcal per gram of carbohydrate and protein. So gram for gram, fat is the most energy dense nutrient, and cutting it removes far more calories for the same reduction in food volume.

cards.title: Anaemia: the commonest nutritional disorder in India
cards.body:
IRON DEFICIENCY is the leading cause, and it is the commonest nutritional disorder in the world.

WHY IT IS SO COMMON HERE: low iron in a mostly cereal based diet, poor absorption of plant iron, blood loss from menstruation, repeated pregnancies, and HOOKWORM.

TWO KINDS OF DIETARY IRON:
• HAEM iron (meat, fish, liver, poultry): well absorbed, about 20 to 30%
• NON-HAEM iron (spinach, jaggery, pulses, cereals): poorly absorbed, only about 1 to 5%

So a vegetarian diet can look iron rich on paper and still leave a woman anaemic.

WHAT INCREASES ABSORPTION:
• VITAMIN C. Lemon, amla, guava, citrus, tomato, taken WITH the meal. It can multiply non-haem absorption several fold
• A small amount of meat or fish alongside plant iron
• Cooking in iron vessels

WHAT BLOCKS ABSORPTION:
• TEA and COFFEE (tannins). This is the big one in India, and it is completely avoidable
• Calcium and milk
• Phytates in whole grains, oxalates in spinach
• Antacids

SO: take iron with lemon water, and never with tea. Leave a gap of at least an hour either side of tea, coffee or milk.

IFA supplementation and deworming are the public health answer.
cards.clinicalNote: The single highest value piece of nutrition advice you can give an anaemic Indian woman costs nothing: do not drink tea with your meal or with your iron tablet. The tannins bind the iron and you excrete most of it. Move the tea an hour away and the same tablet starts working.

cards.title: Iron tablets: teaching that keeps people taking them
cards.body:
Most people stop iron because of the side effects, not because they do not care.

WHAT TO TELL THEM, before they stop:

1. Your STOOLS WILL TURN BLACK. This is normal and harmless. If nobody warns you, you will think you are bleeding and stop the tablet
2. Constipation, nausea and a metallic taste are common. Take it AFTER food if the nausea is bad, though absorption is better on an empty stomach
3. Take it with VITAMIN C (lemon water, orange, amla). It genuinely increases absorption
4. Do NOT take it with TEA, COFFEE, MILK, calcium or antacids
5. Keep taking it for at least 3 MONTHS AFTER the haemoglobin is normal, to refill the body's stores. Stopping when you feel better means the anaemia returns
6. KEEP IRON TABLETS AWAY FROM CHILDREN. Iron overdose is a leading cause of fatal childhood poisoning, and the tablets look like sweets

The antidote for iron poisoning is DESFERRIOXAMINE.

cards.title: Feeding the patient who cannot eat
body: IF THE GUT WORKS, USE IT. Enteral feeding is safer, cheaper and preserves the gut lining
ENTERAL: nasogastric for short term, gastrostomy (PEG) for long term
PARENTERAL (into a vein): only when the gut cannot be used. Bowel obstruction, severe pancreatitis, short bowel
TPN must go through a CENTRAL line. It is too concentrated for a peripheral vein
TPN risks: INFECTION (it is an ideal culture medium), hyperglycaemia, electrolyte shifts, liver dysfunction
Never stop TPN abruptly, it causes rebound HYPOGLYCAEMIA. Taper it
Never add drugs to a TPN bag, and never use the TPN line for anything else
Enteral feeding: head of bed 30–45 degrees, check placement before every feed, check residual volume, flush the tube
The gut atrophies when unused. Even small trophic feeds help preserve it
cards.clinicalNote: TPN is a warm bag of glucose, protein and fat, which is to say a perfect bacterial culture medium hanging on a line that goes directly into the heart. That is why the aseptic care of a TPN line is not a formality.

cards.title: Refeeding syndrome: when feeding is the danger
cards.body:
A starved body adapts. It switches to fat metabolism, and its intracellular stores of PHOSPHATE, POTASSIUM and MAGNESIUM are quietly depleted, even though the blood levels can look normal.

Then you feed them. Carbohydrate arrives, insulin surges, and glucose, phosphate, potassium and magnesium all rush INTO the cells at once.

THE RESULT: the blood levels crash.
• HYPOPHOSPHATAEMIA is the hallmark. It causes muscle weakness, respiratory failure and cardiac failure
• Hypokalaemia: arrhythmia
• Hypomagnesaemia: arrhythmia, seizure
• Fluid overload, cardiac failure, and death

WHO IS AT RISK: severe malnutrition, anorexia nervosa, chronic alcoholism, prolonged fasting or starvation, prolonged vomiting, a very low BMI, and anyone with little or no intake for 5 or more days.

PREVENTION:
• Start feeding SLOWLY and LOW. Begin at a fraction of the target and build up over days
• Give THIAMINE and other vitamins BEFORE and during refeeding
• Check phosphate, potassium and magnesium DAILY at first, and replace them aggressively
• Monitor fluid balance, weight and cardiac status closely

THE PARADOX TO REMEMBER: the treatment for starvation can kill the starving. Slow is safe.
cards.clinicalNote: Every instinct in a nurse who sees a starving patient says to feed them properly and quickly. That instinct has killed people. Feeding is deliberately cautious, and understanding why turns what looks like neglect into the correct treatment.

cards.title: Obesity and metabolic syndrome
body: Obesity: BMI 30 or above. In Asian populations, risk begins at a LOWER BMI, so 25 is often used
WAIST CIRCUMFERENCE predicts metabolic risk better than BMI. Central (abdominal) fat is the dangerous kind
Risk waist: over 90 cm in Asian men, over 80 cm in Asian women
METABOLIC SYNDROME: central obesity PLUS any two of raised triglycerides, low HDL, raised BP, and raised fasting glucose
It multiplies the risk of type 2 diabetes, heart disease and stroke
Consequences of obesity: diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidaemia, osteoarthritis, sleep apnoea, fatty liver, several cancers, infertility
MANAGEMENT: sustained lifestyle change. A deficit of about 500 kcal a day gives roughly 0.5 kg a week
Aim for 150 minutes of moderate activity a week. Crash diets fail, and the weight returns with interest
Behaviour change, realistic goals and long term support beat any specific diet

cards.title: Self-check: black stools on iron
cards.body:
A patient started on iron tablets last week returns alarmed because her stools have turned black. She has stopped the tablets. What do you tell her?

Answer: BLACK STOOLS ARE A NORMAL, HARMLESS EFFECT OF ORAL IRON. She should restart the tablets. This is exactly why the warning must be given BEFORE the first dose: an unwarned patient assumes she is bleeding internally, stops the treatment, and stays anaemic.

cards.title: Number Series: what this tests
cards.body: Number series questions test whether you can spot the rule linking a sequence of numbers and use it to predict the next term. It is pattern recognition under time pressure, not heavy calculation.

cards.title: Number Series: step by step method
body: Write the gaps between consecutive terms, the first differences.
If the gaps are equal, the rule is +constant (arithmetic). If they grow or shrink steadily, look at the second differences.
If differences don’t settle, test ratios (×constant), squares/cubes, or two interleaved series.
Apply the confirmed rule to the last term, then check it fits the whole series.

cards.title: Number Series: worked example
cards.body:
Series: 2, 6, 12, 20, 30, ?

Step 1: First differences: 4, 6, 8, 10.
Step 2: The gaps rise by 2 each time, so the next gap is 12.
Step 3: Confirmed rule: add an increasing even number (equivalently term = n(n+1)).
Step 4: 30 + 12 = 42.

Answer: 42.

cards.title: Number Series: common mistake
cards.body: Don’t grab the first pattern you see and stop. Always extend your rule back across the whole series to confirm it fits every term, not just the last two.

cards.title: Number Series: the six patterns to test
body: Constant difference: +7, +7, +7 (arithmetic)
Constant ratio: ×2, ×2 (geometric)
Difference of the differences is constant (second order)
Alternating: two series woven together, check every other term
Squares/cubes with an offset: 4, 9, 16 → n² ; 2, 9, 28 → n³+1
Prime numbers, or n² minus n, when nothing else fits

cards.title: Number Series: DR SAP
cards.body:
When a series will not crack, run DR SAP in order:

Difference (subtract neighbours)
Ratio (divide neighbours)
Second difference (differences of the differences)
Alternate (split into two series)
Powers (squares, cubes, plus or minus a constant)

Most NORCET series fall out at D or R. Only go deeper if both fail.

cards.title: Blood Relations: what this tests
cards.body: These test your ability to track family links from a worded statement and work out how two people are related, often through a chain of ‘the son of’, ‘the only daughter of’ phrases.

cards.title: Blood Relations: step by step method
body: Read the sentence from the innermost phrase outward, resolve the smallest relation first.
Translate each phrase into a quick family tree (+ for male, − for female, lines for parent/child).
Substitute each resolved person back into the outer phrase, step by step.
State the final relation from the asked person’s point of view, watching the gender.

cards.title: Blood Relations: worked example
cards.body:
‘His mother is the only daughter of my mother’. How is the speaker (a woman) related to him?

Step 1: Innermost: ‘the only daughter of my mother’.
Step 2: The only daughter of the speaker’s mother is the speaker herself.
Step 3: So ‘his mother’ = the speaker.
Step 4: She is his mother.

Answer: Mother.

cards.title: Blood Relations: common mistake
cards.body: The biggest trap is assuming the speaker’s gender or skipping ‘only’. ‘Only daughter’ forces that daughter to be the speaker herself, read those qualifiers literally.

cards.title: Blood Relations: the rules that decide the answer
body: Draw it. Never solve a relation puzzle in your head
Male on the left, female on the right, same generation on one line
"Only son of" and "only daughter of" are the strongest clues, use them first
Maternal = mother's side, paternal = father's side
A "brother in law" can be your sister's husband OR your spouse's brother, check both
The last relation named in the sentence is usually the one being asked about

cards.title: Blood Relations: draw, do not read
cards.body:
Symbols to use every time:

+ for male, − for female
= for a married couple (horizontal line)
| for parent to child (vertical line)
A line across for siblings

Read the sentence backwards, from the person being asked about to the speaker. The answer appears on the diagram, not in the words.

cards.title: Direction Sense: what this tests
cards.body: Direction questions test whether you can track turns and distances on a mental map, then find either the final direction or the straight-line distance from the start.

cards.title: Direction Sense: step by step method
body: Draw axes: North up, South down, East right, West left. Start at the origin.
Plot each leg. ‘Turn right’ rotates your facing 90° clockwise; ‘turn left’ 90° anticlockwise.
Total the movement along each axis separately, net North–South and net East–West.
For distance, use Pythagoras on the two net legs; opposite legs of equal length cancel.

cards.title: Direction Sense: worked example
cards.body:
Walk 5 km N, turn right 3 km, turn right 5 km. Distance from start?

Step 1: Origin, facing North.
Step 2: 5 km North; right → East 3 km; right → South 5 km.
Step 3: North 5 and South 5 cancel; net East = 3.
Step 4: One leg remains, so distance = 3 km.

Answer: 3 km.

cards.title: Direction Sense: common mistake
cards.body: Confusing ‘distance walked’ with ‘distance from start’. The question almost always wants displacement, cancel opposite legs before measuring.

cards.title: Direction Sense: what examiners hide
body: Always start facing NORTH unless told otherwise
A right turn is clockwise, a left turn is anticlockwise. Turning "to his right" depends on which way he faces
Shadow at sunrise falls WEST, at sunset it falls EAST
Final displacement is a straight line, use Pythagoras, not the total distance walked
Count the net movement on each axis, north minus south, east minus west

cards.title: Direction Sense: NEWS clockwise
cards.body:
North → East → South → West, clockwise. Say it out loud once and draw the cross on your paper before you read the question.

Right turn = next one clockwise.
Left turn = previous one anticlockwise.

Two left turns or two right turns always leave you facing the opposite way.

cards.title: Coding & Decoding: what this tests
cards.body: These test whether you can find the rule that turns a word into its code, usually a fixed letter shift, a reversal, or a position swap, and then apply the same rule to a new word.

cards.title: Coding & Decoding: step by step method
body: Line up the original word and its code, letter by letter.
Find the change per letter: count the alphabet gap (C→D is +1) and check it is constant.
Watch for variations: alternating shifts, reversed order, or position-based shifts.
Apply the confirmed rule to each letter of the target word independently.

cards.title: Coding & Decoding: worked example
cards.body:
If CAT = DBU, then DOG = ?

Step 1: Align: C→D, A→B, T→U.
Step 2: Each letter moves +1 in the alphabet.
Step 3: Rule confirmed: shift every letter forward by one.
Step 4: D→E, O→P, G→H.

Answer: EPH.

cards.title: Coding & Decoding: common mistake
cards.body: Forgetting to apply the rule to every letter, students shift the first letter then copy the rest. Process each letter on its own.

cards.title: Coding and Decoding: the four codes that appear
body: Letter shift: each letter moves forward or back a fixed number (+1, −2)
Reverse alphabet: A↔Z, B↔Y. Position of a letter plus its mirror always makes 27
Positional: A=1 to Z=26, the code is a number
Jumbled: the letters are the same, only the order changed
Always write the alphabet with its numbers on the margin before you start

cards.title: Coding and Decoding: EJOTY
cards.body:
Memorise five anchors and you can place any letter instantly:

E = 5, J = 10, O = 15, T = 20, Y = 25

Need R? It is just after O(15), so R = 18. Need W? Just after T(20), so W = 23.

For the reverse alphabet: letter position + mirror position = 27.

cards.title: Syllogisms: what this tests
cards.body: Syllogisms test whether a conclusion necessarily follows from the given statements, pure logic, not real-world knowledge. ‘Necessarily’ is the key word.

cards.title: Syllogisms: step by step method
body: Treat each statement as a set: ‘All A are B’ means A sits entirely inside B.
Sketch overlapping circles (a Venn diagram) for the statements.
A conclusion is valid only if it holds in EVERY possible diagram, not just one.
Reject any conclusion that could be false in some valid arrangement. ‘some’ and ‘all’ must be exact.

cards.title: Syllogisms: worked example
cards.body:
All cats are animals. All animals breathe. Conclusion?

Step 1: Cats sit inside Animals.
Step 2: Animals sit inside ‘things that breathe’.
Step 3: So Cats sit inside ‘things that breathe’ in every diagram.
Step 4: ‘All cats breathe’ always holds.

Answer: All cats breathe.

cards.title: Syllogisms: common mistake
cards.body: Bringing in outside knowledge, or accepting a conclusion that only ‘could’ be true. If you can draw one valid diagram where it fails, it does not follow.

cards.title: Syllogisms: rules that settle most questions
body: Draw Venn circles. Never reason in words
Two negative premises can never give a valid conclusion
Two particular premises ("some") can never give a valid conclusion
If either premise is negative, the conclusion must be negative
"Some A are B" always also means "Some B are A"
"All A are B" does NOT mean "All B are A". This is the classic trap

cards.title: Syllogisms: the possibility test
cards.body:
A conclusion is only true if it holds in EVERY possible diagram, not just the one you drew first.

So: draw the diagram that makes the conclusion FALSE. If you can draw even one, the conclusion "does not follow".

If you cannot draw one, it follows.

That single habit answers most "either or" options correctly.

cards.title: Time and Work: what this tests
cards.body: These test the idea of a work rate, how much of a job someone does per day, and how rates add when people work together.

cards.title: Time and Work: step by step method
body: Convert each worker’s time into a one-day rate: finish in n days → 1/n per day.
Add the rates of everyone working together.
Invert the combined rate to get the combined time.
For staggered work, multiply rate × days for each phase and sum the work done.

cards.title: Time and Work: worked example
cards.body:
A finishes in 10 days, B in 15 days. Together?

Step 1: Rates: A = 1/10, B = 1/15 per day.
Step 2: Combined = 1/10 + 1/15 = 3/30 + 2/30 = 5/30.
Step 3: 5/30 = 1/6 per day.
Step 4: Time = 1 ÷ (1/6) = 6 days.

Answer: 6 days.

cards.title: Time and Work: common mistake
cards.body: Averaging the two times, e.g. (10+15)/2. Work rates add, times do not. Always go via 1/n per day.

cards.title: Time and Work: the numbers to set up
body: Work done in one day = 1 ÷ (days taken). This is the rate
Rates ADD when people work together: 1/A + 1/B
Together they take AB ÷ (A + B) days. Learn this shortcut
Efficiency is inversely proportional to time taken
If a pipe empties the tank, its rate is NEGATIVE, subtract it
Take the LCM of the days as the total work, it kills all fractions

cards.title: Time and Work: use LCM as the tank
cards.body:
A does it in 12 days, B in 18. Do NOT add fractions.

Let the total work = LCM(12, 18) = 36 units.
A does 36÷12 = 3 units a day.
B does 36÷18 = 2 units a day.
Together 5 units a day → 36÷5 = 7.2 days.

Whole numbers all the way. This is faster and far less error prone than 1/12 + 1/18.

cards.title: Percentage: what this tests
cards.body: Percentage questions test conversion between percentages and fractions, and how successive percentage changes combine, a frequent NORCET trap.

cards.title: Percentage: step by step method
body: Turn a percentage into a multiplier: +20% means ×1.20, −20% means ×0.80.
For successive changes, multiply the multipliers in order. Never add the percentages.
Compare the final multiplier to 1: above 1 is a net gain, below 1 a net loss.
Convert the final multiplier back into a percentage change.

cards.title: Percentage: worked example
cards.body:
A value rises 20%, then falls 20%. Net change?

Step 1: +20% → ×1.20; −20% → ×0.80.
Step 2: Combined = 1.20 × 0.80 = 0.96.
Step 3: 0.96 is below 1, so it is a net loss.
Step 4: 0.96 = 96% of the original → 4% decrease.

Answer: 4% decrease.

cards.title: Percentage: common mistake
cards.body: Assuming equal up-and-down percentages cancel to zero. They act on different bases, so the result is always a small net decrease.

cards.title: Percentage: conversions worth memorising
body: A rise of x% then a fall of x% does NOT return you to the start, you end lower
Net change of two successive changes: a + b + (ab ÷ 100), keep the signs
Increase from A to B = (B − A) ÷ A × 100. Always divide by the ORIGINAL

cards.title: Percentage: divide by the original
cards.body:
The single most common mistake is dividing by the wrong number.

Percentage CHANGE always divides by the OLD value.
Percentage OF always multiplies the base.

"20 is what percent more than 16?" → (20 − 16) ÷ 16 = 25%, not ÷ 20.

Say "change over original" once before every percentage question.

cards.title: Ratio and Proportion: what this tests
cards.body: These test whether you can scale and combine ratios, especially chaining two ratios that share a common term.

cards.title: Ratio and Proportion: step by step method
body: Write each ratio clearly and identify the term they share.
Scale both ratios so the shared term has the same value (use the LCM).
Read the combined ratio across all terms.
Pick out the two terms the question asks for and simplify.

cards.title: Ratio and Proportion: worked example
cards.body:
A : B = 2 : 3 and B : C = 4 : 5. Find A : C.

Step 1: Shared term is B (3 in the first, 4 in the second).
Step 2: LCM of 3 and 4 is 12: scale to A : B = 8 : 12 and B : C = 12 : 15.
Step 3: Combined A : B : C = 8 : 12 : 15.
Step 4: A : C = 8 : 15.

Answer: 8 : 15.

cards.title: Ratio and Proportion: common mistake
cards.body: Equalise the shared term before reading across. Blindly multiplying across ratios is unreliable, make B common first.

cards.title: Ratio and Proportion: the setup that never fails
body: A ratio has no units, it is a comparison. Keep both quantities in the SAME unit first
a : b = c : d means a×d = b×c (cross multiply)
To divide 600 in 2 : 3, total parts = 5, one part = 120, so 240 and 360
Direct proportion: one goes up, the other goes up (multiply)
Inverse proportion: one goes up, the other goes down (more workers, fewer days)
A ratio can be scaled: 2 : 3 is the same as 20 : 30

cards.title: Ratio and Proportion: count the parts
cards.body:
Almost every ratio question is solved the same way:

1. Add the ratio numbers to get TOTAL PARTS.
2. Divide the total quantity by total parts to get ONE PART.
3. Multiply one part by each share.

Ratio 3 : 4 : 5 of 1200 → 12 parts → one part = 100 → 300, 400, 500.

If you can find "one part", the question is already answered.

cards.title: Important health days
body: 7 APRIL: World Health Day (the day WHO was founded, in 1948)
12 MAY: International Nurses Day (Florence Nightingale's birthday)
5 MAY: International Day of the Midwife
31 MAY: World No Tobacco Day
1 DECEMBER: World AIDS Day
24 MARCH: World TB Day
25 APRIL: World Malaria Day
14 JUNE: World Blood Donor Day
10 OCTOBER: World Mental Health Day. 1 OCTOBER: International Day of Older Persons
1 JULY: National Doctors' Day in India. 8 MARCH: International Women's Day
cards.clinicalNote: International Nurses Day falls on Florence Nightingale's birthday, and that is not a coincidence worth forgetting. It is the single most commonly asked date in nursing general knowledge papers.

cards.title: The health organisations and what they do
cards.body:
WHO (World Health Organization):
• Founded 7 APRIL 1948. Headquarters GENEVA, Switzerland
• The UN's specialised agency for health
• India belongs to the SOUTH-EAST ASIA region, with its regional office in NEW DELHI

UNICEF: children. Headquarters NEW YORK. Immunisation, nutrition, child protection
RED CROSS: founded by HENRI DUNANT. Headquarters GENEVA. Its emblem is a red cross on a white ground, the reverse of the Swiss flag
INC (Indian Nursing Council): the statutory body regulating nursing education and practice in India. Established 1947
TNAI (Trained Nurses Association of India): the professional association of nurses, founded 1908
ICN (International Council of Nurses): the global federation of national nurses associations, founded 1899. The FIRST international organisation of health professionals
ICMR (Indian Council of Medical Research): the apex body for biomedical research in India
NITI Aayog: the policy think tank that replaced the Planning Commission in 2015

cards.title: Florence Nightingale and the birth of modern nursing
cards.body:
Born 12 MAY 1820 in Florence, Italy. Died 1910.

Known as THE LADY WITH THE LAMP, from her night rounds during the CRIMEAN WAR (1854), where she nursed at the Scutari barrack hospital.

WHAT SHE ACTUALLY DID, which matters more than the lamp:
• She found a mortality rate of over 40% at Scutari, and cut it dramatically, mainly by improving SANITATION, ventilation, hygiene, nutrition and clean water
• She proved that most deaths were from preventable INFECTION, not from battle wounds
• She was a pioneer of STATISTICS in healthcare, and invented the polar area diagram (the coxcomb chart) to make the data undeniable to politicians
• She was elected the first female member of the Royal Statistical Society
• In 1860 she founded the NIGHTINGALE SCHOOL OF NURSING at St Thomas' Hospital, London. This is the beginning of modern professional nursing education
• She wrote 'Notes on Nursing' (1859)
• Her ENVIRONMENTAL THEORY held that the nurse's role is to put the patient in the best condition for NATURE to act upon them: clean air, clean water, light, warmth, quiet and diet

THE NIGHTINGALE PLEDGE is the nursing equivalent of the Hippocratic oath, though it was written after her death, in 1893.
cards.clinicalNote: Nightingale is remembered for the lamp and revered for her compassion, but what actually saved those soldiers was drains, ventilation and data. She is the founder of modern nursing precisely because she treated hygiene as a matter of evidence, not sentiment.

cards.title: Nursing bodies and legal framework in India
body: INDIAN NURSING COUNCIL (INC): established by the Indian Nursing Council Act, 1947. It sets uniform standards of nursing education
STATE NURSING COUNCILS: register nurses and issue the licence to practise in each state
TNAI (Trained Nurses Association of India), 1908: the professional body, affiliated to the ICN
The Consumer Protection Act allows patients to seek redress for deficient service, and nurses are covered by it
The Mental Healthcare Act 2017 decriminalised attempted suicide and set out patients' rights
The PC-PNDT Act prohibits prenatal sex determination, to prevent female foeticide
The MTP Act governs the lawful termination of pregnancy
The Transplantation of Human Organs Act governs organ donation and defines brain death
POCSO Act protects children from sexual offences, and reporting is MANDATORY

cards.title: Health in the Indian Constitution and the five year plans
cards.body:
HEALTH IS A STATE SUBJECT in India, which is why services differ so much between states. The centre funds and guides, and the states deliver.

CONSTITUTIONAL PROVISIONS:
• ARTICLE 21: the right to LIFE, which the Supreme Court has interpreted to include the right to HEALTH and to live with dignity
• ARTICLE 47: the state's duty to raise the level of NUTRITION and the standard of living, and to improve PUBLIC HEALTH. It also directs the prohibition of intoxicating drinks and drugs
• ARTICLE 42: just and humane conditions of work, and MATERNITY RELIEF
• The 73rd and 74th amendments devolved health to PANCHAYATI RAJ institutions and urban local bodies

KEY COMMITTEES, worth knowing by name:
• BHORE COMMITTEE (1946): the blueprint of India's health system. It recommended primary health centres, and the integration of curative and preventive services
• MUDALIAR COMMITTEE (1962): reviewed the Bhore recommendations, suggested strengthening district hospitals
• KARTAR SINGH COMMITTEE (1973): recommended the MULTIPURPOSE health worker
• SRIVASTAVA COMMITTEE (1975): recommended community health workers, and led to the concept behind today's ASHA

ALMA-ATA DECLARATION 1978: 'HEALTH FOR ALL by the year 2000', through PRIMARY HEALTH CARE. India is a signatory.
cards.clinicalNote: The Bhore Committee is the one to remember above the others. Almost the entire architecture of Indian public health, the primary health centre, the sub centre, and the idea that prevention and cure belong together, traces back to it.

cards.title: Self-check: WHO headquarters and founding
cards.body:
When was the World Health Organization founded, where is it headquartered, and which WHO region does India belong to?

Answer: FOUNDED 7 APRIL 1948, headquartered in GENEVA, Switzerland. India is in the SOUTH-EAST ASIA REGION (SEARO), whose regional office is in NEW DELHI. World Health Day is celebrated on 7 April to mark the founding.

cards.title: The major national health missions
body: NATIONAL HEALTH MISSION (NHM): the umbrella. It covers the National Rural Health Mission (NRHM, launched 2005) and the National Urban Health Mission (NUHM, 2013)
AYUSHMAN BHARAT (2018): two components, Health and Wellness Centres, and PM-JAY health insurance
PM-JAY (Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana): health cover of up to 5 lakh rupees per family per year for secondary and tertiary care. The world's largest health assurance scheme
JANANI SURAKSHA YOJANA (JSY): a cash incentive to promote INSTITUTIONAL delivery, reducing maternal and neonatal death
JANANI SHISHU SURAKSHA KARYAKRAM (JSSK): FREE delivery, free caesarean, free drugs, diagnostics, diet and transport for pregnant women and sick newborns
MISSION INDRADHANUSH: intensified drive to fully immunise children and pregnant women who were missed
POSHAN ABHIYAAN: the national nutrition mission, targeting stunting, undernutrition and anaemia
RASHTRIYA BAL SWASTHYA KARYAKRAM (RBSK): child health screening for the 4 Ds, defects at birth, deficiencies, diseases and developmental delay
cards.clinicalNote: Janani Suraksha Yojana works on a simple insight: the biggest barrier to a woman delivering in a facility was often the cost of getting there and being there. Removing the cash barrier moved millions of births out of the home, and maternal deaths fell.

cards.title: The disease control programmes and what each targets
cards.body:
NTEP (National Tuberculosis Elimination Programme), formerly RNTCP: TUBERCULOSIS. Free diagnosis and treatment, directly observed therapy, and the Nikshay Poshan Yojana nutrition support

NVBDCP: VECTOR BORNE disease. Malaria, dengue, chikungunya, filariasis, kala-azar, Japanese encephalitis

NACP (National AIDS Control Programme): HIV and AIDS. Free antiretroviral therapy, testing, and prevention

NLEP: LEPROSY, using multidrug therapy

NPCB&VI: BLINDNESS and visual impairment. Cataract surgery is the largest component

NPCDCS: non communicable disease. CANCER, DIABETES, CARDIOVASCULAR disease and STROKE

NMHP: the National MENTAL HEALTH Programme, with its district component

NPHCE: health care of the ELDERLY

RMNCH+A: reproductive, maternal, newborn, child and adolescent health, the continuum of care from adolescence through pregnancy to childhood

cards.title: The ASHA, and the health worker structure
cards.body:
ASHA (Accredited Social Health Activist), introduced under the NRHM in 2005:
• A woman from the village itself, usually aged 25 to 45, preferably a married, widowed or divorced woman, with about 8 years of schooling
• ONE ASHA per roughly 1000 population
• She is a VOLUNTEER, paid by performance based incentives, not a salaried government employee
• She is the vital link between the community and the health system

HER ROLE:
• Mobilise the community, especially pregnant women, to use health services
• Escort women for institutional delivery, and for antenatal care
• Promote immunisation, family planning, sanitation and nutrition
• Provide basic first contact care: ORS, iron tablets, paracetamol, contraceptives
• Maintain records, and act as a depot for essential supplies

THE WIDER TEAM:
• ANM (Auxiliary Nurse Midwife): at the SUB CENTRE, one per 5000 population (3000 in hilly and tribal areas)
• ANGANWADI WORKER: under the ICDS scheme. Supplementary nutrition, preschool education, growth monitoring, immunisation support
• The ASHA, ANM and Anganwadi worker together are the front line of Indian public health

ICDS (Integrated Child Development Services), launched 1975: supplementary nutrition, immunisation, health check up, referral, preschool education, and nutrition and health education. It targets children under 6, pregnant women and nursing mothers.
cards.clinicalNote: The ASHA is the reason so many public health programmes reach a village at all. She is not a junior nurse, she is a community mobiliser who happens to carry ORS, and treating her as an equal partner is what makes a nurse effective in the field.

cards.title: Health indicators and targets to know
body: The SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS (SDGs) run 2015 to 2030. GOAL 3 is Good Health and Wellbeing
SDG target: reduce MATERNAL MORTALITY to below 70 per 1,00,000 live births
SDG target: reduce NEONATAL mortality to 12 or fewer per 1000 live births, and UNDER-5 mortality to 25 or fewer
The Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) preceded them, running 2000 to 2015
India's National Health Policy 2017 aims to raise public health spending to 2.5% of GDP
INFANT MORTALITY RATE is the most sensitive single index of a nation's health and development
The MATERNAL MORTALITY RATIO is expressed per 1,00,000 live births, unlike the other mortality rates which use 1000
TOTAL FERTILITY RATE at replacement level is 2.1
The NFHS (National Family Health Survey) is the main source of these figures in India

cards.title: Primary health care and Health for All
cards.body:
THE ALMA-ATA DECLARATION, 1978: 'HEALTH FOR ALL by the year 2000', to be achieved through PRIMARY HEALTH CARE. India is a signatory.

PRIMARY HEALTH CARE is not simply 'the first level of care'. It is a philosophy, and it rests on FOUR PRINCIPLES:

1. EQUITABLE DISTRIBUTION: services reach everyone, especially the poor and the remote, not just those who can travel and pay
2. COMMUNITY PARTICIPATION: people are partners, not passive recipients. This is the idea behind the ASHA
3. INTERSECTORAL COORDINATION: health is not made in hospitals. It is made by water, sanitation, food, housing, education, agriculture and roads working together
4. APPROPRIATE TECHNOLOGY: affordable, acceptable, sustainable, and usable by ordinary people. ORS is the perfect example. A sachet costing a few rupees saves more children than any intensive care unit

THE 8 ESSENTIAL COMPONENTS (remember ELEMENTS):
• Education about health problems and their prevention
• Locally endemic disease control
• Expanded programme of immunisation
• Maternal and child health, including family planning
• Essential drugs provision
• Nutrition and food supply
• Treatment of common disease and injury
• Safe water and basic sanitation
cards.clinicalNote: Appropriate technology is the principle most often forgotten and most worth remembering. ORS has saved more children's lives than almost any hospital intervention in history, and it works because it is cheap, simple and can be made by a mother in her own kitchen.

cards.title: Self-check: who is the ASHA
cards.body:
How many people does one ASHA serve, and is she a salaried government employee?

Answer: ONE ASHA PER ROUGHLY 1000 POPULATION, and NO, she is NOT salaried. She is a community VOLUNTEER, selected from the village itself, and paid through performance based incentives. She is the link between the community and the health system, not a junior member of the clinical staff.

cards.title: Vitamins, discoveries and the names behind them
cards.body:
General knowledge papers love the names attached to discoveries. These are the ones that recur.

• LOUIS PASTEUR: germ theory, pasteurisation, the rabies vaccine
• ROBERT KOCH: identified the tubercle bacillus (1882) and the cholera vibrio. Koch's postulates
• EDWARD JENNER: the smallpox vaccine, using cowpox. The FATHER OF IMMUNOLOGY. Vaccine comes from 'vacca', the Latin for cow
• ALEXANDER FLEMING: discovered PENICILLIN in 1928, from the mould Penicillium
• JOSEPH LISTER: antiseptic surgery, using carbolic acid. The father of antisepsis
• IGNAZ SEMMELWEIS: proved HANDWASHING prevented puerperal fever. He was ridiculed in his lifetime
• WILLIAM HARVEY: described the circulation of the blood
• RONALD ROSS: proved the mosquito transmits MALARIA, working in India. Nobel Prize 1902
• KARL LANDSTEINER: discovered the ABO blood groups
• FREDERICK BANTING and CHARLES BEST: discovered INSULIN
• JONAS SALK: the injectable polio vaccine. ALBERT SABIN: the ORAL polio vaccine

SMALLPOX was declared ERADICATED in 1980. It remains the only human disease ever eradicated.

INDIA was declared POLIO FREE in 2014.
cards.clinicalNote: Semmelweis is the one worth pausing on. He proved that doctors washing their hands stopped mothers dying, was ridiculed by his profession, and died in an asylum. The lesson, that evidence is resisted when it implies blame, is not a historical curiosity.

cards.title: Awards, honours and firsts in nursing
body: The FLORENCE NIGHTINGALE AWARD: India's highest national award for nurses, instituted in 1973, presented by the PRESIDENT of India
It is awarded on or around INTERNATIONAL NURSES DAY, 12 May
The INTERNATIONAL COUNCIL OF NURSES (ICN) was founded in 1899, the first international organisation of health professionals
The TNAI (Trained Nurses Association of India) was founded in 1908
The INDIAN NURSING COUNCIL was constituted under the Indian Nursing Council Act, 1947
The Nightingale School of Nursing opened at St Thomas' Hospital, London, in 1860
The INTERNATIONAL RED CROSS was founded by HENRI DUNANT, who won the first Nobel Peace Prize in 1901
Mother Teresa received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1979 for her work with the destitute and dying in Kolkata
The WHO's 2020 designation of the Year of the Nurse and the Midwife marked Nightingale's 200th birth anniversary

cards.title: The blood groups and transfusion rules
cards.body:
Discovered by KARL LANDSTEINER.

GROUP O NEGATIVE is the UNIVERSAL DONOR. It carries no A, B or Rh antigen, so nobody's antibodies attack it.
GROUP AB POSITIVE is the UNIVERSAL RECIPIENT. It has no anti-A or anti-B antibodies, so it can accept anything.

Memory hook: O gives to all, AB takes from all.

Rh: about 95% of Indians are Rh POSITIVE.
An Rh NEGATIVE mother carrying an Rh POSITIVE baby is the classic risk, and it is why ANTI-D is given.

TRANSFUSION SAFETY, the rules that prevent a fatal reaction:
• Check the unit against the patient with a SECOND registered nurse, at the BEDSIDE
• Check: patient identity, blood group, unit number, expiry, and inspect the bag for clots or discolouration
• Baseline observations, then stay with the patient for the FIRST 15 MINUTES. Most fatal reactions declare themselves early
• A unit must be completed within 4 HOURS of leaving the blood bank
• Use only NORMAL SALINE with blood. NEVER dextrose (it haemolyses the cells) and never Ringer's lactate (its calcium clots the blood)

ACUTE HAEMOLYTIC REACTION: fever, chills, loin or back pain, dark urine, hypotension, a sense of impending doom.
STOP THE TRANSFUSION IMMEDIATELY. Keep the line open with NORMAL SALINE using NEW tubing. Do not flush the blood in the line into the patient. Notify the doctor and the blood bank, and return the unit.

cards.title: Recent developments in public health
cards.body:
These themes recur in current affairs sections, and they matter clinically too.

ANTIMICROBIAL RESISTANCE (AMR) is one of the greatest global health threats. Driven by overuse of antibiotics in humans and in agriculture. India carries a heavy burden, and antibiotic stewardship is now a core nursing responsibility.

NON COMMUNICABLE DISEASES have overtaken communicable disease as India's leading cause of death. Diabetes, hypertension, heart disease, stroke, cancer and chronic lung disease. India is often called the diabetes capital of the world.

DOUBLE BURDEN OF MALNUTRITION: undernutrition and obesity now coexist, sometimes in the same household and even in the same family. Stunted children and overweight adults, driven by cheap, energy dense, nutrient poor food.

UNIVERSAL HEALTH COVERAGE: everyone receives the health services they need without financial hardship. OUT OF POCKET expenditure is the central problem in India, and catastrophic health spending pushes millions into poverty every year. This is what PM-JAY was designed to address.

DIGITAL HEALTH: telemedicine, the Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission, electronic health records, and eSanjeevani teleconsultation.

ONE HEALTH: the recognition that human, animal and environmental health are inseparable. Most emerging infections are ZOONOTIC, crossing from animals to humans, which is what makes pandemics.

CLIMATE AND HEALTH: heat stress, changing vector ranges bringing dengue and malaria to new areas, air pollution, and food insecurity.
cards.clinicalNote: Out of pocket expenditure is the quiet catastrophe of Indian healthcare. A family can survive the illness and be destroyed by the bill, and that is why financial protection is treated as a health intervention in its own right, not as an economic footnote.

cards.title: Miscellaneous facts that come up
body: The human body has 206 BONES in an adult, and about 270 at birth (many fuse with growth)
The FEMUR is the longest and strongest bone. The STAPES in the ear is the smallest
The LIVER is the largest internal organ and the largest gland. The SKIN is the largest organ overall
Normal adult blood volume is about 5 litres, roughly 7 to 8% of body weight
Red blood cells live about 120 DAYS. Platelets live about 7 to 10 days
The normal adult heart beats about 60–100 times a minute, and roughly 100,000 times a day
There are 12 pairs of cranial nerves and 31 pairs of spinal nerves
The universal blood donor is O NEGATIVE. The universal recipient is AB POSITIVE
Smallpox is the only human disease ever ERADICATED, declared in 1980
The first successful human heart transplant was performed by CHRISTIAAN BARNARD in 1967

cards.title: Self-check: which fluid with blood
cards.body:
You are setting up a blood transfusion. Which IV fluid may be used in the same line, and why are the others forbidden?

Answer: NORMAL SALINE ONLY. DEXTROSE causes the red cells to swell and HAEMOLYSE. RINGER'S LACTATE contains CALCIUM, which can overcome the anticoagulant in the bag and CLOT the blood. Normal saline is isotonic and calcium free, and it is the only compatible fluid.
